These are closely tied to agricultural ceremonies and based on a natural calendar and worldview, considering the changes in seasons and flora and fauna. === Basic Information Table === {| class="wikitable" !Item!!Description!!Remarks |- !rowspan="3"|Deities |colspan="2"|Yachimatahime no kami・Yachimatahime no kami ({{ill|Yachimatahime no kami|ja|やちまたひめのかみ}}) |- |colspan="2"|Yachimatahiko no kami・Yachimatahiko no kami ({{ill|Yachimatahiko no kami|ja|やちまたひこのかみ}}) |- |colspan="2"|Kunado no kami ({{ill|Kunado no kami|ja|くなどのかみ}}) |- !rowspan="3"|Imperial Codes |rowspan="3"|Jingi-ryō・Minor Ritual||{{ill|Ōmi Code|ja|近江令}} (established in 668) |- |{{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}} (compiled in 681) |- |{{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}} (Chapter 6, Article 20, from 718) |- !Event Date |Lunar June & December||Eve, or an auspicious day |- !rowspan="2"|Faith |rowspan="2"|Divine faith ([[ancient Shinto]])||Natural faith |- |Primitive ancestral spirit faith |- |} == Historical references == === Koji Kon'gen and Nishinomiya Ki === Within the festivals that expel the calamity of {{ill|epidemic gods|ja|疫病神}}, there is the Four Corners and Four Boundaries Festival of the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}Tsuda 2008, p380. Established during the mid-Muromachi period, the "Koji Kon'gen" (authored by {{ill|Ichijō Kanera|ja|一条兼良}}) describes the annual events and rites of the court. It mistakenly refers to the Michiae Festival as "Shinka Michiae Festival" and "Four Corners and Four Boundaries Festival"Kōda 1976, p229. According to the "Nishinomiya Ki" (authored by {{ill|Minamoto no Takamasa|ja|源高明}}), established in the mid-Heian period, which discusses the etiquette and systems of the court ceremonies, the festivals held at the four corners of the capital in years with signs of epidemics were called "Four Corners Festival", and those held at the boundaries of the provinces were called "Four Boundaries Festival (Four Realms Festival)". These were not originally designated as regular festivals venerating deities, but later misinterpretations combined and confused the Michiae Festival and the Extinguishing Festival. Particularly when epidemics spread, regional temporary Michiae Festivals were held, leading to misunderstandings and confusion in venerating epidemic gods. {{Cquote|Four Realms Festival - Directed by the Onmyōryō, a festival of the four realms, the storerooms' people being the emissaries.
Four Corners Festival - Directed by the Onmyōryō, a festival of the four corners of the palace, the emissaries' people being present. Whenever there is an epidemic under the heavens, the Onmyōryō advances the decree. The officials proclaim it.|||"Nishinomiya Ki"|Tsuda 2008, p380}} In addition to public affairs, the types of festivals for epidemic gods in folk beliefs were extremely diverse and held over a wide range. == Norito (Shinto prayers) == {{Quotation|'''Original Text''' - In the high plains of Heaven where matters begin, proclaimed by the decree of the Imperial Grandchild, before the deities at the hot spring rock village of Ōyachimata, speak the names of Yachimatahiko, Yachimatahime, and Kunado, complete the praises, to the root and base countries, the rough and estranged calamities arrive, holding the night and day guards, offering sacrifices and rituals, Advance the silks of Aka-myo, Tera-myo, Nigi-myo, Ara-myo, offer the imperial sake, known high at the jar edges, full bellies, juice in the seeds, the residents of the mountains and fields, the wild and tame furs, the residents of the blue ocean fields, the wide and narrow fins, the deep and coastal sea vegetables, at the foot of the mountains across, advancing the Uzumaki silks, hearing and eating plainly At the hot spring rock village of Ōyachimata, steadfastly perform rituals, blessing the prosperous world, also princes, kings, ministers, all officials, to all the public under the heavens, evenly conduct the rituals, the Shinto priests of Heaven offer the grand norito, completing the praises|Engishiki|Michiae Festival Norito}} === Meaning & Remarks === *'''Takamanohara''' (High Plain of Heaven) - Where the divine ancestors began matters. The Michiae Festival originates from Takamanohara and is performed by the decree of the divine ancestors who began matters there. This description is also found in "Koshi Den" (authored by Hirata Atsutane), following the interpretation. *'''Imperial Grandchild's command''' - Refers to the current Emperor's decree. Originally, it should say "Imperial Grandchild's command," but to avoid redundancy, one character is omittedTsuda 2008, p383. The Imperial Grandchild refers to the reigning Emperor of each era, and the command refers to the decree or proclamation. *'''Tataego''' (Praising Words) - To perform and complete the ritual. Frequently used in prayers, "tataego" means praising words, where "tatae" signifies fulfillment like filling with water, and "oe" represents completing thoroughlyTsuda 2008, p69. Thus, the original meaning is "to complete praising (meaning: to exhaust good and beautiful words and praise the divine virtue)," but as it was used frequently, it also came to mean simply "to perform a ritual" or "to offer silks and other offerings" or "to recite the divine names". *'''Ōyachimata''' - Refers to a grand crossroads outside the palace. The "eight" is not the number but signifies a multitude[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/222073/m0u/ Digital Daijisen "Yachimata (Eight Crossroads)"] Shogakukan, Accessed July 18, 2015. "Yachimata" means crossroads, places where roads branch off in four or more directions like a junction. *'''Yutsui Hamura''' (Like the Sacred Rock Village) - Like the sacred clusters of rocks. "Yu" is the same as in "yun == Referennces == {{draft categories|[[Category:Shinto festivals]]}}

{{User:Immanuelle/Japanese Imperial Rituals updated}} The {{Nihongo|{{Interlanguage link|Road Festival|ja|道饗祭|simple|User:Immanuelle/Road Festival}}|道饗祭[[ja:道饗祭]]|Michiae no Matsuri[[fr:User:Immanuelle/Road Festival]]}} is a Shinto ritual. From ancient times, twice a year in June and December, on the streets at the four corners of the capital, the three pillars: {{ill|Yachimatahiko no kami|ja|やちまたひこのかみ}}, {{ill|Yachimatahime no kami|ja|やちまたひめのかみ}}, and {{ill|Kunado no kami|ja|くなどのかみ}} are enshrined to prevent {{ill|demons|ja|鬼魅}} and {{ill|specters|ja|妖怪}} from entering the palace and the capital, praying for protection in a ritual and festival at the shrine[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/212207/m0u/ Digital Daijisen 'Michiae Festival'] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015. As an imperial ritual, Kyoto was the center, but regional ceremonies were also held during epidemics[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 Encyclopedia of the World, 2nd Edition 'Michiae Festival'] Heibonsha, accessed July 12, 2015. For example, in Musashi Province, there are many place names and family names such as Aeiba, and {{ill|Kunio Yanagita|ja|柳田國男}} in his "Study of Place Names" (Kadokawa Bunko, Ninth Edition 1974, p.216) suggests that Michiae Festival = Jashin Festival, with references to places like "Aino Field" and "Aida Field" in northern Musashi, speculated to be derived from "Aeiba Field" (same book, p.216). == Overview == In the first category of the {{ill|Ritsuryō|lt=Ritsuryō system|ja|令}} as defined in the Jingi-ryō, an imperial decree collection "Engishiki Volume One" (edited by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|ja|藤原時平}}/{{ill|Fujiwara no Tadahira|ja|藤原忠平}}), it is described under the June Festival section, and classified as a minor ritualUnder the Ritsuryo system, rituals are conducted after a day of purification.[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/108492/m0u/%E5%B0%8F%E7%A5%80/ Digital Daijisen 'Shoshi [Minor Ritual]'] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015. The {{ill|Kyōshoku|ja|京職}} (left and right capitals officials) managed the festival, and the {{ill|Urabe clan|ja|卜部氏|simple|User:Immanuelle/Urabe clan|fr|Clan Urabe}} performed the purification rituals[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 Britannica International Encyclopedia 'Michiae Festival'] accessed July 12, 2015. In the Ritsuryo era in Kyoto, it was often combined with the Hoshizume FestivalNishimuta 2003, p119. The festival was held after the {{ill|Ōharae|ja|大祓}} at the end of the months of June and December according to the lunar calendar, but since "Engishiki" and "Taihō Code" do not specify the festival days, it is said that auspicious days were chosen according to the theory in "Shūgai-shō"Tsuda 2008, p378. In Shinto, rituals are performed semi-annually, marking the spring equinox, autumn equinox, summer solstice, winter solstice, and the transition between the upper and lower halves of the year. These are closely tied to agricultural ceremonies and based on a natural calendar and worldview, considering the changes in seasons and flora and fauna. === Basic Information Table === {| class="wikitable" !Item!!Description!!Remarks |- !rowspan="3"|Deities |colspan="2"|Yachimatahime no kami・Yachimatahime no kami ({{ill|Yachimatahime no kami|ja|やちまたひめのかみ}}) |- |colspan="2"|Yachimatahiko no kami・Yachimatahiko no kami ({{ill|Yachimatahiko no kami|ja|やちまたひこのかみ}}) |- |colspan="2"|Kunado no kami ({{ill|Kunado no kami|ja|くなどのかみ}}) |- !rowspan="3"|Imperial Codes |rowspan="3"|Jingi-ryō・Minor Ritual||{{ill|Ōmi Code|ja|近江令}} (established in 668) |- |{{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}} (compiled in 681) |- |{{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}} (Chapter 6, Article 20, from 718) |- !Event Date |Lunar June & December||Eve, or an auspicious day |- !rowspan="2"|Faith |rowspan="2"|Divine faith ([[ancient Shinto]])||Natural faith |- |Primitive ancestral spirit faith |- |} == Historical references == === Koji Kon'gen and Nishinomiya Ki === Within the festivals that expel the calamity of {{ill|epidemic gods|ja|疫病神}}, there is the Four Corners and Four Boundaries Festival of the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}Tsuda 2008, p380. Established during the mid-Muromachi period, the "Koji Kon'gen" (authored by {{ill|Ichijō Kanera|ja|一条兼良}}) describes the annual events and rites of the court. It mistakenly refers to the Michiae Festival as "Shinka Michiae Festival" and "Four Corners and Four Boundaries Festival"Kōda 1976, p229. According to the "Nishinomiya Ki" (authored by {{ill|Minamoto no Takamasa|ja|源高明}}), established in the mid-Heian period, which discusses the etiquette and systems of the court ceremonies, the festivals held at the four corners of the capital in years with signs of epidemics were called "Four Corners Festival", and those held at the boundaries of the provinces were called "Four Boundaries Festival (Four Realms Festival)". These were not originally designated as regular festivals venerating deities, but later misinterpretations combined and confused the Michiae Festival and the Extinguishing Festival. Particularly when epidemics spread, regional temporary Michiae Festivals were held, leading to misunderstandings and confusion in venerating epidemic gods. {{Cquote|Four Realms Festival - Directed by the Onmyōryō, a festival of the four realms, the storerooms' people being the emissaries.
Four Corners Festival - Directed by the Onmyōryō, a festival of the four corners of the palace, the emissaries' people being present. Whenever there is an epidemic under the heavens, the Onmyōryō advances the decree. The officials proclaim it.|||"Nishinomiya Ki"|Tsuda 2008, p380}} In addition to public affairs, the types of festivals for epidemic gods in folk beliefs were extremely diverse and held over a wide range. == Norito (Shinto prayers) == {{Quotation|'''Original Text''' - In the high plains of Heaven where matters begin, proclaimed by the decree of the Imperial Grandchild, before the deities at the hot spring rock village of Ōyachimata, speak the names of Yachimatahiko, Yachimatahime, and Kunado, complete the praises, to the root and base countries, the rough and estranged calamities arrive, holding the night and day guards, offering sacrifices and rituals, Advance the silks of Aka-myo, Tera-myo, Nigi-myo, Ara-myo, offer the imperial sake, known high at the jar edges, full bellies, juice in the seeds, the residents of the mountains and fields, the wild and tame furs, the residents of the blue ocean fields, the wide and narrow fins, the deep and coastal sea vegetables, at the foot of the mountains across, advancing the Uzumaki silks, hearing and eating plainly At the hot spring rock village of Ōyachimata, steadfastly perform rituals, blessing the prosperous world, also princes, kings, ministers, all officials, to all the public under the heavens, evenly conduct the rituals, the Shinto priests of Heaven offer the grand norito, completing the praises|Engishiki|Michiae Festival Norito}} === Meaning & Remarks === *'''Takamanohara''' (High Plain of Heaven) - Where the divine ancestors began matters. The Michiae Festival originates from Takamanohara and is performed by the decree of the divine ancestors who began matters there. This description is also found in "Koshi Den" (authored by Hirata Atsutane), following the interpretation. *'''Imperial Grandchild's command''' - Refers to the current Emperor's decree. The Imperial Grandchild refers to the reigning Emperor of each era, and the command refers to the decree or proclamation. *'''Tataego''' (Praising Words) - To perform and complete the ritual. Frequently used in prayers, "tataego" means praising words, where "tatae" signifies fulfillment like filling with water, and "oe" represents completing thoroughlyTsuda 2008, p69. Thus, the original meaning is "to complete praising (meaning: to exhaust good and beautiful words and praise the divine virtue)," but as it was used frequently, it also came to mean simply "to perform a ritual" or "to offer silks and other offerings" or "to recite the divine names". *'''Ōyachimata''' - Refers to a grand crossroads outside the palace. The "eight" is not the number but signifies a multitude[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/222073/m0u/ Digital Daijisen "Yachimata (Eight Crossroads)"] Shogakukan, Accessed July 18, 2015. "Yachimata" means crossroads, places where roads branch off in four or more directions like a junction. *'''Yutsui Hamura''' (Like the Sacred Rock Village) - Like the sacred clusters of rocks. "Yu" is the same as in "yun == Referennces == {{draft categories|[[Category:Shinto festivals]] [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] }}

The festival was held after the {{ill|Ōharae|ja|大祓}} at the end of the months of June and December according to the lunar calendar, but since "Engishiki" and "Taihō Code" do not specify the festival days, it is said that auspicious days were chosen according to the theory in "Shūgai-shō"Tsuda 2008, p378. In Shinto, rituals are performed semi-annually, marking the spring equinox, autumn equinox, summer solstice, winter solstice, and the transition between the upper and lower halves of the year. These are closely tied to agricultural ceremonies and based on a natural calendar and worldview, considering the changes in seasons and flora and fauna. === Basic Information Table === {| class="wikitable" !Item!!Description!!Remarks |- !rowspan="3"|Deities |colspan="2"|Yachimatahime no kami・Yachimatahime no kami ({{ill|Yachimatahime no kami|ja|やちまたひめのかみ}}) |- |colspan="2"|Yachimatahiko no kami・Yachimatahiko no kami ({{ill|Yachimatahiko no kami|ja|やちまたひこのかみ}}) |- |colspan="2"|Kunado no kami ({{ill|Kunado no kami|ja|くなどのかみ}}) |- !rowspan="3"|Imperial Codes |rowspan="3"|Jingi-ryō・Minor Ritual||{{ill|Ōmi Code|ja|近江令}} (established in 668) |- |{{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}} (compiled in 681) |- |{{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}} (Chapter 6, Article 20, from 718) |- !Event Date |Lunar June & December||Eve, or an auspicious day |- !rowspan="2"|Faith |rowspan="2"|Divine faith ([[ancient Shinto]])||Natural faith |- |Primitive ancestral spirit faith |- |} == Historical references == === Koji Kon'gen and Nishinomiya Ki === Within the festivals that expel the calamity of {{ill|epidemic gods|ja|疫病神}}, there is the Four Corners and Four Boundaries Festival of the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}Tsuda 2008, p380. Established during the mid-Muromachi period, the "Koji Kon'gen" (authored by {{ill|Ichijō Kanera|ja|一条兼良}}) describes the annual events and rites of the court. It mistakenly refers to the Michiae Festival as "Shinka Michiae Festival" and "Four Corners and Four Boundaries Festival"Kōda 1976, p229. According to the "Nishinomiya Ki" (authored by {{ill|Minamoto no Takamasa|ja|源高明}}), established in the mid-Heian period, which discusses the etiquette and systems of the court ceremonies, the festivals held at the four corners of the capital in years with signs of epidemics were called "Four Corners Festival", and those held at the boundaries of the provinces were called "Four Boundaries Festival (Four Realms Festival)". These were not originally designated as regular festivals venerating deities, but later misinterpretations combined and confused the Michiae Festival and the Extinguishing Festival. Particularly when epidemics spread, regional temporary Michiae Festivals were held, leading to misunderstandings and confusion in venerating epidemic gods. {{Cquote|Four Realms Festival - Directed by the Onmyōryō, a festival of the four realms, the storerooms' people being the emissaries.
Four Corners Festival - Directed by the Onmyōryō, a festival of the four corners of the palace, the emissaries' people being present. Whenever there is an epidemic under the heavens, the Onmyōryō advances the decree. The officials proclaim it.|||"Nishinomiya Ki"|Tsuda 2008, p380}} In addition to public affairs, the types of festivals for epidemic gods in folk beliefs were extremely diverse and held over a wide range. == Norito (Shinto prayers) == {{Quotation|'''Original Text''' - In the high plains of Heaven where matters begin, proclaimed by the decree of the Imperial Grandchild, before the deities at the hot spring rock village of Ōyachimata, speak the names of Yachimatahiko, Yachimatahime, and Kunado, complete the praises, to the root and base countries, the rough and estranged calamities arrive, holding the night and day guards, offering sacrifices and rituals, Advance the silks of Aka-myo, Tera-myo, Nigi-myo, Ara-myo, offer the imperial sake, known high at the jar edges, full bellies, juice in the seeds, the residents of the mountains and fields, the wild and tame furs, the residents of the blue ocean fields, the wide and narrow fins, the deep and coastal sea vegetables, at the foot of the mountains across, advancing the Uzumaki silks, hearing and eating plainly At the hot spring rock village of Ōyachimata, steadfastly perform rituals, blessing the prosperous world, also princes, kings, ministers, all officials, to all the public under the heavens, evenly conduct the rituals, the Shinto priests of Heaven offer the grand norito, completing the praises|Engishiki|Michiae Festival Norito}} === Meaning & Remarks === *'''Takamanohara''' (High Plain of Heaven) - Where the divine ancestors began matters. The Michiae Festival originates from Takamanohara and is performed by the decree of the divine ancestors who began matters there. This description is also found in "Koshi Den" (authored by Hirata Atsutane), following the interpretation. *'''Imperial Grandchild's command''' - Refers to the current Emperor's decree. Originally, it should say "Imperial Grandchild's command," but to avoid redundancy, one character is omittedTsuda 2008, p383. The Imperial Grandchild refers to the reigning Emperor of each era, and the command refers to the decree or proclamation. *'''Tataego''' (Praising Words) - To perform and complete the ritual. Frequently used in prayers, "tataego" means praising words, where "tatae" signifies fulfillment like filling with water, and "oe" represents completing thoroughlyTsuda 2008, p69. Thus, the original meaning is "to complete praising (meaning: to exhaust good and beautiful words and praise the divine virtue)," but as it was used frequently, it also came to mean simply "to perform a ritual" or "to offer silks and other offerings" or "to recite the divine names". *'''Ōyachimata''' - Refers to a grand crossroads outside the palace. The "eight" is not the number but signifies a multitude[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/222073/m0u/ Digital Daijisen "Yachimata (Eight Crossroads)"] Shogakukan, Accessed July 18, 2015. "Yachimata" means crossroads, places where roads branch off in four or more directions like a junction. *'''Yutsui Hamura''' (Like the Sacred Rock Village) - Like the sacred clusters of rocks. "Yu" is the same as in "yun == Referennces == {{draft categories|[[Category:Shinto festivals]] [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:June observances]] [[Category:December observances]] }}

{{User:Immanuelle/Japanese Imperial Rituals updated}} The {{Nihongo|{{Interlanguage link|Road Festival|ja|道饗祭|simple|User:Immanuelle/Road Festival}}|道饗祭[[ja:道饗祭]]|Michiae no Matsuri[[fr:User:Immanuelle/Road Festival]]}} is a Shinto ritual. From ancient times, twice a year in June and December, on the streets at the four corners of the capital, the three pillars: {{ill|Yachimatahiko no kami|ja|やちまたひこのかみ}}, {{ill|Yachimatahime no kami|ja|やちまたひめのかみ}}, and {{ill|Kunado no kami|ja|くなどのかみ}} are enshrined to prevent {{ill|demons|ja|鬼魅}} and {{ill|specters|ja|妖怪}} from entering the palace and the capital, praying for protection in a ritual and festival at the shrine[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/212207/m0u/ Digital Daijisen 'Michiae Festival'] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015. As an imperial ritual, Kyoto was the center, but regional ceremonies were also held during epidemics[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 Encyclopedia of the World, 2nd Edition 'Michiae Festival'] Heibonsha, accessed July 12, 2015. For example, in Musashi Province, there are many place names and family names such as Aeiba, and {{ill|Kunio Yanagita|ja|柳田國男}} in his "Study of Place Names" (Kadokawa Bunko, Ninth Edition 1974, p.216) suggests that Michiae Festival = Jashin Festival, with references to places like "Aino Field" and "Aida Field" in northern Musashi, speculated to be derived from "Aeiba Field" (same book, p.216). == Overview == In the first category of the {{ill|Ritsuryō|lt=Ritsuryō system|ja|令}} as defined in the Jingi-ryō, an imperial decree collection "Engishiki Volume One" (edited by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|ja|藤原時平|en}}/{{ill|Fujiwara no Tadahira|ja|藤原忠平|en}}), it is described under the June Festival section, and classified as a minor ritualUnder the Ritsuryo system, rituals are conducted after a day of purification.[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/108492/m0u/%E5%B0%8F%E7%A5%80/ Digital Daijisen 'Shoshi [Minor Ritual]'] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015. The {{ill|Kyōshoku|ja|京職}} (left and right capitals officials) managed the festival, and the {{ill|Urabe clan|ja|卜部氏|simple|User:Immanuelle/Urabe clan|fr|Clan Urabe}} performed the purification rituals[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 Britannica International Encyclopedia 'Michiae Festival'] accessed July 12, 2015. In the Ritsuryo era in Kyoto, it was often combined with the Hoshizume FestivalNishimuta 2003, p119. The festival was held after the {{ill|Ōharae|ja|大祓}} at the end of the months of June and December according to the lunar calendar, but since "Engishiki" and "Taihō Code" do not specify the festival days, it is said that auspicious days were chosen according to the theory in "Shūgai-shō"Tsuda 2008, p378. In Shinto, rituals are performed semi-annually, marking the spring equinox, autumn equinox, summer solstice, winter solstice, and the transition between the upper and lower halves of the year. These are closely tied to agricultural ceremonies and based on a natural calendar and worldview, considering the changes in seasons and flora and fauna. === Basic Information Table === {| class="wikitable" !Item!!Description!!Remarks |- !rowspan="3"|Deities |colspan="2"|Yachimatahime no kami・Yachimatahime no kami ({{ill|Yachimatahime no kami|ja|やちまたひめのかみ}}) |- |colspan="2"|Yachimatahiko no kami・Yachimatahiko no kami ({{ill|Yachimatahiko no kami|ja|やちまたひこのかみ}}) |- |colspan="2"|Kunado no kami ({{ill|Kunado no kami|ja|くなどのかみ}}) |- !rowspan="3"|Imperial Codes |rowspan="3"|Jingi-ryō・Minor Ritual||{{ill|Ōmi Code|ja|近江令}} (established in 668) |- |{{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}} (compiled in 681) |- |{{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}} (Chapter 6, Article 20, from 718) |- !Event Date |Lunar June & December||Eve, or an auspicious day |- !rowspan="2"|Faith |rowspan="2"|Divine faith ([[ancient Shinto]])||Natural faith |- |Primitive ancestral spirit faith |- |} == Historical references == === Koji Kon'gen and Nishinomiya Ki === Within the festivals that expel the calamity of {{ill|epidemic gods|ja|疫病神}}, there is the Four Corners and Four Boundaries Festival of the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}Tsuda 2008, p380. It mistakenly refers to the Michiae Festival as "Shinka Michiae Festival" and "Four Corners and Four Boundaries Festival"Kōda 1976, p229. According to the "Nishinomiya Ki" (authored by {{ill|Minamoto no Takamasa|ja|源高明}}), established in the mid-Heian period, which discusses the etiquette and systems of the court ceremonies, the festivals held at the four corners of the capital in years with signs of epidemics were called "Four Corners Festival", and those held at the boundaries of the provinces were called "Four Boundaries Festival (Four Realms Festival)". These were not originally designated as regular festivals venerating deities, but later misinterpretations combined and confused the Michiae Festival and the Extinguishing Festival. Particularly when epidemics spread, regional temporary Michiae Festivals were held, leading to misunderstandings and confusion in venerating epidemic gods. {{Cquote|Four Realms Festival - Directed by the Onmyōryō, a festival of the four realms, the storerooms' people being the emissaries.
Four Corners Festival - Directed by the Onmyōryō, a festival of the four corners of the palace, the emissaries' people being present. Whenever there is an epidemic under the heavens, the Onmyōryō advances the decree. The officials proclaim it.|||"Nishinomiya Ki"|Tsuda 2008, p380}} In addition to public affairs, the types of festivals for epidemic gods in folk beliefs were extremely diverse and held over a wide range. == Norito (Shinto prayers) == {{Quotation|'''Original Text''' - In the high plains of Heaven where matters begin, proclaimed by the decree of the Imperial Grandchild, before the deities at the hot spring rock village of Ōyachimata, speak the names of Yachimatahiko, Yachimatahime, and Kunado, complete the praises, to the root and base countries, the rough and estranged calamities arrive, holding the night and day guards, offering sacrifices and rituals, Advance the silks of Aka-myo, Tera-myo, Nigi-myo, Ara-myo, offer the imperial sake, known high at the jar edges, full bellies, juice in the seeds, the residents of the mountains and fields, the wild and tame furs, the residents of the blue ocean fields, the wide and narrow fins, the deep and coastal sea vegetables, at the foot of the mountains across, advancing the Uzumaki silks, hearing and eating plainly At the hot spring rock village of Ōyachimata, steadfastly perform rituals, blessing the prosperous world, also princes, kings, ministers, all officials, to all the public under the heavens, evenly conduct the rituals, the Shinto priests of Heaven offer the grand norito, completing the praises|Engishiki|Michiae Festival Norito}} === Meaning & Remarks === *'''Takamanohara''' (High Plain of Heaven) - Where the divine ancestors began matters. Thus, the original meaning is "to complete praising (meaning: to exhaust good and beautiful words and praise the divine virtue)," but as it was used frequently, it also came to mean simply "to perform a ritual" or "to offer silks and other offerings" or "to recite the divine names". *'''Ōyachimata''' - Refers to a grand crossroads outside the palace. The "eight" is not the number but signifies a multitude[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/222073/m0u/ Digital Daijisen "Yachimata (Eight Crossroads)"] Shogakukan, Accessed July 18, 2015. The festival was held after the {{ill|Ōharae-shiki|ja|大祓|en}} at the end of the months of June and December according to the lunar calendar, but since "[[Engishiki]]" and "[[Taihō Code]]" do not specify the festival days, it is said that auspicious days were chosen according to the theory in "Shūgai-shō"Tsuda 2008, p378. In Shinto, rituals are performed semi-annually, marking the spring equinox, autumn equinox, summer solstice, winter solstice, and the transition between the upper and lower halves of the year. These are closely tied to agricultural ceremonies and based on a natural calendar and worldview, considering the changes in seasons and flora and fauna. === Basic Information Table === {| class="wikitable" !Item!!Description!!Remarks |- !rowspan="3"|Deities |colspan="2"|Yachimatahime no kami・Yachimatahime no kami ({{ill|Yachimatahime no kami|ja|やちまたひめのかみ}}) |- |colspan="2"|Yachimatahiko no kami・Yachimatahiko no kami ({{ill|Yachimatahiko no kami|ja|やちまたひこのかみ}}) |- |colspan="2"|Kunado no kami ({{ill|Kunado no kami|ja|くなどのかみ}}) |- !rowspan="3"|Imperial Codes |rowspan="3"|Jingi-ryō・Minor Ritual||{{ill|Ōmi Code|ja|近江令}} (established in 668) |- |{{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}} (compiled in 681) |- |{{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}} (Chapter 6, Article 20, from 718) |- !Event Date |Lunar June & December||Eve, or an auspicious day |- !rowspan="2"|Faith |rowspan="2"|Divine faith ({{Ill|Koshinto|en|}})||Natural faith |- |Primitive ancestral spirit faith |- |} == Historical references == === Koji Kon'gen and Nishinomiya Ki === Within the festivals that expel the calamity of {{ill|epidemic gods|ja|疫病神}}, there is the Four Corners and Four Boundaries Festival of the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}Tsuda 2008, p380. Established during the mid-Muromachi period, the "Koji Kon'gen" (authored by {{ill|Ichijō Kanera|ja|一条兼良}}) describes the annual events and rites of the court. It mistakenly refers to the Michiae Festival as "Shinka Michiae Festival" and "Four Corners and Four Boundaries Festival"Kōda 1976, p229. According to the "Nishinomiya Ki" (authored by {{ill|Minamoto no Takamasa|ja|源高明}}), established in the mid-Heian period, which discusses the etiquette and systems of the court ceremonies, the festivals held at the four corners of the capital in years with signs of epidemics were called "Four Corners Festival", and those held at the boundaries of the provinces were called "Four Boundaries Festival (Four Realms Festival)". These were not originally designated as regular festivals venerating deities, but later misinterpretations combined and confused the Michiae Festival and the Extinguishing Festival. Particularly when epidemics spread, regional temporary Michiae Festivals were held, leading to misunderstandings and confusion in venerating epidemic gods. {{Cquote|Four Realms Festival - Directed by the Onmyōryō, a festival of the four realms, the storerooms' people being the emissaries.
Four Corners Festival - Directed by the Onmyōryō, a festival of the four corners of the palace, the emissaries' people being present. Whenever there is an epidemic under the heavens, the Onmyōryō advances the decree. The officials proclaim it.|||"Nishinomiya Ki"|Tsuda 2008, p380}} In addition to public affairs, the types of festivals for epidemic gods in folk beliefs were extremely diverse and held over a wide range. == Norito (Shinto prayers) == {{Quotation|'''Original Text''' - In the high plains of Heaven where matters begin, proclaimed by the decree of the Imperial Grandchild, before the deities at the hot spring rock village of Ōyachimata, speak the names of Yachimatahiko, Yachimatahime, and Kunado, complete the praises, to the root and base countries, the rough and estranged calamities arrive, holding the night and day guards, offering sacrifices and rituals, Advance the silks of Aka-myo, Tera-myo, Nigi-myo, Ara-myo, offer the imperial sake, known high at the jar edges, full bellies, juice in the seeds, the residents of the mountains and fields, the wild and tame furs, the residents of the blue ocean fields, the wide and narrow fins, the deep and coastal sea vegetables, at the foot of the mountains across, advancing the Uzumaki silks, hearing and eating plainly At the hot spring rock village of Ōyachimata, steadfastly perform rituals, blessing the prosperous world, also princes, kings, ministers, all officials, to all the public under the heavens, evenly conduct the rituals, the Shinto priests of Heaven offer the grand norito, completing the praises|Engishiki|Michiae Festival Norito}} === Meaning & Remarks === *'''Takamanohara''' (High Plain of Heaven) - Where the divine ancestors began matters. The Michiae Festival originates from Takamanohara and is performed by the decree of the divine ancestors who began matters there. This description is also found in "Koshi Den" (authored by Hirata Atsutane), following the interpretation. *'''Imperial Grandchild's command''' - Refers to the current Emperor's decree. Originally, it should say "Imperial Grandchild's command," but to avoid redundancy, one character is omittedTsuda 2008, p383. The Imperial Grandchild refers to the reigning Emperor of each era, and the command refers to the decree or proclamation. *'''Tataego''' (Praising Words) - To perform and complete the ritual. Frequently used in prayers, "tataego" means praising words, where "tatae" signifies fulfillment like filling with water, and "oe" represents completing thoroughlyTsuda 2008, p69. Thus, the original meaning is "to complete praising (meaning: to exhaust good and beautiful words and praise the divine virtue)," but as it was used frequently, it also came to mean simply "to perform a ritual" or "to offer silks and other offerings" or "to recite the divine names". *'''Ōyachimata''' - Refers to a grand crossroads outside the palace. The "eight" is not the number but signifies a multitude[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/222073/m0u/ Digital Daijisen "Yachimata (Eight Crossroads)"] Shogakukan, Accessed July 18, 2015. "Yachimata" means crossroads, places where roads branch off in four or more directions like a junction. *'''Yutsui Hamura''' (Like the Sacred Rock Village) - Like the sacred clusters of rocks. "Yu" is the same as in "yun == Referennces == {{draft categories|[[Category:Shinto festivals]] [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:June observances]] [[Category:December observances]] }}

{{User:Immanuelle/Japanese Imperial Rituals updated}} The {{Nihongo|{{Interlanguage link|Road Festival|ja|道饗祭|simple|User:Immanuelle/Road Festival}}|道饗祭[[ja:道饗祭]]|Michiae no Matsuri[[fr:User:Immanuelle/Road Festival]]}} is a Shinto ritual. From ancient times, twice a year in June and December, on the streets at the four corners of the capital, the three pillars: {{ill|Yachimatahiko no kami|ja|やちまたひこのかみ}}, {{ill|Yachimatahime no kami|ja|やちまたひめのかみ}}, and {{ill|Kunado no kami|ja|くなどのかみ}} are enshrined to prevent {{ill|Demon|lt=demons|ja|鬼魅}} and {{ill|Yōkai|ja|妖怪|en}} from entering the palace and the capital, praying for protection in a ritual and festival at the shrine[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/212207/m0u/ Digital Daijisen 'Michiae Festival'] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015. As an imperial ritual, Kyoto was the center, but regional ceremonies were also held during epidemics[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 Encyclopedia of the World, 2nd Edition 'Michiae Festival'] Heibonsha, accessed July 12, 2015. For example, in Musashi Province, there are many place names and family names such as Aeiba, and {{ill|Kunio Yanagita|ja|柳田國男}} in his "Study of Place Names" (Kadokawa Bunko, Ninth Edition 1974, p.216) suggests that Michiae Festival = Jashin Festival, with references to places like "Aino Field" and "Aida Field" in northern Musashi, speculated to be derived from "Aeiba Field" (same book, p.216). == Overview == In the first category of the {{ill|Ritsuryō|lt=Ritsuryō system|ja|令|en}} as defined in the Jingi-ryō, an imperial decree collection "Engishiki Volume One" (edited by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|ja|藤原時平|en}}/{{ill|Fujiwara no Tadahira|ja|藤原忠平|en}}), it is described under the June Festival section, and classified as a minor ritualUnder the Ritsuryo system, rituals are conducted after a day of purification.[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/108492/m0u/%E5%B0%8F%E7%A5%80/ Digital Daijisen 'Shoshi [Minor Ritual]'] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015. The {{ill|Kyōshoku|ja|京職}} (left and right capitals officials) managed the festival, and the {{ill|Urabe clan|ja|卜部氏|simple|User:Immanuelle/Urabe clan|fr|Clan Urabe}} performed the purification rituals[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 Britannica International Encyclopedia 'Michiae Festival'] accessed July 12, 2015. In the Ritsuryo era in Kyoto, it was often combined with the Hoshizume FestivalNishimuta 2003, p119. The festival was held after the {{ill|Ōharae-shiki|ja|大祓|en}} at the end of the months of June and December according to the lunar calendar, but since "[[Engishiki]]" and "[[Taihō Code]]" do not specify the festival days, it is said that auspicious days were chosen according to the theory in "Shūgai-shō"Tsuda 2008, p378. In Shinto, rituals are performed semi-annually, marking the spring equinox, autumn equinox, summer solstice, winter solstice, and the transition between the upper and lower halves of the year. These are closely tied to agricultural ceremonies and based on a natural calendar and worldview, considering the changes in seasons and flora and fauna. === Basic Information Table === {| class="wikitable" !Item!!Description!!Remarks |- !rowspan="3"|Deities |colspan="2"|Yachimatahime no kami・Yachimatahime no kami ({{ill|Yachimatahime no kami|ja|やちまたひめのかみ}}) |- |colspan="2"|Yachimatahiko no kami・Yachimatahiko no kami ({{ill|Yachimatahiko no kami|ja|やちまたひこのかみ}}) |- |colspan="2"|Kunado no kami ({{ill|Kunado no kami|ja|くなどのかみ}}) |- !rowspan="3"|Imperial Codes |rowspan="3"|Jingi-ryō・Minor Ritual||{{ill|Ōmi Code|ja|近江令}} (established in 668) |- |{{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}} (compiled in 681) |- |{{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|en}} (Chapter 6, Article 20, from 718) |- !Event Date |Lunar June & December||Eve, or an auspicious day |- !rowspan="2"|Faith |rowspan="2"|Divine faith ({{Ill|Koshinto|en|}})||Natural faith |- |Primitive ancestral spirit faith |- |} == Historical references == === Koji Kon'gen and Nishinomiya Ki === Within the festivals that expel the calamity of {{ill|epidemic gods|ja|疫病神}}, there is the Four Corners and Four Boundaries Festival of the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}Tsuda 2008, p380. These were not originally designated as regular festivals venerating deities, but later misinterpretations combined and confused the Michiae Festival and the Extinguishing Festival. Particularly when epidemics spread, regional temporary Michiae Festivals were held, leading to misunderstandings and confusion in venerating epidemic gods. {{Cquote|Four Realms Festival - Directed by the Onmyōryō, a festival of the four realms, the storerooms' people being the emissaries.

Whenever there is an epidemic under the heavens, the Onmyōryō advances the decree. The officials proclaim it.|||"Nishinomiya Ki"|Tsuda 2008, p380}} In addition to public affairs, the types of festivals for epidemic gods in folk beliefs were extremely diverse and held over a wide range. == Norito (Shinto prayers) == {{Quotation|'''Original Text''' - In the high plains of Heaven where matters begin, proclaimed by the decree of the Imperial Grandchild, before the deities at the hot spring rock village of Ōyachimata, speak the names of Yachimatahiko, Yachimatahime, and Kunado, complete the praises, to the root and base countries, the rough and estranged calamities arrive, holding the night and day guards, offering sacrifices and rituals, Advance the silks of Aka-myo, Tera-myo, Nigi-myo, Ara-myo, offer the imperial sake, known high at the jar edges, full bellies, juice in the seeds, the residents of the mountains and fields, the wild and tame furs, the residents of the blue ocean fields, the wide and narrow fins, the deep and coastal sea vegetables, at the foot of the mountains across, advancing the Uzumaki silks, hearing and eating plainly At the hot spring rock village of Ōyachimata, steadfastly perform rituals, blessing the prosperous world, also princes, kings, ministers, all officials, to all the public under the heavens, evenly conduct the rituals, the Shinto priests of Heaven offer the grand norito, completing the praises|Engishiki|Michiae Festival Norito}} === Meaning & Remarks === *'''Takamanohara''' (High Plain of Heaven) - Where the divine ancestors began matters. {{User:Immanuelle/Japanese Imperial Rituals updated}} The {{Nihongo|{{Interlanguage link|Road Festival|ja|道饗祭|simple|User:Immanuelle/Road Festival}}|道饗祭[[ja:道饗祭]]|Michiae no Matsuri[[fr:User:Immanuelle/Road Festival]]}} is a Shinto ritual. From ancient times, twice a year in June and December, on the streets at the four corners of the capital, the three pillars: {{ill|Yachimatahiko no kami|ja|やちまたひこのかみ}}, {{ill|Yachimatahime no kami|ja|やちまたひめのかみ}}, and {{ill|Kunado no kami|ja|くなどのかみ}} are enshrined to prevent {{ill|Demon|lt=demons|ja|鬼魅}} and {{ill|Yōkai|ja|妖怪|en}} from entering the palace and the capital, praying for protection in a ritual and festival at the shrine[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/212207/m0u/ Digital Daijisen 'Michiae Festival'] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015. As an imperial ritual, Kyoto was the center, but regional ceremonies were also held during epidemics[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 Encyclopedia of the World, 2nd Edition 'Michiae Festival'] Heibonsha, accessed July 12, 2015. For example, in Musashi Province, there are many place names and family names such as Aeiba, and {{ill|Kunio Yanagita|ja|柳田國男}} in his "Study of Place Names" (Kadokawa Bunko, Ninth Edition 1974, p.216) suggests that Michiae Festival = Jashin Festival, with references to places like "Aino Field" and "Aida Field" in northern Musashi, speculated to be derived from "Aeiba Field" (same book, p.216). == Overview == The Festival of the Road Gods (道祖神祭り, Michiae no matsuri) was an ancient ceremony prescribed by the Divinities Prescriptions (Jingiryō) intended to prevent evil spirits, such as demons and epidemic gods (ekishin), from entering the capital. This festival was conducted on the main roads (ōji) surrounding Kyoto in each of the four cardinal directions.{{Cite web|date=2023-09-29|title=Michiae no matsuri {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230929230332/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9286|access-date=2024-05-18|website=web.archive.org}} === Historical Context === The precise origins of the Festival of the Road Gods are unclear, but its formal characteristics suggest it is closely linked to the establishment of capital cities in Japan. It likely dates back to the founding of the Fujiwara Palace in Nara at the end of the seventh century. The festival's association with the introduction of capital cities suggests its purpose was to protect these new urban centers from malevolent supernatural influences. === Ritual Elements === The festival was performed by diviners (urabe) from the Department of Divinities (Jingikan), who utilized ritual implements made of fur. This indicates a strong connection to yin-yang divination practices (onmyōdō). The use of these implements and the involvement of the Department of Divinities highlight the festival's spiritual and protective purposes. === Decline and Disappearance === The Festival of the Road Gods gradually disappeared during the mid-Heian period, influenced by the rise of the Festival of the Four Directions (Shikaku sai) and other similar observances. As these other festivals gained prominence, references to the Michiae no matsuri became scarce in historical records. === Interpretations and Records === The Ryō no gige (Explanations of the Prescriptions) notes that the Michiae no matsuri aimed to prevent calamity-causing demons from entering the capital by intercepting and entertaining them in the streets. Conversely, the norito prayer recorded in the Engi Shiki (Engi Regulations) for this festival indicates that it celebrated three deities: Yachimata-hiko and other road deities. This suggests a dual interpretation of the festival's purpose: both as a means of protection from malevolent spirits and as a celebration of specific deities associated with the roads. == Basic information == In the first category of the {{ill|Ritsuryō|lt=Ritsuryō system|ja|令|en}} as defined in the Jingi-ryō, an imperial decree collection "Engishiki Volume One" (edited by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|ja|藤原時平|en}}/{{ill|Fujiwara no Tadahira|ja|藤原忠平|en}}), it is described under the June Festival section, and classified as a minor ritualUnder the Ritsuryo system, rituals are conducted after a day of purification.[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/108492/m0u/%E5%B0%8F%E7%A5%80/ Digital Daijisen 'Shoshi [Minor Ritual]'] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015. The {{ill|Kyōshoku|ja|京職}} (left and right capitals officials) managed the festival, and the {{ill|Urabe clan|ja|卜部氏|simple|User:Immanuelle/Urabe clan|fr|Clan Urabe}} performed the purification rituals[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 Britannica International Encyclopedia 'Michiae Festival'] accessed July 12, 2015. In the Ritsuryo era in Kyoto, it was often combined with the Hoshizume FestivalNishimuta 2003, p119. The festival was held after the {{ill|Ōharae-shiki|ja|大祓|en}} at the end of the months of June and December according to the lunar calendar, but since "[[Engishiki]]" and "[[Taihō Code]]" do not specify the festival days, it is said that auspicious days were chosen according to the theory in "Shūgai-shō"Tsuda 2008, p378. In Shinto, rituals are performed semi-annually, marking the spring equinox, autumn equinox, summer solstice, winter solstice, and the transition between the upper and lower halves of the year. These are closely tied to agricultural ceremonies and based on a natural calendar and worldview, considering the changes in seasons and flora and fauna. === Basic Information Table === {| class="wikitable" !Item!!Description!!Remarks |- !rowspan="3"|Deities |colspan="2"|Yachimatahime no kami・Yachimatahime no kami ({{ill|Yachimatahime no kami|ja|やちまたひめのかみ}}) |- |colspan="2"|Yachimatahiko no kami・Yachimatahiko no kami ({{ill|Yachimatahiko no kami|ja|やちまたひこのかみ}}) |- |colspan="2"|Kunado no kami ({{ill|Kunado no kami|ja|くなどのかみ}}) |- !rowspan="3"|Imperial Codes |rowspan="3"|Jingi-ryō・Minor Ritual||{{ill|Ōmi Code|ja|近江令}} (established in 668) |- |{{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}} (compiled in 681) |- |{{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|en}} (Chapter 6, Article 20, from 718) |- !Event Date |Lunar June & December||Eve, or an auspicious day |- !rowspan="2"|Faith |rowspan="2"|Divine faith ({{Ill|Koshinto|en|}})||Natural faith |- |Primitive ancestral spirit faith |- |} == Historical references == === Koji Kon'gen and Nishinomiya Ki === Within the festivals that expel the calamity of {{ill|epidemic gods|ja|疫病神}}, there is the Four Corners and Four Boundaries Festival of the {{ill|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮|fr|Bureau d'Onmyō|en:draft}}Tsuda 2008, p380. Established during the mid-Muromachi period, the "Koji Kon'gen" (authored by {{ill|Ichijō Kaneyoshi|ja|一条兼良}}) describes the annual events and rites of the court. It mistakenly refers to the Michiae Festival as "Shinka Michiae Festival" and "Four Corners and Four Boundaries Festival"Kōda 1976, p229. According to the "Nishinomiya Ki" (authored by {{ill|Minamoto no Takaakira|ja|源高明|pt}}), established in the mid-Heian period, which discusses the etiquette and systems of the court ceremonies, the festivals held at the four corners of the capital in years with signs of epidemics were called "Four Corners Festival", and those held at the boundaries of the provinces were called "Four Boundaries Festival (Four Realms Festival)". These were not originally designated as regular festivals venerating deities, but later misinterpretations combined and confused the Michiae Festival and the Extinguishing Festival. Particularly when epidemics spread, regional temporary Michiae Festivals were held, leading to misunderstandings and confusion in venerating epidemic gods. {{Cquote|Four Realms Festival - Directed by the Onmyōryō, a festival of the four realms, the storerooms' people being the emissaries.
Four Corners Festival - Directed by the Onmyōryō, a festival of the four corners of the palace, the emissaries' people being present. Whenever there is an epidemic under the heavens, the Onmyōryō advances the decree. The officials proclaim it.|||"Nishinomiya Ki"|Tsuda 2008, p380}} In addition to public affairs, the types of festivals for epidemic gods in folk beliefs were extremely diverse and held over a wide range. == Norito (Shinto prayers) == {{Quotation|'''Original Text''' - In the high plains of Heaven where matters begin, proclaimed by the decree of the Imperial Grandchild, before the deities at the hot spring rock village of Ōyachimata, speak the names of Yachimatahiko, Yachimatahime, and Kunado, complete the praises, to the root and base countries, the rough and estranged calamities arrive, holding the night and day guards, offering sacrifices and rituals, Advance the silks of Aka-myo, Tera-myo, Nigi-myo, Ara-myo, offer the imperial sake, known high at the jar edges, full bellies, juice in the seeds, the residents of the mountains and fields, the wild and tame furs, the residents of the blue ocean fields, the wide and narrow fins, the deep and coastal sea vegetables, at the foot of the mountains across, advancing the Uzumaki silks, hearing and eating plainly At the hot spring rock village of Ōyachimata, steadfastly perform rituals, blessing the prosperous world, also princes, kings, ministers, all officials, to all the public under the heavens, evenly conduct the rituals, the Shinto priests of Heaven offer the grand norito, completing the praises|Engishiki|Michiae Festival Norito}} === Meaning & Remarks === *'''Takamanohara''' (High Plain of Heaven) - Where the divine ancestors began matters. The Michiae Festival originates from Takamanohara and is performed by the decree of the divine ancestors who began matters there. This description is also found in "Koshi Den" (authored by Hirata Atsutane), following the interpretation. *'''Imperial Grandchild's command''' - Refers to the current Emperor's decree. Originally, it should say "Imperial Grandchild's command," but to avoid redundancy, one character is omittedTsuda 2008, p383. The Imperial Grandchild refers to the reigning Emperor of each era, and the command refers to the decree or proclamation. *'''Tataego''' (Praising Words) - To perform and complete the ritual. Frequently used in prayers, "tataego" means praising words, where "tatae" signifies fulfillment like filling with water, and "oe" represents completing thoroughlyTsuda 2008, p69. Thus, the original meaning is "to complete praising (meaning: to exhaust good and beautiful words and praise the divine virtue)," but as it was used frequently, it also came to mean simply "to perform a ritual" or "to offer silks and other offerings" or "to recite the divine names". *'''Ōyachimata''' - Refers to a grand crossroads outside the palace. The "eight" is not the number but signifies a multitude[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/222073/m0u/ Digital Daijisen "Yachimata (Eight Crossroads)"] Shogakukan, Accessed July 18, 2015. "Yachimata" means crossroads, places where roads branch off in four or more directions like a junction. *'''Yutsui Hamura''' (Like the Sacred Rock Village) - Like the sacred clusters of rocks. "Yu" is the same as in "yun == Footnotes == == Referennces == {{draft categories|[[Category:Shinto festivals]] [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:June observances]] [[Category:December observances]] }}

{{User:Immanuelle/Japanese Imperial Rituals updated}}
The Festival of the Road Gods (道祖神祭り, Michiae no matsuri) was an ancient ceremony prescribed by the Divinities Prescriptions (Jingiryō) intended to prevent evil spirits, such as demons and epidemic gods (ekishin), from entering the capital. This festival was conducted on the main roads (ōji) surrounding Kyoto in each of the four cardinal directions.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2023-09-29|title=Michiae no matsuri {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230929230332/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9286|access-date=2024-05-18|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
=== Historical Context === The precise origins of the Festival of the Road Gods are unclear, but its formal characteristics suggest it is closely linked to the establishment of capital cities in Japan. It likely dates back to the founding of the Fujiwara Palace in Nara at the end of the seventh century. The festival's association with the introduction of capital cities suggests its purpose was to protect these new urban centers from malevolent supernatural influences.<ref name=":0" />
=== Ritual Elements === The festival was performed by diviners (urabe) from the Department of Divinities (Jingikan), who utilized ritual implements made of fur. This indicates a strong connection to yin-yang divination practices (onmyōdō). The use of these implements and the involvement of the Department of Divinities highlight the festival's spiritual and protective purposes.<ref name=":0" />
=== Decline and Disappearance === The Festival of the Road Gods gradually disappeared during the mid-Heian period, influenced by the rise of the Festival of the Four Directions (Shikaku sai) and other similar observances. As these other festivals gained prominence, references to the Michiae no matsuri became scarce in historical records.<ref name=":0" />
=== Interpretations and Records === The Ryō no gige (Explanations of the Prescriptions) notes that the Michiae no matsuri aimed to prevent calamity-causing demons from entering the capital by intercepting and entertaining them in the streets. Conversely, the norito prayer recorded in the Engi Shiki (Engi Regulations) for this festival indicates that it celebrated three deities: Yachimata-hiko and other road deities. This suggests a dual interpretation of the festival's purpose: both as a means of protection from malevolent spirits and as a celebration of specific deities associated with the roads.<ref name=":0" /> *
== Footnotes == <references group="lower-alpha" /><references group="note" /> == Referennces == <references />{{draft categories|[[Category:Shinto festivals]] [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:June observances]] [[Category:December observances]] }}

{{User:Immanuelle/Japanese Imperial Rituals updated}}
The Festival of the Road Gods (道祖神祭り, Michiae no matsuri) was an ancient ceremony prescribed by the Divinities Prescriptions (Jingiryō) intended to prevent evil spirits, such as demons and epidemic gods (ekishin), from entering the capital. This festival was conducted on the main roads (ōji) surrounding Kyoto in each of the four cardinal directions.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-09-29|title=Michiae no matsuri {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230929230332/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9286|access-date=2024-05-18|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
== Historical Context == The precise origins of the Festival of the Road Gods are unclear, but its formal characteristics suggest it is closely linked to the establishment of capital cities in Japan. It likely dates back to the founding of the Fujiwara Palace in Nara at the end of the seventh century. The festival's association with the introduction of capital cities suggests its purpose was to protect these new urban centers from malevolent supernatural influences.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Ritual Elements == The festival was performed by diviners (urabe) from the Department of Divinities (Jingikan), who utilized ritual implements made of fur. This indicates a strong connection to yin-yang divination practices (onmyōdō). The use of these implements and the involvement of the Department of Divinities highlight the festival's spiritual and protective purposes.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Decline and Disappearance == The Festival of the Road Gods gradually disappeared during the mid-Heian period, influenced by the rise of the Festival of the Four Directions (Shikaku sai) and other similar observances. As these other festivals gained prominence, references to the Michiae no matsuri became scarce in historical records.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Interpretations and Records == The Ryō no gige (Explanations of the Prescriptions) notes that the Michiae no matsuri aimed to prevent calamity-causing demons from entering the capital by intercepting and entertaining them in the streets. Conversely, the norito prayer recorded in the Engi Shiki (Engi Regulations) for this festival indicates that it celebrated three deities: Yachimata-hiko and other road deities. This suggests a dual interpretation of the festival's purpose: both as a means of protection from malevolent spirits and as a celebration of specific deities associated with the roads.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" /> == Footnotes == <references group="lower-alpha" /><references group="note" /> == Referennces == <references />{{draft categories|[[Category:Shinto festivals]] [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:June observances]] [[Category:December observances]]

{{User:Immanuelle/Japanese Imperial Rituals updated}}
The Festival of the Road Gods (道祖神祭り, Michiae no matsuri) was an ancient ceremony prescribed by the Divinities Prescriptions (Jingiryō) intended to prevent evil spirits, such as demons and epidemic gods (ekishin), from entering the capital. This festival was conducted on the main roads (ōji) surrounding Kyoto in each of the four cardinal directions.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-09-29|title=Michiae no matsuri {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230929230332/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9286|access-date=2024-05-18|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
==Overview==
{{令制祭祀}} '''Michae no Matsuri''' (道饗祭, also read as ちあえのまつり) is one of the Shinto [[rites]]. It is held twice a year, in June and December, at the four corners of the capital's roads to enshrine the deities '''Yachimata-hiko''' (八衢比古神), '''Yachimata-hime''' (八衢比売神), and '''Kunado''' (久那斗神). The purpose of this ceremony is to prevent and protect the capital and the [[Imperial Palace]] from malevolent spirits such as [[demons]] and [[yōkai]], and to pray for divine protection.<ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/212207/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Michae no Matsuri] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> Although the ritual was centered in [[Kyoto]] under the [[Ritsuryō system]], it was also performed in rural areas during outbreaks of [[epidemics]].<ref name="da1">[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 World Encyclopedia, 2nd Edition: Michae no Matsuri] Heibonsha, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> In Musashi Province, the name "Ae-ba" (饗庭) appears frequently as a place name and surname, which folklorist [[Kunio Yanagita]] hypothesized to have originated from Michae no Matsuri.<ref name="da1"/> He also speculated that place names like "Aino-ta" and "Manota" in northern Musashi derived from "fields of Ae-ba."<ref>Kunio Yanagita, *Research on Place Names*, Kadokawa Bunko, 9th edition, 1974, p. 216.</ref>
== Overview == Michae no Matsuri is a recurring festival prescribed under the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令}}, one of the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} system's codes.<ref name="da1"/> It is listed in the "June Festivals" section of the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, compiled during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, and categorized as a "minor ritual."<ref group="注">Rites conducted with one day of purification beforehand under the Ritsuryō system.</ref><ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/108492/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Minor Ritual] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> The ceremony was overseen by the {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職}} (Capital Officials), with the {{ill|Ura-be clan|ja|卜部氏}} performing the purification.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 Britannica International Encyclopedia: Michae no Matsuri] Britannica Japanese Edition, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> It was often held in conjunction with the {{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火祭}} in Kyoto.<ref name="n19">Nishimuta 2003, p. 119</ref>
While traditionally thought to occur after the {{ill|Ōharae|ja|大祓}} (Great Purification) on the 30th day of the 6th and 12th lunar months, no specific festival date is recorded in the *Engishiki* or *Taika Reforms*, leading some to conclude that auspicious days were chosen instead, as suggested by the {{ill|Shūgaishō|ja|拾芥抄}}.<ref name="tu1">Tsugita 2008, p. 378</ref>
Shinto traditions often observe half-yearly rituals based on {{ill|seasonal agriculture|ja|農耕儀礼}}, the passage of {{ill|natural cycles|ja|自然観}}, and the transformations of plants and animals.<ref name="da1"/>
== Summary Table == {| class="wikitable" ! Item !! Description !! Remarks |- ! Deities | colspan="2"|Yachimata-hime (八衢比売神), Yachimata-hiko (八衢比古神), Kunado (久那斗神) |- ! Code | colspan="2"|Jingiryō, classified as a minor ritual |- | rowspan="3"|Legal References | * {{ill|Ōmi Code|ja|近江令}} (668 CE) |- | * {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}} (681 CE) |- | * {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}} (Chapter 6, Article 20, 718 CE) |- ! Dates | 6th and 12th lunar months || Specific dates vary |- ! Faith | rowspan="2"|Ancient Shinto || Includes natural worship |- | Rooted in ancestral worship |}
== Historical Interpretations == === *Kujigen* and *Nishinomiya-ki* === The {{ill|Kujigen|ja|公事根源}} by {{ill|Ichijō Kanera|ja|一条兼良}} misidentifies Michae no Matsuri as the "Festival of the Four Corners," combining it with the {{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火祭}}, which were separate.<ref name="ko1">Kōda 1976, p. 229</ref>
Meanwhile, the *Nishinomiya-ki* by {{ill|Minamoto no Takaaki|ja|源高明}} clarifies the distinctions: the "Four Corners Festival" was conducted during epidemic outbreaks at Kyoto's periphery, and the "Four Borders Festival" targeted the nation's outermost provinces.<ref name="tu2">Tsugita 2008, p. 380</ref>
Despite these clear distinctions, misunderstandings led later generations to conflate the rites.<ref name="tu1"/> Epidemic outbreaks further blurred the line between Michae no Matsuri and rites honoring pestilence gods.<ref name="tu3">Tsugita 2008, p. 381</ref>
== Liturgical Text == A key portion of the Michae no Matsuri prayer: {{Quotation|'''Original Text:''' 高天之原尓事始氐、皇御孫命止称辞竟奉、...|source=Engishiki}}
== Significance == Key phrases in the prayer include: * '''Takamahara''' (高天原) signifies the celestial origin of the ritual. * '''Ōyamato''' (大八衢) denotes sacred crossroads that safeguard the imperial capital.
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Annotations === {{Reflist|group="注"}} === References === {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * Nishimuta, Takaoki. *Heisei New Edition Norito Dictionary.* 2003, ISBN 4-900901-32-6. * Kōda, Toshio. *Annotated Annual Events.* 1976, ISBN 4-7971-0525-9. * Tsugita, Jun. *Revised Norito Interpretations.* 2008, ISBN 978-4-900901-85-8.
{{Shinto Sidebar}} {{Shinto-stub}}
[[Category:Shinto rituals]] [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:6th lunar month festivals]] [[Category:12th lunar month festivals]] [[ja:道饗祭]]
== Historical Context == The precise origins of the Festival of the Road Gods are unclear, but its formal characteristics suggest it is closely linked to the establishment of capital cities in Japan. It likely dates back to the founding of the Fujiwara Palace in Nara at the end of the seventh century. The festival's association with the introduction of capital cities suggests its purpose was to protect these new urban centers from malevolent supernatural influences.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Ritual Elements == The festival was performed by diviners (urabe) from the Department of Divinities (Jingikan), who utilized ritual implements made of fur. This indicates a strong connection to yin-yang divination practices (onmyōdō). The use of these implements and the involvement of the Department of Divinities highlight the festival's spiritual and protective purposes.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Decline and Disappearance == The Festival of the Road Gods gradually disappeared during the mid-Heian period, influenced by the rise of the Festival of the Four Directions (Shikaku sai) and other similar observances. As these other festivals gained prominence, references to the Michiae no matsuri became scarce in historical records.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Interpretations and Records == The Ryō no gige (Explanations of the Prescriptions) notes that the Michiae no matsuri aimed to prevent calamity-causing demons from entering the capital by intercepting and entertaining them in the streets. Conversely, the norito prayer recorded in the Engi Shiki (Engi Regulations) for this festival indicates that it celebrated three deities: Yachimata-hiko and other road deities. This suggests a dual interpretation of the festival's purpose: both as a means of protection from malevolent spirits and as a celebration of specific deities associated with the roads.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" /> == Footnotes == <references group="lower-alpha" /><references group="note" /> == Referennces == <references />{{draft categories|[[Category:Shinto festivals]] [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:June observances]] [[Category:December observances]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Michiae no matsuri]][[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Michiae no matsuri]][[en:Michiae no matsuri]][[en:Michiae no matsuri]][[simple:user:Immanuelle/{{PAGENAME}}]] }} [[ja:道饗祭]]

{{User:Immanuelle/Japanese Imperial Rituals updated}}
The Festival of the Road Gods (道祖神祭り, Michiae no matsuri) was an ancient ceremony prescribed by the Divinities Prescriptions (Jingiryō) intended to prevent evil spirits, such as demons and epidemic gods (ekishin), from entering the capital. This festival was conducted on the main roads (ōji) surrounding Kyoto in each of the four cardinal directions.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-09-29|title=Michiae no matsuri {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230929230332/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9286|access-date=2024-05-18|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
==Overview==
{{令制祭祀}} '''Michae no Matsuri''' (道饗祭, also read as ちあえのまつり) is one of the Shinto [[rites]]. It is held twice a year, in June and December, at the four corners of the capital's roads to enshrine the deities '''Yachimata-hiko''' (八衢比古神), '''Yachimata-hime''' (八衢比売神), and '''Kunado''' (久那斗神). The purpose of this ceremony is to prevent and protect the capital and the [[Imperial Palace]] from malevolent spirits such as [[demons]] and [[yōkai]], and to pray for divine protection.<ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/212207/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Michae no Matsuri] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> Although the ritual was centered in [[Kyoto]] under the [[Ritsuryō system]], it was also performed in rural areas during outbreaks of [[epidemics]].<ref name="da1">[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 World Encyclopedia, 2nd Edition: Michae no Matsuri] Heibonsha, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> In Musashi Province, the name "Ae-ba" (饗庭) appears frequently as a place name and surname, which folklorist [[Kunio Yanagita]] hypothesized to have originated from Michae no Matsuri.<ref name="da1"/> He also speculated that place names like "Aino-ta" and "Manota" in northern Musashi derived from "fields of Ae-ba."<ref>Kunio Yanagita, *Research on Place Names*, Kadokawa Bunko, 9th edition, 1974, p. 216.</ref>
== Overview == Michae no Matsuri is a recurring festival prescribed under the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令}}, one of the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} system's codes.<ref name="da1"/> It is listed in the "June Festivals" section of the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, compiled during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, and categorized as a "minor ritual."<ref group="注">Rites conducted with one day of purification beforehand under the Ritsuryō system.</ref><ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/108492/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Minor Ritual] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> The ceremony was overseen by the {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職}} (Capital Officials), with the {{ill|Ura-be clan|ja|卜部氏}} performing the purification.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 Britannica International Encyclopedia: Michae no Matsuri] Britannica Japanese Edition, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> It was often held in conjunction with the {{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火祭}} in Kyoto.<ref name="n19">Nishimuta 2003, p. 119</ref>
While traditionally thought to occur after the {{ill|Ōharae|ja|大祓}} (Great Purification) on the 30th day of the 6th and 12th lunar months, no specific festival date is recorded in the *Engishiki* or *Taika Reforms*, leading some to conclude that auspicious days were chosen instead, as suggested by the {{ill|Shūgaishō|ja|拾芥抄}}.<ref name="tu1">Tsugita 2008, p. 378</ref>
Shinto traditions often observe half-yearly rituals based on {{ill|seasonal agriculture|ja|農耕儀礼}}, the passage of {{ill|natural cycles|ja|自然観}}, and the transformations of plants and animals.<ref name="da1"/>
== Summary Table == {| class="wikitable" ! Item !! Description !! Remarks |- ! Deities | colspan="2"|Yachimata-hime (八衢比売神), Yachimata-hiko (八衢比古神), Kunado (久那斗神) |- ! Code | colspan="2"|Jingiryō, classified as a minor ritual |- | rowspan="3"|Legal References | * {{ill|Ōmi Code|ja|近江令}} (668 CE) |- | * {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}} (681 CE) |- | * {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}} (Chapter 6, Article 20, 718 CE) |- ! Dates | 6th and 12th lunar months || Specific dates vary |- ! Faith | rowspan="2"|Ancient Shinto || Includes natural worship |- | Rooted in ancestral worship |}
== Historical Interpretations == === *Kujigen* and *Nishinomiya-ki* === The {{ill|Kujigen|ja|公事根源}} by {{ill|Ichijō Kanera|ja|一条兼良}} misidentifies Michae no Matsuri as the "Festival of the Four Corners," combining it with the {{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火祭}}, which were separate.<ref name="ko1">Kōda 1976, p. 229</ref>
Meanwhile, the *Nishinomiya-ki* by {{ill|Minamoto no Takaaki|ja|源高明}} clarifies the distinctions: the "Four Corners Festival" was conducted during epidemic outbreaks at Kyoto's periphery, and the "Four Borders Festival" targeted the nation's outermost provinces.<ref name="tu2">Tsugita 2008, p. 380</ref>
Despite these clear distinctions, misunderstandings led later generations to conflate the rites.<ref name="tu1"/> Epidemic outbreaks further blurred the line between Michae no Matsuri and rites honoring pestilence gods.<ref name="tu3">Tsugita 2008, p. 381</ref>
== Liturgical Text == A key portion of the Michae no Matsuri prayer: {{Quotation|'''Original Text:''' 高天之原尓事始氐、皇御孫命止称辞竟奉、...|source=Engishiki}}
== Significance == Key phrases in the prayer include: * '''Takamahara''' (高天原) signifies the celestial origin of the ritual. * '''Ōyamato''' (大八衢) denotes sacred crossroads that safeguard the imperial capital.
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Annotations === {{Reflist|group="注"}} === References === {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * Nishimuta, Takaoki. *Heisei New Edition Norito Dictionary.* 2003, ISBN 4-900901-32-6. * Kōda, Toshio. *Annotated Annual Events.* 1976, ISBN 4-7971-0525-9. * Tsugita, Jun. *Revised Norito Interpretations.* 2008, ISBN 978-4-900901-85-8.
{{Shinto Sidebar}} {{Shinto-stub}}
[[Category:Shinto rituals]] [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:6th lunar month festivals]] [[Category:12th lunar month festivals]] [[ja:道饗祭]]
== Historical Context == The precise origins of the Festival of the Road Gods are unclear, but its formal characteristics suggest it is closely linked to the establishment of capital cities in Japan. It likely dates back to the founding of the Fujiwara Palace in Nara at the end of the seventh century. The festival's association with the introduction of capital cities suggests its purpose was to protect these new urban centers from malevolent supernatural influences.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Ritual Elements == The festival was performed by diviners (urabe) from the Department of Divinities (Jingikan), who utilized ritual implements made of fur. This indicates a strong connection to yin-yang divination practices (onmyōdō). The use of these implements and the involvement of the Department of Divinities highlight the festival's spiritual and protective purposes.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Decline and Disappearance == The Festival of the Road Gods gradually disappeared during the mid-Heian period, influenced by the rise of the Festival of the Four Directions (Shikaku sai) and other similar observances. As these other festivals gained prominence, references to the Michiae no matsuri became scarce in historical records.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Interpretations and Records == The Ryō no gige (Explanations of the Prescriptions) notes that the Michiae no matsuri aimed to prevent calamity-causing demons from entering the capital by intercepting and entertaining them in the streets. Conversely, the norito prayer recorded in the Engi Shiki (Engi Regulations) for this festival indicates that it celebrated three deities: Yachimata-hiko and other road deities. This suggests a dual interpretation of the festival's purpose: both as a means of protection from malevolent spirits and as a celebration of specific deities associated with the roads.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" /> == Footnotes == <references group="lower-alpha" /><references group="note" /> == Referennces == <references />{{draft categories|[[Category:Shinto festivals]] [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:June observances]] [[Category:December observances]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Michiae no matsuri]][[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Michiae no matsuri]][[en:Michiae no matsuri]][[en:Michiae no matsuri]][[simple:user:Immanuelle/{{PAGENAME}}]] }} [[ja:道饗祭]]
