[[simple:user:immanuelle/Michiae no matsuri]][[en:Michiae no matsuri]][[en:Michiae no matsuri]][[simple:user:Immanuelle/{{PAGENAME}}]] }} [[ja:道饗祭]]

{{User:Immanuelle/Japanese Imperial Rituals updated}}
The Festival of the Road Gods (道祖神祭り, Michiae no matsuri) was an ancient ceremony prescribed by the Divinities Prescriptions (Jingiryō) intended to prevent evil spirits, such as demons and epidemic gods (ekishin), from entering the capital. This festival was conducted on the main roads (ōji) surrounding Kyoto in each of the four cardinal directions.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-09-29|title=Michiae no matsuri {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230929230332/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9286|access-date=2024-05-18|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
==Overview==
{{令制祭祀}} '''Michae no Matsuri''' (道饗祭, also read as ちあえのまつり) is one of the Shinto [[rites]]. It is held twice a year, in June and December, at the four corners of the capital's roads to enshrine the deities '''Yachimata-hiko''' (八衢比古神), '''Yachimata-hime''' (八衢比売神), and '''Kunado''' (久那斗神). The purpose of this ceremony is to prevent and protect the capital and the [[Imperial Palace]] from malevolent spirits such as [[demons]] and [[yōkai]], and to pray for divine protection.<ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/212207/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Michae no Matsuri] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> Although the ritual was centered in [[Kyoto]] under the [[Ritsuryō system]], it was also performed in rural areas during outbreaks of [[epidemics]].<ref name="da1">[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 World Encyclopedia, 2nd Edition: Michae no Matsuri] Heibonsha, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> In Musashi Province, the name "Ae-ba" (饗庭) appears frequently as a place name and surname, which folklorist [[Kunio Yanagita]] hypothesized to have originated from Michae no Matsuri.<ref name="da1"/> He also speculated that place names like "Aino-ta" and "Manota" in northern Musashi derived from "fields of Ae-ba."<ref>Kunio Yanagita, *Research on Place Names*, Kadokawa Bunko, 9th edition, 1974, p. 216.</ref>
== Overview == Michae no Matsuri is a recurring festival prescribed under the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令}}, one of the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} system's codes.<ref name="da1"/> It is listed in the "June Festivals" section of the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, compiled during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, and categorized as a "minor ritual."<ref group="注">Rites conducted with one day of purification beforehand under the Ritsuryō system.</ref><ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/108492/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Minor Ritual] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> The ceremony was overseen by the {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職}} (Capital Officials), with the {{ill|Urabe clan|ja|卜部氏}} performing the purification.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 Britannica International Encyclopedia: Michae no Matsuri] Britannica Japanese Edition, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> It was often held in conjunction with the {{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火祭}} in Kyoto.<ref name="n19">Nishimuta 2003, p. 119</ref>
While traditionally thought to occur after the {{ill|Ōharae|ja|大祓}} (Great Purification) on the 30th day of the 6th and 12th lunar months, no specific festival date is recorded in the *Engishiki* or *Taika Reforms*, leading some to conclude that auspicious days were chosen instead, as suggested by the {{ill|Shūgaishō|ja|拾芥抄}}.<ref name="tu1">Tsugita 2008, p. 378</ref>
Shinto traditions often observe half-yearly rituals based on {{ill|seasonal agriculture|ja|農耕儀礼}}, the passage of {{ill|natural cycles|ja|自然観}}, and the transformations of plants and animals.<ref name="da1"/>
== Summary Table == {| class="wikitable" ! Item !! Description !! Remarks |- ! Deities | colspan="2"|Yachimata-hime (八衢比売神), Yachimata-hiko (八衢比古神), Kunado (久那斗神) |- ! Code | colspan="2"|Jingiryō, classified as a minor ritual |- | rowspan="3"|Legal References | * {{ill|Ōmi Code|ja|近江令}} (668 CE) |- | * {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}} (681 CE) |- | * {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}} (Chapter 6, Article 20, 718 CE) |- ! Dates | 6th and 12th lunar months || Specific dates vary |- ! Faith | rowspan="2"|Ancient Shinto || Includes natural worship |- | Rooted in ancestral worship |}
== Historical Interpretations == === *Kujigen* and *Nishinomiya-ki* === The {{ill|Kujigen|ja|公事根源}} by {{ill|Ichijō Kanera|ja|一条兼良}} misidentifies Michae no Matsuri as the "Festival of the Four Corners," combining it with the {{ill|Fire Calming Festival|ja|鎮火祭}}, which were separate.<ref name="ko1">Kōda 1976, p. 229</ref>
Meanwhile, the *Nishinomiya-ki* by {{ill|Minamoto no Takaaki|ja|源高明}} clarifies the distinctions: the "Four Corners Festival" was conducted during epidemic outbreaks at Kyoto's periphery, and the "Four Borders Festival" targeted the nation's outermost provinces.<ref name="tu2">Tsugita 2008, p. 380</ref>
Despite these clear distinctions, misunderstandings led later generations to conflate the rites.<ref name="tu1"/> Epidemic outbreaks further blurred the line between Michae no Matsuri and rites honoring pestilence gods.<ref name="tu3">Tsugita 2008, p. 381</ref>
== Liturgical Text == A key portion of the Michae no Matsuri prayer: {{Quotation|'''Original Text:''' 高天之原尓事始氐、皇御孫命止称辞竟奉、...|source=Engishiki}}
== Significance == Key phrases in the prayer include: * '''Takamahara''' (高天原) signifies the celestial origin of the ritual. * '''Ōyamato''' (大八衢) denotes sacred crossroads that safeguard the imperial capital.
[[Category:Shinto rituals]] [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:6th lunar month festivals]] [[Category:12th lunar month festivals]] [[ja:道饗祭]]
== Historical Context == The precise origins of the Festival of the Road Gods are unclear, but its formal characteristics suggest it is closely linked to the establishment of capital cities in Japan. It likely dates back to the founding of the Fujiwara Palace in Nara at the end of the seventh century. The festival's association with the introduction of capital cities suggests its purpose was to protect these new urban centers from malevolent supernatural influences.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Ritual Elements == The festival was performed by diviners (urabe) from the Department of Divinities (Jingikan), who utilized ritual implements made of fur. This indicates a strong connection to yin-yang divination practices (onmyōdō). The use of these implements and the involvement of the Department of Divinities highlight the festival's spiritual and protective purposes.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Decline and Disappearance == The Festival of the Road Gods gradually disappeared during the mid-Heian period, influenced by the rise of the Festival of the Four Directions (Shikaku sai) and other similar observances. As these other festivals gained prominence, references to the Michiae no matsuri became scarce in historical records.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Interpretations and Records == The Ryō no gige (Explanations of the Prescriptions) notes that the Michiae no matsuri aimed to prevent calamity-causing demons from entering the capital by intercepting and entertaining them in the streets. Conversely, the norito prayer recorded in the Engi Shiki (Engi Regulations) for this festival indicates that it celebrated three deities: Yachimata-hiko and other road deities. This suggests a dual interpretation of the festival's purpose: both as a means of protection from malevolent spirits and as a celebration of specific deities associated with the roads.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Annotations === {{Reflist|group="注"}} <references group="lower-alpha"/>
=== References === {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * Nishimuta, Takaoki. *Heisei New Edition Norito Dictionary.* 2003, ISBN 4-900901-32-6. * Kōda, Toshio. *Annotated Annual Events.* 1976, ISBN 4-7971-0525-9. * Tsugita, Jun. *Revised Norito Interpretations.* 2008, ISBN 978-4-900901-85-8.

== Decline and Disappearance == The Festival of the Road Gods gradually disappeared during the mid-Heian period, influenced by the rise of the Festival of the Four Directions (Shikaku sai) and other similar observances. As these other festivals gained prominence, references to the Michiae no matsuri became scarce in historical records.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Interpretations and Records == The Ryō no gige (Explanations of the Prescriptions) notes that the Michiae no matsuri aimed to prevent calamity-causing demons from entering the capital by intercepting and entertaining them in the streets. Conversely, the norito prayer recorded in the Engi Shiki (Engi Regulations) for this festival indicates that it celebrated three deities: Yachimata-hiko and other road deities. This suggests a dual interpretation of the festival's purpose: both as a means of protection from malevolent spirits and as a celebration of specific deities associated with the roads.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Annotations === {{Reflist|group="注"}} <references group="lower-alpha"/>

{{draft categories|[[Category:Shinto festivals]] [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:June observances]] [[Category:December observances]] [[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Michiae no matsuri]][[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Michiae no matsuri-qq]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Michiae no matsuri-qq]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Michiae no matsuri-qq]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]]
[[Category:kkk]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Michiae no matsuri]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]]
[[simple:user:immanuelle/Michiae no matsuri]][[en:Michiae no matsuri]][[en:Michiae no matsuri]][[simple:user:Immanuelle/{{PAGENAME}}]] }} [[ja:道饗祭]]

{{User:Immanuelle/Japanese Imperial Rituals updated}}
The Festival of the Road Gods (道祖神祭り, Michiae no matsuri) was an ancient ceremony prescribed by the Divinities Prescriptions (Jingiryō) intended to prevent evil spirits, such as demons and epidemic gods (ekishin), from entering the capital. This festival was conducted on the main roads (ōji) surrounding Kyoto in each of the four cardinal directions.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-09-29|title=Michiae no matsuri {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230929230332/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9286|access-date=2024-05-18|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
==Overview==
{{令制祭祀}} '''Michae no Matsuri''' (道饗祭, also read as ちあえのまつり) is one of the Shinto [[rites]]. It is held twice a year, in June and December, at the four corners of the capital's roads to enshrine the deities '''Yachimata-hiko''' (八衢比古神), '''Yachimata-hime''' (八衢比売神), and '''Kunado''' (久那斗神). The purpose of this ceremony is to prevent and protect the capital and the [[Imperial Palace]] from malevolent spirits such as [[demons]] and [[yōkai]], and to pray for divine protection.<ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/212207/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Michae no Matsuri] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> Although the ritual was centered in [[Kyoto]] under the [[Ritsuryō system]], it was also performed in rural areas during outbreaks of [[epidemics]].<ref name="da1">[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 World Encyclopedia, 2nd Edition: Michae no Matsuri] Heibonsha, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> In Musashi Province, the name "Ae-ba" (饗庭) appears frequently as a place name and surname, which folklorist [[Kunio Yanagita]] hypothesized to have originated from Michae no Matsuri.<ref name="da1"/> He also speculated that place names like "Aino-ta" and "Manota" in northern Musashi derived from "fields of Ae-ba."<ref>Kunio Yanagita, *Research on Place Names*, Kadokawa Bunko, 9th edition, 1974, p. 216.</ref>
== Overview == Michae no Matsuri is a recurring festival prescribed under the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令}}, one of the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} system's codes.<ref name="da1"/> It is listed in the "June Festivals" section of the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, compiled during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, and categorized as a "minor ritual."<ref group="注">Rites conducted with one day of purification beforehand under the Ritsuryō system.</ref><ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/108492/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Minor Ritual] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> The ceremony was overseen by the {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職}} (Capital Officials), with the {{ill|Urabe clan|ja|卜部氏}} performing the purification.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 Britannica International Encyclopedia: Michae no Matsuri] Britannica Japanese Edition, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> It was often held in conjunction with the {{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火祭}} in Kyoto.<ref name="n19">Nishimuta 2003, p. 119</ref>
While traditionally thought to occur after the {{ill|Ōharae|ja|大祓}} (Great Purification) on the 30th day of the 6th and 12th lunar months, no specific festival date is recorded in the *Engishiki* or *Taika Reforms*, leading some to conclude that auspicious days were chosen instead, as suggested by the {{ill|Shūgaishō|ja|拾芥抄}}.<ref name="tu1">Tsugita 2008, p. 378</ref>
Shinto traditions often observe half-yearly rituals based on {{ill|seasonal agriculture|ja|農耕儀礼}}, the passage of {{ill|natural cycles|ja|自然観}}, and the transformations of plants and animals.<ref name="da1"/>
== Summary Table == {| class="wikitable" ! Item !! Description !! Remarks |- ! Deities | colspan="2"|Yachimata-hime (八衢比売神), Yachimata-hiko (八衢比古神), Kunado (久那斗神) |- ! Code | colspan="2"|Jingiryō, classified as a minor ritual |- | rowspan="3"|Legal References | * {{ill|Ōmi Code|ja|近江令}} (668 CE) |- | * {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}} (681 CE) |- | * {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}} (Chapter 6, Article 20, 718 CE) |- ! Dates | 6th and 12th lunar months || Specific dates vary |- ! Faith | rowspan="2"|Ancient Shinto || Includes natural worship |- | Rooted in ancestral worship |}
== Historical Interpretations == === *Kujigen* and *Nishinomiya-ki* === The {{ill|Kujigen|ja|公事根源}} by {{ill|Ichijō Kanera|ja|一条兼良}} misidentifies Michae no Matsuri as the "Festival of the Four Corners," combining it with the {{ill|Fire Calming Festival|ja|鎮火祭}}, which were separate.<ref name="ko1">Kōda 1976, p. 229</ref>
Meanwhile, the *Nishinomiya-ki* by {{ill|Minamoto no Takaaki|ja|源高明}} clarifies the distinctions: the "Four Corners Festival" was conducted during epidemic outbreaks at Kyoto's periphery, and the "Four Borders Festival" targeted the nation's outermost provinces.<ref name="tu2">Tsugita 2008, p. 380</ref>
Despite these clear distinctions, misunderstandings led later generations to conflate the rites.<ref name="tu1"/> Epidemic outbreaks further blurred the line between Michae no Matsuri and rites honoring pestilence gods.<ref name="tu3">Tsugita 2008, p. 381</ref>
== Liturgical Text == A key portion of the Michae no Matsuri prayer: {{Quotation|'''Original Text:''' 高天之原尓事始氐、皇御孫命止称辞竟奉、...|source=Engishiki}}
== Significance == Key phrases in the prayer include: * '''Takamahara''' (高天原) signifies the celestial origin of the ritual. * '''Ōyamato''' (大八衢) denotes sacred crossroads that safeguard the imperial capital.
[[Category:Shinto rituals]] [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:6th lunar month festivals]] [[Category:12th lunar month festivals]] [[ja:道饗祭]]
== Historical Context == The precise origins of the Festival of the Road Gods are unclear, but its formal characteristics suggest it is closely linked to the establishment of capital cities in Japan. It likely dates back to the founding of the Fujiwara Palace in Nara at the end of the seventh century. The festival's association with the introduction of capital cities suggests its purpose was to protect these new urban centers from malevolent supernatural influences.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Ritual Elements == The festival was performed by diviners (urabe) from the Department of Divinities (Jingikan), who utilized ritual implements made of fur. This indicates a strong connection to yin-yang divination practices (onmyōdō). The use of these implements and the involvement of the Department of Divinities highlight the festival's spiritual and protective purposes.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Decline and Disappearance == The Festival of the Road Gods gradually disappeared during the mid-Heian period, influenced by the rise of the Festival of the Four Directions (Shikaku sai) and other similar observances. As these other festivals gained prominence, references to the Michiae no matsuri became scarce in historical records.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Interpretations and Records == The Ryō no gige (Explanations of the Prescriptions) notes that the Michiae no matsuri aimed to prevent calamity-causing demons from entering the capital by intercepting and entertaining them in the streets. Conversely, the norito prayer recorded in the Engi Shiki (Engi Regulations) for this festival indicates that it celebrated three deities: Yachimata-hiko and other road deities. This suggests a dual interpretation of the festival's purpose: both as a means of protection from malevolent spirits and as a celebration of specific deities associated with the roads.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Annotations === {{Reflist|group="注"}} <references group="lower-alpha"/>

== Historical Interpretations == === *Kujigen* and *Nishinomiya-ki* === The {{ill|Kujigen|ja|公事根源}} by {{ill|Ichijō Kanera|ja|一条兼良}} misidentifies Michae no Matsuri as the "Festival of the Four Corners," combining it with the {{ill|Fire Calming Festival|ja|鎮火祭}}, which were separate.<ref name="ko1">Kōda 1976, p. 229</ref>
Meanwhile, the *Nishinomiya-ki* by {{ill|Minamoto no Takaaki|ja|源高明}} clarifies the distinctions: the "Four Corners Festival" was conducted during epidemic outbreaks at Kyoto's periphery, and the "Four Borders Festival" targeted the nation's outermost provinces.<ref name="tu2">Tsugita 2008, p. 380</ref>
Despite these clear distinctions, misunderstandings led later generations to conflate the rites.<ref name="tu1"/> Epidemic outbreaks further blurred the line between Michae no Matsuri and rites honoring pestilence gods.<ref name="tu3">Tsugita 2008, p. 381</ref>
== Liturgical Text == A key portion of the Michae no Matsuri prayer: {{Quotation|'''Original Text:''' 高天之原尓事始氐、皇御孫命止称辞竟奉、...|source=Engishiki}}
== Significance == Key phrases in the prayer include: * '''Takamahara''' (高天原) signifies the celestial origin of the ritual. * '''Ōyamato''' (大八衢) denotes sacred crossroads that safeguard the imperial capital.
[[Category:Shinto rituals]] [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:6th lunar month festivals]] [[Category:12th lunar month festivals]] [[ja:道饗祭]]
== Historical Context == The precise origins of the Festival of the Road Gods are unclear, but its formal characteristics suggest it is closely linked to the establishment of capital cities in Japan. It likely dates back to the founding of the Fujiwara Palace in Nara at the end of the seventh century. The festival's association with the introduction of capital cities suggests its purpose was to protect these new urban centers from malevolent supernatural influences.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Ritual Elements == The festival was performed by diviners (urabe) from the Department of Divinities (Jingikan), who utilized ritual implements made of fur. This indicates a strong connection to yin-yang divination practices (onmyōdō). The use of these implements and the involvement of the Department of Divinities highlight the festival's spiritual and protective purposes.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Decline and Disappearance == The Festival of the Road Gods gradually disappeared during the mid-Heian period, influenced by the rise of the Festival of the Four Directions (Shikaku sai) and other similar observances. As these other festivals gained prominence, references to the Michiae no matsuri became scarce in historical records.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Interpretations and Records == The Ryō no gige (Explanations of the Prescriptions) notes that the Michiae no matsuri aimed to prevent calamity-causing demons from entering the capital by intercepting and entertaining them in the streets. Conversely, the norito prayer recorded in the Engi Shiki (Engi Regulations) for this festival indicates that it celebrated three deities: Yachimata-hiko and other road deities. This suggests a dual interpretation of the festival's purpose: both as a means of protection from malevolent spirits and as a celebration of specific deities associated with the roads.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Annotations === {{Reflist|group="注"}} <references group="lower-alpha"/>

{{draft categories|[[Category:Shinto festivals]] [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:June observances]] [[Category:December observances]] [[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Michiae no matsuri]][[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Michiae no matsuri-qq]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Michiae no matsuri-qq]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Michiae no matsuri-qq]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]]
[[Category:kkk]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Michiae no matsuri]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Michiae no matsuri]][[en:Michiae no matsuri]][[en:Michiae no matsuri]][[simple:user:Immanuelle/{{PAGENAME}}]] }} [[ja:道饗祭]]

{{User:Immanuelle/Japanese Imperial Rituals updated}}
The Festival of the Road Gods (道祖神祭り, Michiae no matsuri) was an ancient ceremony prescribed by the Divinities Prescriptions (Jingiryō) intended to prevent evil spirits, such as demons and epidemic gods (ekishin), from entering the capital. This festival was conducted on the main roads (ōji) surrounding Kyoto in each of the four cardinal directions.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-09-29|title=Michiae no matsuri {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230929230332/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9286|access-date=2024-05-18|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
==Overview==
{{令制祭祀}} '''Michae no Matsuri''' (道饗祭, also read as ちあえのまつり) is one of the Shinto [[rites]]. It is held twice a year, in June and December, at the four corners of the capital's roads to enshrine the deities '''Yachimata-hiko''' (八衢比古神), '''Yachimata-hime''' (八衢比売神), and '''Kunado''' (久那斗神). The purpose of this ceremony is to prevent and protect the capital and the [[Imperial Palace]] from malevolent spirits such as [[demons]] and [[yōkai]], and to pray for divine protection.<ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/212207/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Michae no Matsuri] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> Although the ritual was centered in [[Kyoto]] under the [[Ritsuryō system]], it was also performed in rural areas during outbreaks of [[epidemics]].<ref name="da1">[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 World Encyclopedia, 2nd Edition: Michae no Matsuri] Heibonsha, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> In Musashi Province, the name "Ae-ba" (饗庭) appears frequently as a place name and surname, which folklorist [[Kunio Yanagita]] hypothesized to have originated from Michae no Matsuri.<ref name="da1"/> He also speculated that place names like "Aino-ta" and "Manota" in northern Musashi derived from "fields of Ae-ba."<ref>Kunio Yanagita, *Research on Place Names*, Kadokawa Bunko, 9th edition, 1974, p. 216.</ref>
== Overview == Michae no Matsuri is a recurring festival prescribed under the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令}}, one of the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} system's codes.<ref name="da1"/> It is listed in the "June Festivals" section of the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, compiled during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, and categorized as a "minor ritual."<ref group="注">Rites conducted with one day of purification beforehand under the Ritsuryō system.</ref><ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/108492/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Minor Ritual] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> The ceremony was overseen by the {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職}} (Capital Officials), with the {{ill|Urabe clan|ja|卜部氏}} performing the purification.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 Britannica International Encyclopedia: Michae no Matsuri] Britannica Japanese Edition, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> It was often held in conjunction with the {{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火祭}} in Kyoto.<ref name="n19">Nishimuta 2003, p. 119</ref>
While traditionally thought to occur after the {{ill|Ōharae|ja|大祓}} (Great Purification) on the 30th day of the 6th and 12th lunar months, no specific festival date is recorded in the *Engishiki* or *Taika Reforms*, leading some to conclude that auspicious days were chosen instead, as suggested by the {{ill|Shūgaishō|ja|拾芥抄}}.<ref name="tu1">Tsugita 2008, p. 378</ref>
Shinto traditions often observe half-yearly rituals based on {{ill|seasonal agriculture|ja|農耕儀礼}}, the passage of {{ill|natural cycles|ja|自然観}}, and the transformations of plants and animals.<ref name="da1"/>
== Summary Table == {| class="wikitable" ! Item !! Description !! Remarks |- ! Deities | colspan="2"|Yachimata-hime (八衢比売神), Yachimata-hiko (八衢比古神), Kunado (久那斗神) |- ! Code | colspan="2"|Jingiryō, classified as a minor ritual |- | rowspan="3"|Legal References | * {{ill|Ōmi Code|ja|近江令}} (668 CE) |- | * {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}} (681 CE) |- | * {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}} (Chapter 6, Article 20, 718 CE) |- ! Dates | 6th and 12th lunar months || Specific dates vary |- ! Faith | rowspan="2"|Ancient Shinto || Includes natural worship |- | Rooted in ancestral worship |}

Meanwhile, the *Nishinomiya-ki* by {{ill|Minamoto no Takaaki|ja|源高明}} clarifies the distinctions: the "Four Corners Festival" was conducted during epidemic outbreaks at Kyoto's periphery, and the "Four Borders Festival" targeted the nation's outermost provinces.<ref name="tu2">Tsugita 2008, p. 380</ref>
Despite these clear distinctions, misunderstandings led later generations to conflate the rites.<ref name="tu1"/> Epidemic outbreaks further blurred the line between Michae no Matsuri and rites honoring pestilence gods.<ref name="tu3">Tsugita 2008, p. 381</ref>

== Significance == Key phrases in the prayer include: * '''Takamahara''' (高天原) signifies the celestial origin of the ritual. * '''Ōyamato''' (大八衢) denotes sacred crossroads that safeguard the imperial capital.

== Overview == Michae no Matsuri is a recurring festival prescribed under the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令}}, one of the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} system's codes.<ref name="da1"/> It is listed in the "June Festivals" section of the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, compiled during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, and categorized as a "minor ritual."<ref group="注">Rites conducted with one day of purification beforehand under the Ritsuryō system.</ref><ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/108492/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Minor Ritual] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> The ceremony was overseen by the {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職}} (Capital Officials), with the {{ill|Urabe clan|ja|卜部氏}} performing the purification.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 Britannica International Encyclopedia: Michae no Matsuri] Britannica Japanese Edition, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> It was often held in conjunction with the {{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火祭}} in Kyoto.<ref name="n19">Nishimuta 2003, p. 119</ref>
While traditionally thought to occur after the {{ill|Ōharae|ja|大祓}} (Great Purification) on the 30th day of the 6th and 12th lunar months, no specific festival date is recorded in the *Engishiki* or *Taika Reforms*, leading some to conclude that auspicious days were chosen instead, as suggested by the {{ill|Shūgaishō|ja|拾芥抄}}.<ref name="tu1">Tsugita 2008, p. 378</ref>
Shinto traditions often observe half-yearly rituals based on {{ill|seasonal agriculture|ja|農耕儀礼}}, the passage of {{ill|natural cycles|ja|自然観}}, and the transformations of plants and animals.<ref name="da1"/>

Code | colspan="2"|Jingiryō, classified as a minor ritual |- | rowspan="3"|Legal References | * {{ill|Ōmi Code|ja|近江令}} (668 CE) |- | * {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}} (681 CE) |- | * {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}} (Chapter 6, Article 20, 718 CE) |- ! Dates | 6th and 12th lunar months || Specific dates vary |- ! Faith | rowspan="2"|Ancient Shinto || Includes natural worship |- | Rooted in ancestral worship |}
== Historical Interpretations == === *Kujigen* and *Nishinomiya-ki* === The {{ill|Kujigen|ja|公事根源}} by {{ill|Ichijō Kanera|ja|一条兼良}} misidentifies Michae no Matsuri as the "Festival of the Four Corners," combining it with the {{ill|Fire Calming Festival|ja|鎮火祭}}, which were separate.<ref name="ko1">Kōda 1976, p. 229</ref>
Meanwhile, the *Nishinomiya-ki* by {{ill|Minamoto no Takaaki|ja|源高明}} clarifies the distinctions: the "Four Corners Festival" was conducted during epidemic outbreaks at Kyoto's periphery, and the "Four Borders Festival" targeted the nation's outermost provinces.<ref name="tu2">Tsugita 2008, p. 380</ref>
Despite these clear distinctions, misunderstandings led later generations to conflate the rites.<ref name="tu1"/> Epidemic outbreaks further blurred the line between Michae no Matsuri and rites honoring pestilence gods.<ref name="tu3">Tsugita 2008, p. 381</ref>
== Liturgical Text == A key portion of the Michae no Matsuri prayer: {{Quotation|'''Original Text:''' 高天之原尓事始氐、皇御孫命止称辞竟奉、...|source=Engishiki}}
== Significance == Key phrases in the prayer include: * '''Takamahara''' (高天原) signifies the celestial origin of the ritual. * '''Ōyamato''' (大八衢) denotes sacred crossroads that safeguard the imperial capital.
[[Category:Shinto rituals]] [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:6th lunar month festivals]] [[Category:12th lunar month festivals]] [[ja:道饗祭]]
== Historical Context == The precise origins of the Festival of the Road Gods are unclear, but its formal characteristics suggest it is closely linked to the establishment of capital cities in Japan. It likely dates back to the founding of the Fujiwara Palace in Nara at the end of the seventh century. The festival's association with the introduction of capital cities suggests its purpose was to protect these new urban centers from malevolent supernatural influences.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Ritual Elements == The festival was performed by diviners (urabe) from the Department of Divinities (Jingikan), who utilized ritual implements made of fur. This indicates a strong connection to yin-yang divination practices (onmyōdō). The use of these implements and the involvement of the Department of Divinities highlight the festival's spiritual and protective purposes.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Decline and Disappearance == The Festival of the Road Gods gradually disappeared during the mid-Heian period, influenced by the rise of the Festival of the Four Directions (Shikaku sai) and other similar observances. As these other festivals gained prominence, references to the Michiae no matsuri became scarce in historical records.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Interpretations and Records == The Ryō no gige (Explanations of the Prescriptions) notes that the Michiae no matsuri aimed to prevent calamity-causing demons from entering the capital by intercepting and entertaining them in the streets. Conversely, the norito prayer recorded in the Engi Shiki (Engi Regulations) for this festival indicates that it celebrated three deities: Yachimata-hiko and other road deities. This suggests a dual interpretation of the festival's purpose: both as a means of protection from malevolent spirits and as a celebration of specific deities associated with the roads.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Annotations === {{Reflist|group="注"}} <references group="lower-alpha"/>

{{User:Immanuelle/Japanese Imperial Rituals updated}}
The Festival of the Road Gods (道祖神祭り, Michiae no matsuri) was an ancient ceremony prescribed by the Divinities Prescriptions (Jingiryō) intended to prevent evil spirits, such as demons and epidemic gods (ekishin), from entering the capital. This festival was conducted on the main roads (ōji) surrounding Kyoto in each of the four cardinal directions.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-09-29|title=Michiae no matsuri {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230929230332/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9286|access-date=2024-05-18|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
==Overview==
{{令制祭祀}} '''Michae no Matsuri''' (道饗祭, also read as ちあえのまつり) is one of the Shinto [[rites]]. It is held twice a year, in June and December, at the four corners of the capital's roads to enshrine the deities '''Yachimata-hiko''' (八衢比古神), '''Yachimata-hime''' (八衢比売神), and '''Kunado''' (久那斗神). The purpose of this ceremony is to prevent and protect the capital and the [[Imperial Palace]] from malevolent spirits such as [[demons]] and [[yōkai]], and to pray for divine protection.<ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/212207/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Michae no Matsuri] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> Although the ritual was centered in [[Kyoto]] under the [[Ritsuryō system]], it was also performed in rural areas during outbreaks of [[epidemics]].<ref name="da1">[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 World Encyclopedia, 2nd Edition: Michae no Matsuri] Heibonsha, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> In Musashi Province, the name "Ae-ba" (饗庭) appears frequently as a place name and surname, which folklorist [[Kunio Yanagita]] hypothesized to have originated from Michae no Matsuri.<ref name="da1"/> He also speculated that place names like "Aino-ta" and "Manota" in northern Musashi derived from "fields of Ae-ba."<ref>Kunio Yanagita, *Research on Place Names*, Kadokawa Bunko, 9th edition, 1974, p. 216.</ref>

== Decline and Disappearance == The Festival of the Road Gods gradually disappeared during the mid-Heian period, influenced by the rise of the Festival of the Four Directions (Shikaku sai) and other similar observances. As these other festivals gained prominence, references to the Michiae no matsuri became scarce in historical records.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Interpretations and Records == The Ryō no gige (Explanations of the Prescriptions) notes that the Michiae no matsuri aimed to prevent calamity-causing demons from entering the capital by intercepting and entertaining them in the streets. Conversely, the norito prayer recorded in the Engi Shiki (Engi Regulations) for this festival indicates that it celebrated three deities: Yachimata-hiko and other road deities. This suggests a dual interpretation of the festival's purpose: both as a means of protection from malevolent spirits and as a celebration of specific deities associated with the roads.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />

{{User:Immanuelle/Japanese Imperial Rituals updated}}
The Festival of the Road Gods (道祖神祭り, Michiae no matsuri) was an ancient ceremony prescribed by the Divinities Prescriptions (Jingiryō) intended to prevent evil spirits, such as demons and epidemic gods (ekishin), from entering the capital. This festival was conducted on the main roads (ōji) surrounding Kyoto in each of the four cardinal directions.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-09-29|title=Michiae no matsuri {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230929230332/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9286|access-date=2024-05-18|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
==Overview==
{{令制祭祀}} '''Michae no Matsuri''' (道饗祭, also read as ちあえのまつり) is one of the Shinto [[rites]]. It is held twice a year, in June and December, at the four corners of the capital's roads to enshrine the deities '''Yachimata-hiko''' (八衢比古神), '''Yachimata-hime''' (八衢比売神), and '''Kunado''' (久那斗神). The purpose of this ceremony is to prevent and protect the capital and the [[Imperial Palace]] from malevolent spirits such as [[demons]] and [[yōkai]], and to pray for divine protection.<ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/212207/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Michae no Matsuri] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> Although the ritual was centered in [[Kyoto]] under the [[Ritsuryō system]], it was also performed in rural areas during outbreaks of [[epidemics]].<ref name="da1">[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 World Encyclopedia, 2nd Edition: Michae no Matsuri] Heibonsha, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> In Musashi Province, the name "Ae-ba" (饗庭) appears frequently as a place name and surname, which folklorist [[Kunio Yanagita]] hypothesized to have originated from Michae no Matsuri.<ref name="da1"/> He also speculated that place names like "Aino-ta" and "Manota" in northern Musashi derived from "fields of Ae-ba."<ref>Kunio Yanagita, *Research on Place Names*, Kadokawa Bunko, 9th edition, 1974, p. 216.</ref>
== Overview == Michae no Matsuri is a recurring festival prescribed under the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令}}, one of the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} system's codes.<ref name="da1"/> It is listed in the "June Festivals" section of the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, compiled during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, and categorized as a "minor ritual."<ref group="注">Rites conducted with one day of purification beforehand under the Ritsuryō system.</ref><ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/108492/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Minor Ritual] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> The ceremony was overseen by the {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職}} (Capital Officials), with the {{ill|Urabe clan|ja|卜部氏}} performing the purification.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 Britannica International Encyclopedia: Michae no Matsuri] Britannica Japanese Edition, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> It was often held in conjunction with the {{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火祭}} in Kyoto.<ref name="n19">Nishimuta 2003, p. 119</ref>
While traditionally thought to occur after the {{ill|Ōharae|ja|大祓}} (Great Purification) on the 30th day of the 6th and 12th lunar months, no specific festival date is recorded in the *Engishiki* or *Taika Reforms*, leading some to conclude that auspicious days were chosen instead, as suggested by the {{ill|Shūgaishō|ja|拾芥抄}}.<ref name="tu1">Tsugita 2008, p. 378</ref>
Shinto traditions often observe half-yearly rituals based on {{ill|seasonal agriculture|ja|農耕儀礼}}, the passage of {{ill|natural cycles|ja|自然観}}, and the transformations of plants and animals.<ref name="da1"/>
== Summary Table == {| class="wikitable" ! Item !! Description !! Remarks |- ! Deities | colspan="2"|Yachimata-hime (八衢比売神), Yachimata-hiko (八衢比古神), Kunado (久那斗神) |- ! Code | colspan="2"|Jingiryō, classified as a minor ritual |- | rowspan="3"|Legal References | * {{ill|Ōmi Code|ja|近江令}} (668 CE) |- | * {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}} (681 CE) |- | * {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}} (Chapter 6, Article 20, 718 CE) |- ! Dates | 6th and 12th lunar months || Specific dates vary |- ! Faith | rowspan="2"|Ancient Shinto || Includes natural worship |- | Rooted in ancestral worship |}
== Historical Interpretations == === *Kujigen* and *Nishinomiya-ki* === The {{ill|Kujigen|ja|公事根源}} by {{ill|Ichijō Kanera|ja|一条兼良}} misidentifies Michae no Matsuri as the "Festival of the Four Corners," combining it with the {{ill|Fire Calming Festival|ja|鎮火祭}}, which were separate.<ref name="ko1">Kōda 1976, p. 229</ref>
Meanwhile, the *Nishinomiya-ki* by {{ill|Minamoto no Takaaki|ja|源高明}} clarifies the distinctions: the "Four Corners Festival" was conducted during epidemic outbreaks at Kyoto's periphery, and the "Four Borders Festival" targeted the nation's outermost provinces.<ref name="tu2">Tsugita 2008, p. 380</ref>
Despite these clear distinctions, misunderstandings led later generations to conflate the rites.<ref name="tu1"/> Epidemic outbreaks further blurred the line between Michae no Matsuri and rites honoring pestilence gods.<ref name="tu3">Tsugita 2008, p. 381</ref>
== Liturgical Text == A key portion of the Michae no Matsuri prayer: {{Quotation|'''Original Text:''' 高天之原尓事始氐、皇御孫命止称辞竟奉、...|source=Engishiki}}
== Significance == Key phrases in the prayer include: * '''Takamahara''' (高天原) signifies the celestial origin of the ritual. * '''Ōyamato''' (大八衢) denotes sacred crossroads that safeguard the imperial capital.
[[Category:Shinto rituals]] [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:6th lunar month festivals]] [[Category:12th lunar month festivals]] [[ja:道饗祭]]
== Historical Context == The precise origins of the Festival of the Road Gods are unclear, but its formal characteristics suggest it is closely linked to the establishment of capital cities in Japan. It likely dates back to the founding of the Fujiwara Palace in Nara at the end of the seventh century. The festival's association with the introduction of capital cities suggests its purpose was to protect these new urban centers from malevolent supernatural influences.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Ritual Elements == The festival was performed by diviners (urabe) from the Department of Divinities (Jingikan), who utilized ritual implements made of fur. This indicates a strong connection to yin-yang divination practices (onmyōdō). The use of these implements and the involvement of the Department of Divinities highlight the festival's spiritual and protective purposes.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Decline and Disappearance == The Festival of the Road Gods gradually disappeared during the mid-Heian period, influenced by the rise of the Festival of the Four Directions (Shikaku sai) and other similar observances. As these other festivals gained prominence, references to the Michiae no matsuri became scarce in historical records.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Interpretations and Records == The Ryō no gige (Explanations of the Prescriptions) notes that the Michiae no matsuri aimed to prevent calamity-causing demons from entering the capital by intercepting and entertaining them in the streets. Conversely, the norito prayer recorded in the Engi Shiki (Engi Regulations) for this festival indicates that it celebrated three deities: Yachimata-hiko and other road deities. This suggests a dual interpretation of the festival's purpose: both as a means of protection from malevolent spirits and as a celebration of specific deities associated with the roads.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />

It is held twice a year, in June and December, at the four corners of the capital's roads to enshrine the deities '''Yachimata-hiko''' (八衢比古神), '''Yachimata-hime''' (八衢比売神), and '''Kunado''' (久那斗神). == Overview == Michae no Matsuri is a recurring festival prescribed under the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令}}, one of the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} system's codes.<ref name="da1"/> It is listed in the "June Festivals" section of the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, compiled during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, and categorized as a "minor ritual."<ref group="注">Rites conducted with one day of purification beforehand under the Ritsuryō system.</ref><ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/108492/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Minor Ritual] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> The ceremony was overseen by the {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職}} (Capital Officials), with the {{ill|Urabe clan|ja|卜部氏}} performing the purification.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 Britannica International Encyclopedia: Michae no Matsuri] Britannica Japanese Edition, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> It was often held in conjunction with the {{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火祭}} in Kyoto.<ref name="n19">Nishimuta 2003, p. 119</ref>
While traditionally thought to occur after the {{ill|Ōharae|ja|大祓}} (Great Purification) on the 30th day of the 6th and 12th lunar months, no specific festival date is recorded in the *Engishiki* or *Taika Reforms*, leading some to conclude that auspicious days were chosen instead, as suggested by the {{ill|Shūgaishō|ja|拾芥抄}}.<ref name="tu1">Tsugita 2008, p. 378</ref>
Shinto traditions often observe half-yearly rituals based on {{ill|seasonal agriculture|ja|農耕儀礼}}, the passage of {{ill|natural cycles|ja|自然観}}, and the transformations of plants and animals.<ref name="da1"/>

Code | colspan="2"|Jingiryō, classified as a minor ritual |- | rowspan="3"|Legal References | * {{ill|Ōmi Code|ja|近江令}} (668 CE) |- | * {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}} (681 CE) |- | * {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}} (Chapter 6, Article 20, 718 CE) |- ! Dates | 6th and 12th lunar months || Specific dates vary |- ! Faith | rowspan="2"|Ancient Shinto || Includes natural worship |- | Rooted in ancestral worship |}
== Historical Interpretations == === *Kujigen* and *Nishinomiya-ki* === The {{ill|Kujigen|ja|公事根源}} by {{ill|Ichijō Kanera|ja|一条兼良}} misidentifies Michae no Matsuri as the "Festival of the Four Corners," combining it with the {{ill|Fire Calming Festival|ja|鎮火祭}}, which were separate.<ref name="ko1">Kōda 1976, p. 229</ref>
Meanwhile, the *Nishinomiya-ki* by {{ill|Minamoto no Takaaki|ja|源高明}} clarifies the distinctions: the "Four Corners Festival" was conducted during epidemic outbreaks at Kyoto's periphery, and the "Four Borders Festival" targeted the nation's outermost provinces.<ref name="tu2">Tsugita 2008, p. 380</ref>
Despite these clear distinctions, misunderstandings led later generations to conflate the rites.<ref name="tu1"/> Epidemic outbreaks further blurred the line between Michae no Matsuri and rites honoring pestilence gods.<ref name="tu3">Tsugita 2008, p. 381</ref>
== Liturgical Text == A key portion of the Michae no Matsuri prayer: {{Quotation|'''Original Text:''' 高天之原尓事始氐、皇御孫命止称辞竟奉、...|source=Engishiki}}
== Significance == Key phrases in the prayer include: * '''Takamahara''' (高天原) signifies the celestial origin of the ritual. * '''Ōyamato''' (大八衢) denotes sacred crossroads that safeguard the imperial capital.
[[Category:Shinto rituals]] [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:6th lunar month festivals]] [[Category:12th lunar month festivals]] [[ja:道饗祭]]
== Historical Context == The precise origins of the Festival of the Road Gods are unclear, but its formal characteristics suggest it is closely linked to the establishment of capital cities in Japan. It likely dates back to the founding of the Fujiwara Palace in Nara at the end of the seventh century. The festival's association with the introduction of capital cities suggests its purpose was to protect these new urban centers from malevolent supernatural influences.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Ritual Elements == The festival was performed by diviners (urabe) from the Department of Divinities (Jingikan), who utilized ritual implements made of fur. This indicates a strong connection to yin-yang divination practices (onmyōdō). The use of these implements and the involvement of the Department of Divinities highlight the festival's spiritual and protective purposes.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Decline and Disappearance == The Festival of the Road Gods gradually disappeared during the mid-Heian period, influenced by the rise of the Festival of the Four Directions (Shikaku sai) and other similar observances. As these other festivals gained prominence, references to the Michiae no matsuri became scarce in historical records.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Interpretations and Records == The Ryō no gige (Explanations of the Prescriptions) notes that the Michiae no matsuri aimed to prevent calamity-causing demons from entering the capital by intercepting and entertaining them in the streets. Conversely, the norito prayer recorded in the Engi Shiki (Engi Regulations) for this festival indicates that it celebrated three deities: Yachimata-hiko and other road deities. This suggests a dual interpretation of the festival's purpose: both as a means of protection from malevolent spirits and as a celebration of specific deities associated with the roads.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Annotations === {{Reflist|group="注"}} <references group="lower-alpha"/>

{{User:Immanuelle/Japanese Imperial Rituals updated}}
The Festival of the Road Gods (道祖神祭り, Michiae no matsuri) was an ancient ceremony prescribed by the Divinities Prescriptions (Jingiryō) intended to prevent evil spirits, such as demons and epidemic gods (ekishin), from entering the capital. This festival was conducted on the main roads (ōji) surrounding Kyoto in each of the four cardinal directions.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-09-29|title=Michiae no matsuri {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230929230332/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9286|access-date=2024-05-18|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
==Overview==
{{令制祭祀}} '''Michae no Matsuri''' (道饗祭, also read as ちあえのまつり) is one of the Shinto [[rites]]. It is held twice a year, in June and December, at the four corners of the capital's roads to enshrine the deities '''Yachimata-hiko''' (八衢比古神), '''Yachimata-hime''' (八衢比売神), and '''Kunado''' (久那斗神). The purpose of this ceremony is to prevent and protect the capital and the [[Imperial Palace]] from malevolent spirits such as [[demons]] and [[yōkai]], and to pray for divine protection.<ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/212207/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Michae no Matsuri] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> Although the ritual was centered in [[Kyoto]] under the [[Ritsuryō system]], it was also performed in rural areas during outbreaks of [[epidemics]].<ref name="da1">[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 World Encyclopedia, 2nd Edition: Michae no Matsuri] Heibonsha, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> In Musashi Province, the name "Ae-ba" (饗庭) appears frequently as a place name and surname, which folklorist [[Kunio Yanagita]] hypothesized to have originated from Michae no Matsuri.<ref name="da1"/> He also speculated that place names like "Aino-ta" and "Manota" in northern Musashi derived from "fields of Ae-ba."<ref>Kunio Yanagita, *Research on Place Names*, Kadokawa Bunko, 9th edition, 1974, p. 216.</ref>

The festival's association with the introduction of capital cities suggests its purpose was to protect these new urban centers from malevolent supernatural influences.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Ritual Elements == The festival was performed by diviners (urabe) from the Department of Divinities (Jingikan), who utilized ritual implements made of fur. This indicates a strong connection to yin-yang divination practices (onmyōdō). The use of these implements and the involvement of the Department of Divinities highlight the festival's spiritual and protective purposes.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Decline and Disappearance == The Festival of the Road Gods gradually disappeared during the mid-Heian period, influenced by the rise of the Festival of the Four Directions (Shikaku sai) and other similar observances. As these other festivals gained prominence, references to the Michiae no matsuri became scarce in historical records.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Interpretations and Records == The Ryō no gige (Explanations of the Prescriptions) notes that the Michiae no matsuri aimed to prevent calamity-causing demons from entering the capital by intercepting and entertaining them in the streets. Conversely, the norito prayer recorded in the Engi Shiki (Engi Regulations) for this festival indicates that it celebrated three deities: Yachimata-hiko and other road deities. This suggests a dual interpretation of the festival's purpose: both as a means of protection from malevolent spirits and as a celebration of specific deities associated with the roads.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />

[[Category:kkk]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Michiae no matsuri]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[sn:Michiae no matsuri]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Michiae no matsuri]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Michiae no matsuri]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Michiae no matsuri]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Michiae no matsuri]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Michiae no matsuri]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Michiae no matsuri]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Michiae no matsuri]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Michiae no matsuri]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Michiae no matsuri]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Michiae no matsuri]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Michiae no matsuri]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Michiae no matsuri]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Michiae no matsuri]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Michiae no matsuri]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Michiae no matsuri]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Michiae no matsuri]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Michiae no matsuri]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Michiae no matsuri]][[Category:qq]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Michiae no matsuri]][[en:Michiae no matsuri]][[en:Michiae no matsuri]][[simple:user:Immanuelle/{{PAGENAME}}]] }} [[ja:道饗祭]]

{{User:Immanuelle/Japanese Imperial Rituals updated}}
The Festival of the Road Gods (道祖神祭り, Michiae no matsuri) was an ancient ceremony prescribed by the Divinities Prescriptions (Jingiryō) intended to prevent evil spirits, such as demons and epidemic gods (ekishin), from entering the capital. This festival was conducted on the main roads (ōji) surrounding Kyoto in each of the four cardinal directions.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-09-29|title=Michiae no matsuri {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230929230332/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9286|access-date=2024-05-18|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
==Overview==
{{令制祭祀}} '''Michae no Matsuri''' (道饗祭, also read as ちあえのまつり) is one of the Shinto [[rites]]. It is held twice a year, in June and December, at the four corners of the capital's roads to enshrine the deities '''Yachimata-hiko''' (八衢比古神), '''Yachimata-hime''' (八衢比売神), and '''Kunado''' (久那斗神). The purpose of this ceremony is to prevent and protect the capital and the [[Imperial Palace]] from malevolent spirits such as [[demons]] and [[yōkai]], and to pray for divine protection.<ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/212207/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Michae no Matsuri] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> Although the ritual was centered in [[Kyoto]] under the [[Ritsuryō system]], it was also performed in rural areas during outbreaks of [[epidemics]].<ref name="da1">[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 World Encyclopedia, 2nd Edition: Michae no Matsuri] Heibonsha, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> In Musashi Province, the name "Ae-ba" (饗庭) appears frequently as a place name and surname, which folklorist [[Kunio Yanagita]] hypothesized to have originated from Michae no Matsuri.<ref name="da1"/> He also speculated that place names like "Aino-ta" and "Manota" in northern Musashi derived from "fields of Ae-ba."<ref>Kunio Yanagita, *Research on Place Names*, Kadokawa Bunko, 9th edition, 1974, p. 216.</ref> [[Category:Lunar June]] [[Category:Lunar December]]
== Overview == Michae no Matsuri is a recurring festival prescribed under the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令}}, one of the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} system's codes.<ref name="da1"/> It is listed in the "June Festivals" section of the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, compiled during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, and categorized as a "minor ritual."<ref group="注">Rites conducted with one day of purification beforehand under the Ritsuryō system.</ref><ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/108492/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Minor Ritual] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> The ceremony was overseen by the {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職}} (Capital Officials), with the {{ill|Urabe clan|ja|卜部氏}} performing the purification.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 Britannica International Encyclopedia: Michae no Matsuri] Britannica Japanese Edition, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> It was often held in conjunction with the {{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火祭}} in Kyoto.<ref name="n19">Nishimuta 2003, p. 119</ref>
While traditionally thought to occur after the {{ill|Ōharae|ja|大祓}} (Great Purification) on the 30th day of the 6th and 12th lunar months, no specific festival date is recorded in the *Engishiki* or *Taika Reforms*, leading some to conclude that auspicious days were chosen instead, as suggested by the {{ill|Shūgaishō|ja|拾芥抄}}.<ref name="tu1">Tsugita 2008, p. 378</ref>
Shinto traditions often observe half-yearly rituals based on {{ill|seasonal agriculture|ja|農耕儀礼}}, the passage of {{ill|natural cycles|ja|自然観}}, and the transformations of plants and animals.<ref name="da1"/>
== Summary Table == {| class="wikitable" ! Item !! Description !! Remarks |- ! Deities | colspan="2"|Yachimata-hime (八衢比売神), Yachimata-hiko (八衢比古神), Kunado (久那斗神) |- ! Code | colspan="2"|Jingiryō, classified as a minor ritual |- | rowspan="3"|Legal References | * {{ill|Ōmi Code|ja|近江令}} (668 CE) |- | * {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}} (681 CE) |- | * {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}} (Chapter 6, Article 20, 718 CE) |- ! Dates | 6th and 12th lunar months || Specific dates vary |- ! Faith | rowspan="2"|Ancient Shinto || Includes natural worship |- | Rooted in ancestral worship |}
== Historical Interpretations == === *Kujigen* and *Nishinomiya-ki* === The {{ill|Kujigen|ja|公事根源}} by {{ill|Ichijō Kanera|ja|一条兼良}} misidentifies Michae no Matsuri as the "Festival of the Four Corners," combining it with the {{ill|Fire Calming Festival|ja|鎮火祭}}, which were separate.<ref name="ko1">Kōda 1976, p. 229</ref>
Meanwhile, the *Nishinomiya-ki* by {{ill|Minamoto no Takaaki|ja|源高明}} clarifies the distinctions: the "Four Corners Festival" was conducted during epidemic outbreaks at Kyoto's periphery, and the "Four Borders Festival" targeted the nation's outermost provinces.<ref name="tu2">Tsugita 2008, p. 380</ref>
Despite these clear distinctions, misunderstandings led later generations to conflate the rites.<ref name="tu1"/> Epidemic outbreaks further blurred the line between Michae no Matsuri and rites honoring pestilence gods.<ref name="tu3">Tsugita 2008, p. 381</ref>
== Liturgical Text == A key portion of the Michae no Matsuri prayer: {{Quotation|'''Original Text:''' 高天之原尓事始氐、皇御孫命止称辞竟奉、...|source=Engishiki}}
== Significance == Key phrases in the prayer include: * '''Takamahara''' (高天原) signifies the celestial origin of the ritual. * '''Ōyamato''' (大八衢) denotes sacred crossroads that safeguard the imperial capital.
[[Category:Shinto rituals]] [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:6th lunar month festivals]] [[Category:12th lunar month festivals]] [[ja:道饗祭]]
== Historical Context == The precise origins of the Festival of the Road Gods are unclear, but its formal characteristics suggest it is closely linked to the establishment of capital cities in Japan. It likely dates back to the founding of the Fujiwara Palace in Nara at the end of the seventh century. The festival's association with the introduction of capital cities suggests its purpose was to protect these new urban centers from malevolent supernatural influences.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Ritual Elements == The festival was performed by diviners (urabe) from the Department of Divinities (Jingikan), who utilized ritual implements made of fur. This indicates a strong connection to yin-yang divination practices (onmyōdō). The use of these implements and the involvement of the Department of Divinities highlight the festival's spiritual and protective purposes.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Decline and Disappearance == The Festival of the Road Gods gradually disappeared during the mid-Heian period, influenced by the rise of the Festival of the Four Directions (Shikaku sai) and other similar observances. As these other festivals gained prominence, references to the Michiae no matsuri became scarce in historical records.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Interpretations and Records == The Ryō no gige (Explanations of the Prescriptions) notes that the Michiae no matsuri aimed to prevent calamity-causing demons from entering the capital by intercepting and entertaining them in the streets. Conversely, the norito prayer recorded in the Engi Shiki (Engi Regulations) for this festival indicates that it celebrated three deities: Yachimata-hiko and other road deities. This suggests a dual interpretation of the festival's purpose: both as a means of protection from malevolent spirits and as a celebration of specific deities associated with the roads.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />

{{令制祭祀}} '''Michae no Matsuri''' (道饗祭, also read as ちあえのまつり) is one of the Shinto [[rites]]. It is held twice a year, in June and December, at the four corners of the capital's roads to enshrine the deities '''Yachimata-hiko''' (八衢比古神), '''Yachimata-hime''' (八衢比売神), and '''Kunado''' (久那斗神). The purpose of this ceremony is to prevent and protect the capital and the [[Imperial Palace]] from malevolent spirits such as [[demons]] and [[yōkai]], and to pray for divine protection.<ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/212207/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Michae no Matsuri] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> Although the ritual was centered in [[Kyoto]] under the [[Ritsuryō system]], it was also performed in rural areas during outbreaks of [[epidemics]].<ref name="da1">[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 World Encyclopedia, 2nd Edition: Michae no Matsuri] Heibonsha, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> In Musashi Province, the name "Ae-ba" (饗庭) appears frequently as a place name and surname, which folklorist [[Kunio Yanagita]] hypothesized to have originated from Michae no Matsuri.<ref name="da1"/> He also speculated that place names like "Aino-ta" and "Manota" in northern Musashi derived from "fields of Ae-ba."<ref>Kunio Yanagita, *Research on Place Names*, Kadokawa Bunko, 9th edition, 1974, p. 216.</ref> [[Category:Lunar June]] [[Category:Lunar December]]
== Overview == Michae no Matsuri is a recurring festival prescribed under the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令}}, one of the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} system's codes.<ref name="da1"/> It is listed in the "June Festivals" section of the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, compiled during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, and categorized as a "minor ritual."<ref group="注">Rites conducted with one day of purification beforehand under the Ritsuryō system.</ref><ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/108492/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Minor Ritual] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> The ceremony was overseen by the {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職}} (Capital Officials), with the {{ill|Urabe clan|ja|卜部氏}} performing the purification.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 Britannica International Encyclopedia: Michae no Matsuri] Britannica Japanese Edition, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> It was often held in conjunction with the {{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火祭}} in Kyoto.<ref name="n19">Nishimuta 2003, p. 119</ref>
While traditionally thought to occur after the {{ill|Ōharae|ja|大祓}} (Great Purification) on the 30th day of the 6th and 12th lunar months, no specific festival date is recorded in the *Engishiki* or *Taika Reforms*, leading some to conclude that auspicious days were chosen instead, as suggested by the {{ill|Shūgaishō|ja|拾芥抄}}.<ref name="tu1">Tsugita 2008, p. 378</ref>
Shinto traditions often observe half-yearly rituals based on {{ill|seasonal agriculture|ja|農耕儀礼}}, the passage of {{ill|natural cycles|ja|自然観}}, and the transformations of plants and animals.<ref name="da1"/>
== Summary Table == {| class="wikitable" ! Item !! Description !! Remarks |- ! Deities | colspan="2"|Yachimata-hime (八衢比売神), Yachimata-hiko (八衢比古神), Kunado (久那斗神) |- ! Code | colspan="2"|Jingiryō, classified as a minor ritual |- | rowspan="3"|Legal References | * {{ill|Ōmi Code|ja|近江令}} (668 CE) |- | * {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}} (681 CE) |- | * {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}} (Chapter 6, Article 20, 718 CE) |- ! Dates | 6th and 12th lunar months || Specific dates vary |- ! Faith | rowspan="2"|Ancient Shinto || Includes natural worship |- | Rooted in ancestral worship |}
== Historical Interpretations == === *Kujigen* and *Nishinomiya-ki* === The {{ill|Kujigen|ja|公事根源}} by {{ill|Ichijō Kanera|ja|一条兼良}} misidentifies Michae no Matsuri as the "Festival of the Four Corners," combining it with the {{ill|Fire Calming Festival|ja|鎮火祭}}, which were separate.<ref name="ko1">Kōda 1976, p. 229</ref>
Meanwhile, the *Nishinomiya-ki* by {{ill|Minamoto no Takaaki|ja|源高明}} clarifies the distinctions: the "Four Corners Festival" was conducted during epidemic outbreaks at Kyoto's periphery, and the "Four Borders Festival" targeted the nation's outermost provinces.<ref name="tu2">Tsugita 2008, p. 380</ref>
Despite these clear distinctions, misunderstandings led later generations to conflate the rites.<ref name="tu1"/> Epidemic outbreaks further blurred the line between Michae no Matsuri and rites honoring pestilence gods.<ref name="tu3">Tsugita 2008, p. 381</ref>
== Liturgical Text == A key portion of the Michae no Matsuri prayer: {{Quotation|'''Original Text:''' 高天之原尓事始氐、皇御孫命止称辞竟奉、...|source=Engishiki}}
== Significance == Key phrases in the prayer include: * '''Takamahara''' (高天原) signifies the celestial origin of the ritual. * '''Ōyamato''' (大八衢) denotes sacred crossroads that safeguard the imperial capital.
[[Category:Shinto rituals]] [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:6th lunar month festivals]] [[Category:12th lunar month festivals]] [[ja:道饗祭]]
== Historical Context == The precise origins of the Festival of the Road Gods are unclear, but its formal characteristics suggest it is closely linked to the establishment of capital cities in Japan. It likely dates back to the founding of the Fujiwara Palace in Nara at the end of the seventh century. The festival's association with the introduction of capital cities suggests its purpose was to protect these new urban centers from malevolent supernatural influences.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Ritual Elements == The festival was performed by diviners (urabe) from the Department of Divinities (Jingikan), who utilized ritual implements made of fur. This indicates a strong connection to yin-yang divination practices (onmyōdō). The use of these implements and the involvement of the Department of Divinities highlight the festival's spiritual and protective purposes.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Decline and Disappearance == The Festival of the Road Gods gradually disappeared during the mid-Heian period, influenced by the rise of the Festival of the Four Directions (Shikaku sai) and other similar observances. As these other festivals gained prominence, references to the Michiae no matsuri became scarce in historical records.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Interpretations and Records == The Ryō no gige (Explanations of the Prescriptions) notes that the Michiae no matsuri aimed to prevent calamity-causing demons from entering the capital by intercepting and entertaining them in the streets. Conversely, the norito prayer recorded in the Engi Shiki (Engi Regulations) for this festival indicates that it celebrated three deities: Yachimata-hiko and other road deities. This suggests a dual interpretation of the festival's purpose: both as a means of protection from malevolent spirits and as a celebration of specific deities associated with the roads.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Annotations === {{Reflist|group="注"}} <references group="lower-alpha"/>

{{User:Immanuelle/Japanese Imperial Rituals updated}}
The Festival of the Road Gods (道祖神祭り, Michiae no matsuri) was an ancient ceremony prescribed by the Divinities Prescriptions (Jingiryō) intended to prevent evil spirits, such as demons and epidemic gods (ekishin), from entering the capital. This festival was conducted on the main roads (ōji) surrounding Kyoto in each of the four cardinal directions.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-09-29|title=Michiae no matsuri {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230929230332/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9286|access-date=2024-05-18|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>

== Historical Context == The precise origins of the Festival of the Road Gods are unclear, but its formal characteristics suggest it is closely linked to the establishment of capital cities in Japan. It likely dates back to the founding of the Fujiwara Palace in Nara at the end of the seventh century. The festival's association with the introduction of capital cities suggests its purpose was to protect these new urban centers from malevolent supernatural influences.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Ritual Elements == The festival was performed by diviners (urabe) from the Department of Divinities (Jingikan), who utilized ritual implements made of fur. This indicates a strong connection to yin-yang divination practices (onmyōdō). The use of these implements and the involvement of the Department of Divinities highlight the festival's spiritual and protective purposes.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Decline and Disappearance == The Festival of the Road Gods gradually disappeared during the mid-Heian period, influenced by the rise of the Festival of the Four Directions (Shikaku sai) and other similar observances. As these other festivals gained prominence, references to the Michiae no matsuri became scarce in historical records.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Interpretations and Records == The Ryō no gige (Explanations of the Prescriptions) notes that the Michiae no matsuri aimed to prevent calamity-causing demons from entering the capital by intercepting and entertaining them in the streets. Conversely, the norito prayer recorded in the Engi Shiki (Engi Regulations) for this festival indicates that it celebrated three deities: Yachimata-hiko and other road deities. This suggests a dual interpretation of the festival's purpose: both as a means of protection from malevolent spirits and as a celebration of specific deities associated with the roads.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Annotations === {{Reflist|group="注"}} <references group="lower-alpha"/>
=== References === {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * Nishimuta, Takaoki. *Heisei New Edition Norito Dictionary.* 2003, ISBN 4-900901-32-6. * Kōda, Toshio. *Annotated Annual Events.* 1976, ISBN 4-7971-0525-9. * Tsugita, Jun. *Revised Norito Interpretations.* 2008, ISBN 978-4-900901-85-8.
