revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{User:Immanuelle/Japanese Imperial Rituals updated}}
The Festival of the Road Gods (道祖神祭り, Michiae no matsuri) was an ancient ceremony prescribed by the Divinities Prescriptions (Jingiryō) intended to prevent evil spirits, such as demons and epidemic gods (ekishin), from entering the capital. This festival was conducted on the main roads (ōji) surrounding Kyoto in each of the four cardinal directions.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-09-29|title=Michiae no matsuri {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230929230332/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9286|access-date=2024-05-18|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
==Overview==
{{令制祭祀}} '''Michae no Matsuri''' (道饗祭, also read as ちあえのまつり) is one of the Shinto [[rites]]. It is held twice a year, in June and December, at the four corners of the capital's roads to enshrine the deities '''Yachimata-hiko''' (八衢比古神), '''Yachimata-hime''' (八衢比売神), and '''Kunado''' (久那斗神). The purpose of this ceremony is to prevent and protect the capital and the [[Imperial Palace]] from malevolent spirits such as [[demons]] and [[yōkai]], and to pray for divine protection.<ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/212207/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Michae no Matsuri] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> Although the ritual was centered in [[Kyoto]] under the [[Ritsuryō system]], it was also performed in rural areas during outbreaks of [[epidemics]].<ref name="da1">[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 World Encyclopedia, 2nd Edition: Michae no Matsuri] Heibonsha, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> In Musashi Province, the name "Ae-ba" (饗庭) appears frequently as a place name and surname, which folklorist [[Kunio Yanagita]] hypothesized to have originated from Michae no Matsuri.<ref name="da1"/> He also speculated that place names like "Aino-ta" and "Manota" in northern Musashi derived from "fields of Ae-ba."<ref>Kunio Yanagita, *Research on Place Names*, Kadokawa Bunko, 9th edition, 1974, p. 216.</ref> == Overview == Michae no Matsuri is a recurring festival prescribed under the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令|12=draft}}, one of the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令|12=draft}} system's codes.<ref name="da1"/> It is listed in the "June Festivals" section of the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|12=draft}}, compiled during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}}, and categorized as a "minor ritual."<ref group="注">Rites conducted with one day of purification beforehand under the Ritsuryō system.</ref><ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/108492/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Minor Ritual] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> The ceremony was overseen by the {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職|12=draft}} (Capital Officials), with the {{ill|Urabe clan|ja|卜部氏|12=draft}} performing the purification.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 Britannica International Encyclopedia: Michae no Matsuri] Britannica Japanese Edition, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> It was often held in conjunction with the {{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火祭|12=draft}} in Kyoto.<ref name="n19">Nishimuta 2003, p. 119</ref>
While traditionally thought to occur after the {{ill|Ōharae|ja|大祓|12=draft}} (Great Purification) on the 30th day of the 6th and 12th lunar months, no specific festival date is recorded in the *Engishiki* or *Taika Reforms*, leading some to conclude that auspicious days were chosen instead, as suggested by the {{ill|Shūgaishō|ja|拾芥抄|12=draft}}.<ref name="tu1">Tsugita 2008, p. 378</ref>
Shinto traditions often observe half-yearly rituals based on {{ill|seasonal agriculture|ja|農耕儀礼|12=draft}}, the passage of {{ill|natural cycles|ja|自然観|12=draft}}, and the transformations of plants and animals.<ref name="da1"/>
== Summary Table == {| class="wikitable" ! Item !! Description !! Remarks |- ! Deities | colspan="2"|Yachimata-hime (八衢比売神), Yachimata-hiko (八衢比古神), Kunado (久那斗神) |- ! Code | colspan="2"|Jingiryō, classified as a minor ritual |- | rowspan="3"|Legal References | * {{ill|Ōmi Code|ja|近江令|12=draft}} (668 CE) |- | * {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令|12=draft}} (681 CE) |- | * {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=draft}} (Chapter 6, Article 20, 718 CE) |- ! Dates | 6th and 12th lunar months || Specific dates vary |- ! Faith | rowspan="2"|Ancient Shinto || Includes natural worship |- | Rooted in ancestral worship |}
== Historical Interpretations == === *Kujigen* and *Nishinomiya-ki* === The {{ill|Kujigen|ja|公事根源|12=draft}} by {{ill|Ichijō Kanera|ja|一条兼良|12=draft}} misidentifies Michae no Matsuri as the "Festival of the Four Corners," combining it with the {{ill|Fire Calming Festival|ja|鎮火祭|12=draft}}, which were separate.<ref name="ko1">Kōda 1976, p. 229</ref>
Meanwhile, the *Nishinomiya-ki* by {{ill|Minamoto no Takaaki|ja|源高明|12=draft}} clarifies the distinctions: the "Four Corners Festival" was conducted during epidemic outbreaks at Kyoto's periphery, and the "Four Borders Festival" targeted the nation's outermost provinces.<ref name="tu2">Tsugita 2008, p. 380</ref>
Despite these clear distinctions, misunderstandings led later generations to conflate the rites.<ref name="tu1"/> Epidemic outbreaks further blurred the line between Michae no Matsuri and rites honoring pestilence gods.<ref name="tu3">Tsugita 2008, p. 381</ref>
== Liturgical Text == A key portion of the Michae no Matsuri prayer: {{Quotation|'''Original Text:''' 高天之原尓事始氐、皇御孫命止称辞竟奉、...|source=Engishiki}}
== Significance == Key phrases in the prayer include: * '''Takamahara''' (高天原) signifies the celestial origin of the ritual. * '''Ōyamato''' (大八衢) denotes sacred crossroads that safeguard the imperial capital.
== Historical Context == The precise origins of the Festival of the Road Gods are unclear, but its formal characteristics suggest it is closely linked to the establishment of capital cities in Japan. It likely dates back to the founding of the Fujiwara Palace in Nara at the end of the seventh century. The festival's association with the introduction of capital cities suggests its purpose was to protect these new urban centers from malevolent supernatural influences.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Ritual Elements == The festival was performed by diviners (urabe) from the Department of Divinities (Jingikan), who utilized ritual implements made of fur. This indicates a strong connection to yin-yang divination practices (onmyōdō). The use of these implements and the involvement of the Department of Divinities highlight the festival's spiritual and protective purposes.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Decline and Disappearance == The Festival of the Road Gods gradually disappeared during the mid-Heian period, influenced by the rise of the Festival of the Four Directions (Shikaku sai) and other similar observances. As these other festivals gained prominence, references to the Michiae no matsuri became scarce in historical records.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Interpretations and Records == The Ryō no gige (Explanations of the Prescriptions) notes that the Michiae no matsuri aimed to prevent calamity-causing demons from entering the capital by intercepting and entertaining them in the streets. Conversely, the norito prayer recorded in the Engi Shiki (Engi Regulations) for this festival indicates that it celebrated three deities: Yachimata-hiko and other road deities. This suggests a dual interpretation of the festival's purpose: both as a means of protection from malevolent spirits and as a celebration of specific deities associated with the roads.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Notes ==
=== Annotations === {{Reflist|group="注"}} <references group="lower-alpha"/>
=== References === {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * Nishimuta, Takaoki. *Heisei New Edition Norito Dictionary.* 2003, ISBN 4-900901-32-6. * Kōda, Toshio. *Annotated Annual Events.* 1976, ISBN 4-7971-0525-9. * Tsugita, Jun. *Revised Norito Interpretations.* 2008, ISBN 978-4-900901-85-8.

revision.text:
{{令制祭祀}} '''道饗祭'''（'''みちあえのまつり'''、ちあえのまつり）とは、神道[[祭祀]]の1つ。[[古代|上代]]から毎年6月と12月の2回、都の四隅道上で、'''八衢比古神'''（やちまたひこのかみ）、'''八衢比売神'''（やちまたひめのかみ）、'''久那斗神'''（くなどのかみ)の3柱を祀り、都や[[朝庭|宮城]]の中に災いをもたらす[[魑魅|鬼魅]]や[[妖怪]]が入らぬよう防ぎ、守護を祈願する[[神事]]、および神社の[[祭]][http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/212207/m0u/ デジタル大辞泉「みちあえのまつり〔道饗の祭〕」] 小学館 2015年07月12日閲覧。令制祭祀としては[[京都]]が中心だったが、[[疫病|疾疫]]が起こった時は[[地方]]でも斎行した[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 世界大百科事典 第2版「みちあえのまつり〔道饗祭〕」] 平凡社 2015年07月12日閲覧。例えば、武蔵国では、饗庭（あえば）という地名や氏名が多いが、[[柳田國男]]は『地名の研究』（角川文庫 九版1974年 p.216）において、道饗祭＝邪神祭却に由来するものであり、北武蔵の「アイノ田」「間の田」に関しても、「饗場の田」に由来すると推測している（同書 p.216）。 == 概要 == [[令]]の編目の一、神祇令に定められた恒例の祭典である。[[平安時代]]成立の法令集『[[延喜式]]・第一巻』（[[藤原時平]]/[[藤原忠平]]ら編集）6月祭条に記され、小祀に区分する律令制で、前1日間を[[物忌み]]して行う祭祀。[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/108492/m0u/%E5%B0%8F%E7%A5%80/ デジタル大辞泉「しょうし〔小祀〕」] 小学館 2015年07月12日閲覧。[[京職|左右京職]]が司り、[[卜部氏|卜部（うらべ）氏]]がはらえを務めた[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典「みちあえのまつり〔道饗祭〕」] 日本語版 2015年07月12日閲覧。律令時代[[京都]]では鎮火祭（ほしずめのまつり）と兼ねて行うことが多かった西牟田 2003年、p119。祭日は[[陰暦]]の[[6月]]と[[12月]]、その月の晦日にある[[大祓]]の後に開催したと言われるが、『延喜式』や『[[大宝律令|大宝令]]』に祭日の明記がないことから、吉日を選び執り行ったとする『[[拾芥抄]]』の説をとる見解もある次田 2008年、p378。 神道では[[春分]]と[[秋分]]・[[夏至]]と[[冬至]]・上半期と下半期など、半年周期を節目としながら斎行する神事例が多く見当たり、これらは[[農耕]]儀礼との結びつきや、[[季節]]・動植物の移り変わりを目安とする自然暦・[[自然観]]に基づき、[[半年]]毎を基本としていた所以である。 === 基本情報表 === {| class="wikitable" !項目!!説明!!備考 |- !rowspan="3"|祭神 |colspan="2"|八衢比売神・八衢姫神（やちまたひめのかみ） |- |colspan="2"|八衢比古神・八衢彦神（やちまたひこのかみ） |- |colspan="2"|久那斗神（くなどのかみ） |- !rowspan="3"|律令 |rowspan="3"|神祇令・小祀||[[近江令]]（[[668年]]制定） |- |[[飛鳥浄御原令]]（[[681年]]編纂） |- |[[養老令]]（第6篇・20条、[[718年]]から） |- !開催日 |陰暦の6月・12月||晦日、又は吉日 |- !rowspan="2"|信仰 |rowspan="2"|神祇信仰（[[古神道]]）||自然信仰 |- |原始祖霊信仰 |- |} == 文献 == === 公事根源と西宮記 === [[疫病神]]の災厄をはらう祭典の内に、[[陰陽寮]]の四角四境祭がある次田 2008年、p380。[[室町時代|室町中期]]成立、朝廷における年間行事や儀式の由来・沿革を述べた『[[公事根源]]』（[[一条兼良]]・著書）では、道饗祭を「鎮火道饗の祭を四角四境の祭とも申也（もうすなり）」と記しているが誤りで、鎮火祭も道饗祭も四角祭や四境祭とは別の祭である甲田 1976年、p229。 平安中期成立、[[朝廷 (日本)|朝廷]]儀式の作法や制度などを述べた『[[西宮記]]』（[[源高明]]・著書）によると、疫病の兆しがあった歳に臨時に都の四隅で行う祭を「四角祭」、諸国の四境で行う祭を「四境祭（四界祭）」と呼んだものと記している。恒例的に神々を祀る祭を指してはいなかったが、後世の誤認により道饗祭や鎮火祭は一緒くたに纏められ解釈されていた。特に疫病が流行ると地方でも臨時に道饗祭を行ない、疫病神を祀る祭祀と誤解や混同が生じやすい環境にあった。 {{Cquote|四界祭 - 陰陽寮向ニ四界一祭、以ニ蔵人所人一為レ使。
四角祭 - 陰陽寮向ニ宮城四角一祭、有レ使所人。 已上天下有レ疫之時、陰陽寮進ニ示度。科物官宣。|||『西宮記』|次田 2008年、p380}} なお公事の他、[[民間信仰]]で疫病神の祭の種類は極めて多種多様に富み、広範囲で開催した次田 2008年、p381。 == 祝詞 == {{Quotation|'''原文''' - 高天之原尓事始氐、皇御孫之命止称辞竟奉、大八衢尓湯津磐村之如久塞坐皇神等之前尓申久、八衢比古・八衢比売・久那斗止御名者申氐、辞竟奉久波、根国底国与里麁備疎備来物尓、相率相口会事無氐、下行者下乎守理、上往者上乎守理、夜之守・日之守尓守奉斎奉礼止、 進幣帛者明妙・照妙・和妙・荒妙尓備奉、御酒者瓺辺高知、瓺腹満双氐、汁尓母穎尓母、山野尓住物者、毛能和物・毛能荒物、青海原尓住物者、鰭乃広物・鰭乃狭物、奥津海菜・辺津海菜尓至万尓氐、横山之如久置所足氐、進宇豆乃幣帛乎平気久聞食氐 八衢尓湯津磐村之如久塞坐氐皇御孫命乎堅磐尓常磐尓齋奉、茂御世尓幸閉奉給止申、又親王等・王等・臣等・百官人等、天下公民尓至万氐尓 平久斎給部止、神官天津祝詞乃太祝詞事乎以氐、称辞竟奉止申|『延喜式』|道饗祭祝詞}} === 意義・備考 === *'''高天之原'''（たかまのはら）'''に事始'''（ことはじ）'''めて'''/高天之原尓事始氐 - 意味：高天原に坐す御祖神等が事始め給いて。 :道饗祭は[[高天原]]に起源を発する神事であり、斎行している道饗祭は高天原で事始まった[[皇祖神|皇祖天神]]の[[神勅]]によって行っている、といった内容である。同様の記述が『[[古史伝]]』（平田篤胤・著書）にもあり、踏襲の解釈。 *'''皇御孫の命と'''/皇御孫之命止 - 意味：御代知らし食す今上天皇の詔（みことのり）・仰言によって。 :本来は「皇御孫命の命」が正しいが、命の漢字が重複するため一つ省いてある次田 2008年、p383。皇御孫は各時代国を治める[[今上天皇]]を指し、命は御言の意味であり[[詔]]や仰言である。 *'''称辞'''（たたへごと）'''竟'''（を）'''へ奉る'''/称辞竟奉 - 意味：祭祀を行い奉る。 :祝詞に多く用いる一種の[[慣用句]]次田 2008年、p69。「たたへごと」は「たたへことば」のことで賛詞を意味し、「たたへ」は水を湛えるの「たたえ」と同じく満ち足りを表現している、次の「をへ」は極め尽くすの意味である。従って本来の原義は「称辞を竟へ奉る（意味：善言美辞を尽くし、神の威徳を褒め称え奉る）」になるものの、慣用句と頻繁に用いられた結果、単に「祭る」や「幣帛などを奉献する」「神の御名を唱え奉る」の意味にも転じるようになった。 *'''大八衢'''（おおやちまた） - 意味：宮城外にある大道路の衢（ちまた）。 :「八」は数字の8ではなく、数が多いことを表す[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/222073/m0u/ デジタル大辞泉「や‐ちまた〔八衢〕」] 小学館 2015年7月18日閲覧。「衢」は道俣（ちまた）を意味し、辻道のように四方（又はそれ以上）に道が分岐した場所のことである。 *'''湯津磐村'''（ゆついはむら）'''の如く'''/湯津磐村之如久 - 意味：神聖な巌群（いわむら）の如く。 :「ゆ」は斎庭（ゆにわ、祭場のこと）の「ゆ」と同じ次田 2008年、p105。「ゆつ」は[[中臣寿詞]]（なかとみのよごと）に見える由都五百（ゆついほ）篁（たかむら）の「ゆつ」（厳橿（いつかし）[http://www.excite.co.jp/dictionary/japanese/?search=%E3%81%84%E3%81%A4%E3%81%8B%E3%81%97&match=beginswith&itemid=DJR_itukasi_-010 大辞林 第三版「いつかし〔厳し〕」] 三省堂 2015年7月19日閲覧の古語）と同じで、神聖や清浄を表し、即ち「神聖な岩の群のように」の意味になる。 *'''坐り塞す'''/塞坐 - 意味：立ち塞がり邪悪を防ぎ給う。 :『[[古事記]]』[[黄泉比良坂]]の段で、黄泉国から逃げ出す[[伊弉諾尊]]（いざなぎのみこと）は「爾ち千引石を其の黄泉比良坂に引き塞（さ）へて」と偉大なる岩石の力によって禍を塞いだが、それと同様の意義である。この場合の「さやる」は塞ぐ、防ぐ、遮る、留める、封じる、などの意味を表す。 *'''皇神'''（すめかみ） - 意味：神の尊称。 :神を敬う尊称、または皇室の祖先の神を敬う尊称、皇祖神のことである[http://www.excite.co.jp/dictionary/japanese/?search=%E3%81%99%E3%82%81%E3%81%8B%E3%81%BF&match=beginswith&itemid=DJR_sumekami_-010 大辞林 第三版「すめかみ〔皇神〕」] 三省堂 2015年7月19日閲覧。ここでは神の尊称を指す。 *'''根国底国'''（ねのくにそこのくに） - 意味：[[黄泉国]]。 :地下深くや遥か海の向こうにあるとする、死者が行く[[現世]]とは別の[[他界]][http://www.excite.co.jp/dictionary/japanese/?search=%E3%81%AD%E3%81%AE%E3%81%8F%E3%81%AB&match=beginswith&itemid=DJR_nenokuni_-010 大辞林 第三版「ねのくに〔根の国〕」] 三省堂 2015年7月18日閲覧。思想的には罪や厄災が行き来し、[[祓い]]の後[[穢れ]]が流れてゆく場所。 *'''麁'''（あら）'''び疎'''（うと）'''び来'''（こ）'''む物'''/麁備疎備来物 - 意味：朝廷に対して親しまずに荒ぶる禍神（まがかみ[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/207200/m0u/ デジタル大辞泉「まが-かみ〔禍神〕」] 小学館 2015年7月19日閲覧）や物の怪。 :多くの災禍は黄泉国よりやって来ると考えられていたことから、黄泉国から来る禍事（まがごと災厄、禍、災難、不幸な出来事。縁起の悪い不吉な言葉も意味するが、ここでは凶事を指す。[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/207208/m0u/ デジタル大辞泉「まが-ごと〔禍事・禍言〕」] 小学館 2015年7月19日閲覧）のことである次田 2008年、p384。言葉としての「荒び」は和む・穏やかな様子の対義語概念、荒々しい・荒立っている様子の表現で使われている次田 2008年、p272。かつ疎疎しいといった親しまない意味。「疎び」は睦むの対義語で、忌み嫌う表現である。 :「物」は「[[物の怪|もののけ]]（物の怪・物の気）の「もの」であり、邪神[[悪霊]]や妖怪などの類い全般を指す。 *'''明妙'''（あかるたへ）'''照妙'''（てるたへ）'''和妙'''（にぎたへ）'''荒妙'''（あらたへ）/明妙・照妙・和妙・荒妙 - 意味：多彩な織物。 :上代、[[布|布帛]]の原料は[[麻 (繊維)|麻]]・ 木綿（ゆう）・[[絹]]が多く、それらによる色々な[[織物]]次田 2008年、p82。特に繊維料植物の中で穀（かじ）の樹木樹皮からの織った織物は古代から盛んに用い、これを「たへ（栲）」と呼び原文の「妙（たへ）」に当る。そして布帛の材料となる緒（お）を[[木綿 (ゆう)|木綿（ゆう）]]と言った緒（お）とは[[糸]]や[[紐]]を含め、繊維によった細く線状のもの。。また穀の木は[[穀紙]]（こくし、又は楮紙）の原料となる[[楮]]のこととされる。 *'''皇御孫命'''（すめみまのみこと） - 意味：今上天皇 :「皇」は皇神（すめかみ）や天皇命（すめらみこと）と同じ「すめ」次田 2008年、p71。「孫命（みま）」は御真子（みまなご）の意味と御子に親愛を示した表現であり、本来は皇孫[[瓊瓊杵尊]]（ににぎのみこと）を指していたが、転じて各時代国を治める今上天皇のことを呼ぶようになった。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{Reflist|group="注"}} === 出典 === {{Reflist|2}} == 参考文献 == *{{Cite book|和書|author=西牟田 崇生|title=平成新編祝詞事典 縮刷版|publisher=続戎光祥|date=2003|isbn=4-900901-32-6}} *{{Cite book|和書|author=甲田 利雄|title=年中行事御障子文註解|publisher=続群書類従完成会|date=1976|isbn=4-7971-0525-9}} *{{Cite book|和書|author=次田 潤|title=祝詞新講 新版|publisher=戎光祥|date=2008||isbn=978-4-900901-85-8}} {{神道 横}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:みちあえのまつり}} [[Category:神道行事]] [[Category:日本の祭り]] [[Category:旧暦6月]] [[Category:旧暦12月]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[道饗祭]] to [[Michiae no matsuri]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
{{令制祭祀}} '''道饗祭'''（'''みちあえのまつり'''、ちあえのまつり）とは、神道[[祭祀]]の1つ。[[古代|上代]]から毎年6月と12月の2回、都の四隅道上で、'''八衢比古神'''（やちまたひこのかみ）、'''八衢比売神'''（やちまたひめのかみ）、'''久那斗神'''（くなどのかみ)の3柱を祀り、都や[[朝庭|宮城]]の中に災いをもたらす[[魑魅|鬼魅]]や[[妖怪]]が入らぬよう防ぎ、守護を祈願する[[神事]]、および神社の[[祭]][http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/212207/m0u/ デジタル大辞泉「みちあえのまつり〔道饗の祭〕」] 小学館 2015年07月12日閲覧。令制祭祀としては[[京都]]が中心だったが、[[疫病|疾疫]]が起こった時は[[地方]]でも斎行した[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 世界大百科事典 第2版「みちあえのまつり〔道饗祭〕」] 平凡社 2015年07月12日閲覧。例えば、武蔵国では、饗庭（あえば）という地名や氏名が多いが、[[柳田國男]]は『地名の研究』（角川文庫 九版1974年 p.216）において、道饗祭＝邪神祭却に由来するものであり、北武蔵の「アイノ田」「間の田」に関しても、「饗場の田」に由来すると推測している（同書 p.216）。 == 概要 == [[令]]の編目の一、神祇令に定められた恒例の祭典である。[[平安時代]]成立の法令集『[[延喜式]]・第一巻』（[[藤原時平]]/[[藤原忠平]]ら編集）6月祭条に記され、小祀に区分する律令制で、前1日間を[[物忌み]]して行う祭祀。[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/108492/m0u/%E5%B0%8F%E7%A5%80/ デジタル大辞泉「しょうし〔小祀〕」] 小学館 2015年07月12日閲覧。[[京職|左右京職]]が司り、[[卜部氏|卜部（うらべ）氏]]がはらえを務めた[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典「みちあえのまつり〔道饗祭〕」] 日本語版 2015年07月12日閲覧。律令時代[[京都]]では鎮火祭（ほしずめのまつり）と兼ねて行うことが多かった西牟田 2003年、p119。祭日は[[陰暦]]の[[6月]]と[[12月]]、その月の晦日にある[[大祓]]の後に開催したと言われるが、『延喜式』や『[[大宝律令|大宝令]]』に祭日の明記がないことから、吉日を選び執り行ったとする『[[拾芥抄]]』の説をとる見解もある次田 2008年、p378。 神道では[[春分]]と[[秋分]]・[[夏至]]と[[冬至]]・上半期と下半期など、半年周期を節目としながら斎行する神事例が多く見当たり、これらは[[農耕]]儀礼との結びつきや、[[季節]]・動植物の移り変わりを目安とする自然暦・[[自然観]]に基づき、[[半年]]毎を基本としていた所以である。 === 基本情報表 === {| class="wikitable" !項目!!説明!!備考 |- !rowspan="3"|祭神 |colspan="2"|八衢比売神・八衢姫神（やちまたひめのかみ） |- |colspan="2"|八衢比古神・八衢彦神（やちまたひこのかみ） |- |colspan="2"|久那斗神（くなどのかみ） |- !rowspan="3"|律令 |rowspan="3"|神祇令・小祀||[[近江令]]（[[668年]]制定） |- |[[飛鳥浄御原令]]（[[681年]]編纂） |- |[[養老令]]（第6篇・20条、[[718年]]から） |- !開催日 |陰暦の6月・12月||晦日、又は吉日 |- !rowspan="2"|信仰 |rowspan="2"|神祇信仰（[[古神道]]）||自然信仰 |- |原始祖霊信仰 |- |} == 文献 == === 公事根源と西宮記 === [[疫病神]]の災厄をはらう祭典の内に、[[陰陽寮]]の四角四境祭がある次田 2008年、p380。[[室町時代|室町中期]]成立、朝廷における年間行事や儀式の由来・沿革を述べた『[[公事根源]]』（[[一条兼良]]・著書）では、道饗祭を「鎮火道饗の祭を四角四境の祭とも申也（もうすなり）」と記しているが誤りで、鎮火祭も道饗祭も四角祭や四境祭とは別の祭である甲田 1976年、p229。 平安中期成立、[[朝廷 (日本)|朝廷]]儀式の作法や制度などを述べた『[[西宮記]]』（[[源高明]]・著書）によると、疫病の兆しがあった歳に臨時に都の四隅で行う祭を「四角祭」、諸国の四境で行う祭を「四境祭（四界祭）」と呼んだものと記している。恒例的に神々を祀る祭を指してはいなかったが、後世の誤認により道饗祭や鎮火祭は一緒くたに纏められ解釈されていた。特に疫病が流行ると地方でも臨時に道饗祭を行ない、疫病神を祀る祭祀と誤解や混同が生じやすい環境にあった。 {{Cquote|四界祭 - 陰陽寮向ニ四界一祭、以ニ蔵人所人一為レ使。
四角祭 - 陰陽寮向ニ宮城四角一祭、有レ使所人。 已上天下有レ疫之時、陰陽寮進ニ示度。科物官宣。|||『西宮記』|次田 2008年、p380}} なお公事の他、[[民間信仰]]で疫病神の祭の種類は極めて多種多様に富み、広範囲で開催した次田 2008年、p381。 == 祝詞 == {{Quotation|'''原文''' - 高天之原尓事始氐、皇御孫之命止称辞竟奉、大八衢尓湯津磐村之如久塞坐皇神等之前尓申久、八衢比古・八衢比売・久那斗止御名者申氐、辞竟奉久波、根国底国与里麁備疎備来物尓、相率相口会事無氐、下行者下乎守理、上往者上乎守理、夜之守・日之守尓守奉斎奉礼止、 進幣帛者明妙・照妙・和妙・荒妙尓備奉、御酒者瓺辺高知、瓺腹満双氐、汁尓母穎尓母、山野尓住物者、毛能和物・毛能荒物、青海原尓住物者、鰭乃広物・鰭乃狭物、奥津海菜・辺津海菜尓至万尓氐、横山之如久置所足氐、進宇豆乃幣帛乎平気久聞食氐 八衢尓湯津磐村之如久塞坐氐皇御孫命乎堅磐尓常磐尓齋奉、茂御世尓幸閉奉給止申、又親王等・王等・臣等・百官人等、天下公民尓至万氐尓 平久斎給部止、神官天津祝詞乃太祝詞事乎以氐、称辞竟奉止申|『延喜式』|道饗祭祝詞}} === 意義・備考 === *'''高天之原'''（たかまのはら）'''に事始'''（ことはじ）'''めて'''/高天之原尓事始氐 - 意味：高天原に坐す御祖神等が事始め給いて。 :道饗祭は[[高天原]]に起源を発する神事であり、斎行している道饗祭は高天原で事始まった[[皇祖神|皇祖天神]]の[[神勅]]によって行っている、といった内容である。同様の記述が『[[古史伝]]』（平田篤胤・著書）にもあり、踏襲の解釈。 *'''皇御孫の命と'''/皇御孫之命止 - 意味：御代知らし食す今上天皇の詔（みことのり）・仰言によって。 :本来は「皇御孫命の命」が正しいが、命の漢字が重複するため一つ省いてある次田 2008年、p383。皇御孫は各時代国を治める[[今上天皇]]を指し、命は御言の意味であり[[詔]]や仰言である。 *'''称辞'''（たたへごと）'''竟'''（を）'''へ奉る'''/称辞竟奉 - 意味：祭祀を行い奉る。 :祝詞に多く用いる一種の[[慣用句]]次田 2008年、p69。「たたへごと」は「たたへことば」のことで賛詞を意味し、「たたへ」は水を湛えるの「たたえ」と同じく満ち足りを表現している、次の「をへ」は極め尽くすの意味である。従って本来の原義は「称辞を竟へ奉る（意味：善言美辞を尽くし、神の威徳を褒め称え奉る）」になるものの、慣用句と頻繁に用いられた結果、単に「祭る」や「幣帛などを奉献する」「神の御名を唱え奉る」の意味にも転じるようになった。 *'''大八衢'''（おおやちまた） - 意味：宮城外にある大道路の衢（ちまた）。 :「八」は数字の8ではなく、数が多いことを表す[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/222073/m0u/ デジタル大辞泉「や‐ちまた〔八衢〕」] 小学館 2015年7月18日閲覧。「衢」は道俣（ちまた）を意味し、辻道のように四方（又はそれ以上）に道が分岐した場所のことである。 *'''湯津磐村'''（ゆついはむら）'''の如く'''/湯津磐村之如久 - 意味：神聖な巌群（いわむら）の如く。 :「ゆ」は斎庭（ゆにわ、祭場のこと）の「ゆ」と同じ次田 2008年、p105。「ゆつ」は[[中臣寿詞]]（なかとみのよごと）に見える由都五百（ゆついほ）篁（たかむら）の「ゆつ」（厳橿（いつかし）[http://www.excite.co.jp/dictionary/japanese/?search=%E3%81%84%E3%81%A4%E3%81%8B%E3%81%97&match=beginswith&itemid=DJR_itukasi_-010 大辞林 第三版「いつかし〔厳し〕」] 三省堂 2015年7月19日閲覧の古語）と同じで、神聖や清浄を表し、即ち「神聖な岩の群のように」の意味になる。 *'''坐り塞す'''/塞坐 - 意味：立ち塞がり邪悪を防ぎ給う。 :『[[古事記]]』[[黄泉比良坂]]の段で、黄泉国から逃げ出す[[伊弉諾尊]]（いざなぎのみこと）は「爾ち千引石を其の黄泉比良坂に引き塞（さ）へて」と偉大なる岩石の力によって禍を塞いだが、それと同様の意義である。この場合の「さやる」は塞ぐ、防ぐ、遮る、留める、封じる、などの意味を表す。 *'''皇神'''（すめかみ） - 意味：神の尊称。 :神を敬う尊称、または皇室の祖先の神を敬う尊称、皇祖神のことである[http://www.excite.co.jp/dictionary/japanese/?search=%E3%81%99%E3%82%81%E3%81%8B%E3%81%BF&match=beginswith&itemid=DJR_sumekami_-010 大辞林 第三版「すめかみ〔皇神〕」] 三省堂 2015年7月19日閲覧。ここでは神の尊称を指す。 *'''根国底国'''（ねのくにそこのくに） - 意味：[[黄泉国]]。 :地下深くや遥か海の向こうにあるとする、死者が行く[[現世]]とは別の[[他界]][http://www.excite.co.jp/dictionary/japanese/?search=%E3%81%AD%E3%81%AE%E3%81%8F%E3%81%AB&match=beginswith&itemid=DJR_nenokuni_-010 大辞林 第三版「ねのくに〔根の国〕」] 三省堂 2015年7月18日閲覧。思想的には罪や厄災が行き来し、[[祓い]]の後[[穢れ]]が流れてゆく場所。 *'''麁'''（あら）'''び疎'''（うと）'''び来'''（こ）'''む物'''/麁備疎備来物 - 意味：朝廷に対して親しまずに荒ぶる禍神（まがかみ[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/207200/m0u/ デジタル大辞泉「まが-かみ〔禍神〕」] 小学館 2015年7月19日閲覧）や物の怪。 :多くの災禍は黄泉国よりやって来ると考えられていたことから、黄泉国から来る禍事（まがごと災厄、禍、災難、不幸な出来事。縁起の悪い不吉な言葉も意味するが、ここでは凶事を指す。[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/207208/m0u/ デジタル大辞泉「まが-ごと〔禍事・禍言〕」] 小学館 2015年7月19日閲覧）のことである次田 2008年、p384。言葉としての「荒び」は和む・穏やかな様子の対義語概念、荒々しい・荒立っている様子の表現で使われている次田 2008年、p272。かつ疎疎しいといった親しまない意味。「疎び」は睦むの対義語で、忌み嫌う表現である。 :「物」は「[[物の怪|もののけ]]（物の怪・物の気）の「もの」であり、邪神[[悪霊]]や妖怪などの類い全般を指す。 *'''明妙'''（あかるたへ）'''照妙'''（てるたへ）'''和妙'''（にぎたへ）'''荒妙'''（あらたへ）/明妙・照妙・和妙・荒妙 - 意味：多彩な織物。 :上代、[[布|布帛]]の原料は[[麻 (繊維)|麻]]・ 木綿（ゆう）・[[絹]]が多く、それらによる色々な[[織物]]次田 2008年、p82。特に繊維料植物の中で穀（かじ）の樹木樹皮からの織った織物は古代から盛んに用い、これを「たへ（栲）」と呼び原文の「妙（たへ）」に当る。そして布帛の材料となる緒（お）を[[木綿 (ゆう)|木綿（ゆう）]]と言った緒（お）とは[[糸]]や[[紐]]を含め、繊維によった細く線状のもの。。また穀の木は[[穀紙]]（こくし、又は楮紙）の原料となる[[楮]]のこととされる。 *'''皇御孫命'''（すめみまのみこと） - 意味：今上天皇 :「皇」は皇神（すめかみ）や天皇命（すめらみこと）と同じ「すめ」次田 2008年、p71。「孫命（みま）」は御真子（みまなご）の意味と御子に親愛を示した表現であり、本来は皇孫[[瓊瓊杵尊]]（ににぎのみこと）を指していたが、転じて各時代国を治める今上天皇のことを呼ぶようになった。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{Reflist|group="注"}} === 出典 === {{Reflist|2}} == 参考文献 == *{{Cite book|和書|author=西牟田 崇生|title=平成新編祝詞事典 縮刷版|publisher=続戎光祥|date=2003|isbn=4-900901-32-6}} *{{Cite book|和書|author=甲田 利雄|title=年中行事御障子文註解|publisher=続群書類従完成会|date=1976|isbn=4-7971-0525-9}} *{{Cite book|和書|author=次田 潤|title=祝詞新講 新版|publisher=戎光祥|date=2008||isbn=978-4-900901-85-8}} {{神道 横}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:みちあえのまつり}} [[Category:神道行事]] [[Category:日本の祭り]] [[Category:旧暦6月]] [[Category:旧暦12月]]

{{User:Immanuelle/Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace updated}}
The Festival of the Road Gods (道祖神祭り, Michiae no matsuri) was an ancient ceremony prescribed by the Divinities Prescriptions (Jingiryō) intended to prevent evil spirits, such as demons and epidemic gods (ekishin), from entering the capital. This festival was conducted on the main roads (ōji) surrounding Kyoto in each of the four cardinal directions.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-09-29|title=Michiae no matsuri {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230929230332/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9286|access-date=2024-05-18|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
==Overview==
{{令制祭祀}} '''Michae no Matsuri''' (道饗祭, also read as ちあえのまつり) is one of the Shinto [[rites]]. It is held twice a year, in June and December, at the four corners of the capital's roads to enshrine the deities '''Yachimata-hiko''' (八衢比古神), '''Yachimata-hime''' (八衢比売神), and '''Kunado''' (久那斗神). The purpose of this ceremony is to prevent and protect the capital and the [[Imperial Palace]] from malevolent spirits such as [[demons]] and [[yōkai]], and to pray for divine protection.<ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/212207/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Michae no Matsuri] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> Although the ritual was centered in [[Kyoto]] under the [[Ritsuryō system]], it was also performed in rural areas during outbreaks of [[epidemics]].<ref name="da1">[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 World Encyclopedia, 2nd Edition: Michae no Matsuri] Heibonsha, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> In Musashi Province, the name "Ae-ba" (饗庭) appears frequently as a place name and surname, which folklorist [[Kunio Yanagita]] hypothesized to have originated from Michae no Matsuri.<ref name="da1"/> He also speculated that place names like "Aino-ta" and "Manota" in northern Musashi derived from "fields of Ae-ba."<ref>Kunio Yanagita, *Research on Place Names*, Kadokawa Bunko, 9th edition, 1974, p. 216.</ref> == Overview == Michae no Matsuri is a recurring festival prescribed under the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令|12=draft}}, one of the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令|12=draft}} system's codes.<ref name="da1"/> It is listed in the "June Festivals" section of the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|12=draft}}, compiled during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}}, and categorized as a "minor ritual."<ref group="注">Rites conducted with one day of purification beforehand under the Ritsuryō system.</ref><ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/108492/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Minor Ritual] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> The ceremony was overseen by the {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職|12=draft}} (Capital Officials), with the {{ill|Urabe clan|ja|卜部氏|12=draft}} performing the purification.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 Britannica International Encyclopedia: Michae no Matsuri] Britannica Japanese Edition, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> It was often held in conjunction with the {{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火祭|12=draft}} in Kyoto.<ref name="n19">Nishimuta 2003, p. 119</ref>
While traditionally thought to occur after the {{ill|Ōharae|ja|大祓|12=draft}} (Great Purification) on the 30th day of the 6th and 12th lunar months, no specific festival date is recorded in the *Engishiki* or *Taika Reforms*, leading some to conclude that auspicious days were chosen instead, as suggested by the {{ill|Shūgaishō|ja|拾芥抄|12=draft}}.<ref name="tu1">Tsugita 2008, p. 378</ref>
Shinto traditions often observe half-yearly rituals based on {{ill|seasonal agriculture|ja|農耕儀礼|12=draft}}, the passage of {{ill|natural cycles|ja|自然観|12=draft}}, and the transformations of plants and animals.<ref name="da1"/>
== Summary Table == {| class="wikitable" ! Item !! Description !! Remarks |- ! Deities | colspan="2"|Yachimata-hime (八衢比売神), Yachimata-hiko (八衢比古神), Kunado (久那斗神) |- ! Code | colspan="2"|Jingiryō, classified as a minor ritual |- | rowspan="3"|Legal References | * {{ill|Ōmi Code|ja|近江令|12=draft}} (668 CE) |- | * {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令|12=draft}} (681 CE) |- | * {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=draft}} (Chapter 6, Article 20, 718 CE) |- ! Dates | 6th and 12th lunar months || Specific dates vary |- ! Faith | rowspan="2"|Ancient Shinto || Includes natural worship |- | Rooted in ancestral worship |}
== Historical Interpretations == === *Kujigen* and *Nishinomiya-ki* === The {{ill|Kujigen|ja|公事根源|12=draft}} by {{ill|Ichijō Kanera|ja|一条兼良|12=draft}} misidentifies Michae no Matsuri as the "Festival of the Four Corners," combining it with the {{ill|Fire Calming Festival|ja|鎮火祭|12=draft}}, which were separate.<ref name="ko1">Kōda 1976, p. 229</ref>
Meanwhile, the *Nishinomiya-ki* by {{ill|Minamoto no Takaaki|ja|源高明|12=draft}} clarifies the distinctions: the "Four Corners Festival" was conducted during epidemic outbreaks at Kyoto's periphery, and the "Four Borders Festival" targeted the nation's outermost provinces.<ref name="tu2">Tsugita 2008, p. 380</ref>
Despite these clear distinctions, misunderstandings led later generations to conflate the rites.<ref name="tu1"/> Epidemic outbreaks further blurred the line between Michae no Matsuri and rites honoring pestilence gods.<ref name="tu3">Tsugita 2008, p. 381</ref>
== Liturgical Text == A key portion of the Michae no Matsuri prayer: {{Quotation|'''Original Text:''' 高天之原尓事始氐、皇御孫命止称辞竟奉、...|source=Engishiki}}
== Significance == Key phrases in the prayer include: * '''Takamahara''' (高天原) signifies the celestial origin of the ritual. * '''Ōyamato''' (大八衢) denotes sacred crossroads that safeguard the imperial capital.
== Historical Context == The precise origins of the Festival of the Road Gods are unclear, but its formal characteristics suggest it is closely linked to the establishment of capital cities in Japan. It likely dates back to the founding of the Fujiwara Palace in Nara at the end of the seventh century. The festival's association with the introduction of capital cities suggests its purpose was to protect these new urban centers from malevolent supernatural influences.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Ritual Elements == The festival was performed by diviners (urabe) from the Department of Divinities (Jingikan), who utilized ritual implements made of fur. This indicates a strong connection to yin-yang divination practices (onmyōdō). The use of these implements and the involvement of the Department of Divinities highlight the festival's spiritual and protective purposes.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Decline and Disappearance == The Festival of the Road Gods gradually disappeared during the mid-Heian period, influenced by the rise of the Festival of the Four Directions (Shikaku sai) and other similar observances. As these other festivals gained prominence, references to the Michiae no matsuri became scarce in historical records.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Interpretations and Records == The Ryō no gige (Explanations of the Prescriptions) notes that the Michiae no matsuri aimed to prevent calamity-causing demons from entering the capital by intercepting and entertaining them in the streets. Conversely, the norito prayer recorded in the Engi Shiki (Engi Regulations) for this festival indicates that it celebrated three deities: Yachimata-hiko and other road deities. This suggests a dual interpretation of the festival's purpose: both as a means of protection from malevolent spirits and as a celebration of specific deities associated with the roads.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Notes ==

revision.comment: Bot: add simplewiki draft link to ill templates
revision.text:
{{User:Immanuelle/Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace updated}}
The Festival of the Road Gods (道祖神祭り, Michiae no matsuri) was an ancient ceremony prescribed by the Divinities Prescriptions (Jingiryō) intended to prevent evil spirits, such as demons and epidemic gods (ekishin), from entering the capital. This festival was conducted on the main roads (ōji) surrounding Kyoto in each of the four cardinal directions.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-09-29|title=Michiae no matsuri {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230929230332/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9286|access-date=2024-05-18|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
==Overview==
{{令制祭祀}} '''Michae no Matsuri''' (道饗祭, also read as ちあえのまつり) is one of the Shinto [[rites]]. It is held twice a year, in June and December, at the four corners of the capital's roads to enshrine the deities '''Yachimata-hiko''' (八衢比古神), '''Yachimata-hime''' (八衢比売神), and '''Kunado''' (久那斗神). The purpose of this ceremony is to prevent and protect the capital and the [[Imperial Palace]] from malevolent spirits such as [[demons]] and [[yōkai]], and to pray for divine protection.<ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/212207/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Michae no Matsuri] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> Although the ritual was centered in [[Kyoto]] under the [[Ritsuryō system]], it was also performed in rural areas during outbreaks of [[epidemics]].<ref name="da1">[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 World Encyclopedia, 2nd Edition: Michae no Matsuri] Heibonsha, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> In Musashi Province, the name "Ae-ba" (饗庭) appears frequently as a place name and surname, which folklorist [[Kunio Yanagita]] hypothesized to have originated from Michae no Matsuri.<ref name="da1"/> He also speculated that place names like "Aino-ta" and "Manota" in northern Musashi derived from "fields of Ae-ba."<ref>Kunio Yanagita, *Research on Place Names*, Kadokawa Bunko, 9th edition, 1974, p. 216.</ref> == Overview == Michae no Matsuri is a recurring festival prescribed under the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingiryō|qq=}}, one of the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō|qq=}} system's codes.<ref name="da1"/> It is listed in the "June Festivals" section of the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|qq=}}, compiled during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}}, and categorized as a "minor ritual."<ref group="注">Rites conducted with one day of purification beforehand under the Ritsuryō system.</ref><ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/108492/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Minor Ritual] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> The ceremony was overseen by the {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōshiki|qq=}} (Capital Officials), with the {{ill|Urabe clan|ja|卜部氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urabe clan|qq=}} performing the purification.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 Britannica International Encyclopedia: Michae no Matsuri] Britannica Japanese Edition, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> It was often held in conjunction with the {{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fire-Pacification Festival|qq=}} in Kyoto.<ref name="n19">Nishimuta 2003, p. 119</ref>
While traditionally thought to occur after the {{ill|Ōharae|ja|大祓|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōharae|qq=}} (Great Purification) on the 30th day of the 6th and 12th lunar months, no specific festival date is recorded in the *Engishiki* or *Taika Reforms*, leading some to conclude that auspicious days were chosen instead, as suggested by the {{ill|Shūgaishō|ja|拾芥抄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shūgaishō|qq=}}.<ref name="tu1">Tsugita 2008, p. 378</ref>
Shinto traditions often observe half-yearly rituals based on {{ill|seasonal agriculture|ja|農耕儀礼|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/seasonal agriculture|qq=}}, the passage of {{ill|natural cycles|ja|自然観|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/natural cycles|qq=}}, and the transformations of plants and animals.<ref name="da1"/>
== Summary Table == {| class="wikitable" ! Item !! Description !! Remarks |- ! Deities | colspan="2"|Yachimata-hime (八衢比売神), Yachimata-hiko (八衢比古神), Kunado (久那斗神) |- ! Code | colspan="2"|Jingiryō, classified as a minor ritual |- | rowspan="3"|Legal References | * {{ill|Ōmi Code|ja|近江令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi Code|qq=}} (668 CE) |- | * {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Kiyomihara Code|qq=}} (681 CE) |- | * {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|qq=}} (Chapter 6, Article 20, 718 CE) |- ! Dates | 6th and 12th lunar months || Specific dates vary |- ! Faith | rowspan="2"|Ancient Shinto || Includes natural worship |- | Rooted in ancestral worship |}
== Historical Interpretations == === *Kujigen* and *Nishinomiya-ki* === The {{ill|Kujigen|ja|公事根源|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujigen|qq=}} by {{ill|Ichijō Kanera|ja|一条兼良|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichijō Kanera|qq=}} misidentifies Michae no Matsuri as the "Festival of the Four Corners," combining it with the {{ill|Fire Calming Festival|ja|鎮火祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fire Calming Festival|qq=}}, which were separate.<ref name="ko1">Kōda 1976, p. 229</ref>
Meanwhile, the *Nishinomiya-ki* by {{ill|Minamoto no Takaaki|ja|源高明|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Takaaki|qq=}} clarifies the distinctions: the "Four Corners Festival" was conducted during epidemic outbreaks at Kyoto's periphery, and the "Four Borders Festival" targeted the nation's outermost provinces.<ref name="tu2">Tsugita 2008, p. 380</ref>
Despite these clear distinctions, misunderstandings led later generations to conflate the rites.<ref name="tu1"/> Epidemic outbreaks further blurred the line between Michae no Matsuri and rites honoring pestilence gods.<ref name="tu3">Tsugita 2008, p. 381</ref>
== Liturgical Text == A key portion of the Michae no Matsuri prayer: {{Quotation|'''Original Text:''' 高天之原尓事始氐、皇御孫命止称辞竟奉、...|source=Engishiki}}
== Significance == Key phrases in the prayer include: * '''Takamahara''' (高天原) signifies the celestial origin of the ritual. * '''Ōyamato''' (大八衢) denotes sacred crossroads that safeguard the imperial capital.
== Historical Context == The precise origins of the Festival of the Road Gods are unclear, but its formal characteristics suggest it is closely linked to the establishment of capital cities in Japan. It likely dates back to the founding of the Fujiwara Palace in Nara at the end of the seventh century. The festival's association with the introduction of capital cities suggests its purpose was to protect these new urban centers from malevolent supernatural influences.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Ritual Elements == The festival was performed by diviners (urabe) from the Department of Divinities (Jingikan), who utilized ritual implements made of fur. This indicates a strong connection to yin-yang divination practices (onmyōdō). The use of these implements and the involvement of the Department of Divinities highlight the festival's spiritual and protective purposes.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Decline and Disappearance == The Festival of the Road Gods gradually disappeared during the mid-Heian period, influenced by the rise of the Festival of the Four Directions (Shikaku sai) and other similar observances. As these other festivals gained prominence, references to the Michiae no matsuri became scarce in historical records.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Interpretations and Records == The Ryō no gige (Explanations of the Prescriptions) notes that the Michiae no matsuri aimed to prevent calamity-causing demons from entering the capital by intercepting and entertaining them in the streets. Conversely, the norito prayer recorded in the Engi Shiki (Engi Regulations) for this festival indicates that it celebrated three deities: Yachimata-hiko and other road deities. This suggests a dual interpretation of the festival's purpose: both as a means of protection from malevolent spirits and as a celebration of specific deities associated with the roads.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Notes ==
=== Annotations === {{Reflist|group="注"}} <references group="lower-alpha"/>
=== References === {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * Nishimuta, Takaoki. *Heisei New Edition Norito Dictionary.* 2003, ISBN 4-900901-32-6. * Kōda, Toshio. *Annotated Annual Events.* 1976, ISBN 4-7971-0525-9. * Tsugita, Jun. *Revised Norito Interpretations.* 2008, ISBN 978-4-900901-85-8.
{{Shinto Sidebar}} {{Shinto-stub}}

Code | colspan="2"|Jingiryō, classified as a minor ritual |- | rowspan="3"|Legal References | * [[:en:Ōmi Code|Ōmi Code]] (668 CE) |- | * [[:en:Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka Kiyomihara Code]] (681 CE) |- | * {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|qq=}} (Chapter 6, Article 20, 718 CE) |- ! Dates | 6th and 12th lunar months || Specific dates vary |- ! Faith | rowspan="2"|Ancient Shinto || Includes natural worship |- | Rooted in ancestral worship |}
== Historical Interpretations == === *Kujigen* and *Nishinomiya-ki* === The {{ill|Kujigen|ja|公事根源|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujigen|qq=}} by [[:en:Ichijō Kaneyoshi|Ichijō Kanera]] misidentifies Michae no Matsuri as the "Festival of the Four Corners," combining it with the {{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Fire Calming Festival|ja|鎮火祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fire Calming Festival|qq=}}, which were separate.<ref name="ko1">Kōda 1976, p. 229</ref>
Meanwhile, the *Nishinomiya-ki* by {{ill|Minamoto no Takaaki|ja|源高明|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Takaaki|qq=}} clarifies the distinctions: the "Four Corners Festival" was conducted during epidemic outbreaks at Kyoto's periphery, and the "Four Borders Festival" targeted the nation's outermost provinces.<ref name="tu2">Tsugita 2008, p. 380</ref>
Despite these clear distinctions, misunderstandings led later generations to conflate the rites.<ref name="tu1"/> Epidemic outbreaks further blurred the line between Michae no Matsuri and rites honoring pestilence gods.<ref name="tu3">Tsugita 2008, p. 381</ref>
== Liturgical Text == A key portion of the Michae no Matsuri prayer: {{Quotation|'''Original Text:''' 高天之原尓事始氐、皇御孫命止称辞竟奉、...|source=Engishiki}}
== Significance == Key phrases in the prayer include: * '''Takamahara''' (高天原) signifies the celestial origin of the ritual. * '''Ōyamato''' (大八衢) denotes sacred crossroads that safeguard the imperial capital.
== Historical Context == The precise origins of the Festival of the Road Gods are unclear, but its formal characteristics suggest it is closely linked to the establishment of capital cities in Japan. It likely dates back to the founding of the Fujiwara Palace in Nara at the end of the seventh century. The festival's association with the introduction of capital cities suggests its purpose was to protect these new urban centers from malevolent supernatural influences.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Ritual Elements == The festival was performed by diviners (urabe) from the Department of Divinities (Jingikan), who utilized ritual implements made of fur. This indicates a strong connection to yin-yang divination practices (onmyōdō). The use of these implements and the involvement of the Department of Divinities highlight the festival's spiritual and protective purposes.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Decline and Disappearance == The Festival of the Road Gods gradually disappeared during the mid-Heian period, influenced by the rise of the Festival of the Four Directions (Shikaku sai) and other similar observances. As these other festivals gained prominence, references to the Michiae no matsuri became scarce in historical records.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Interpretations and Records == The Ryō no gige (Explanations of the Prescriptions) notes that the Michiae no matsuri aimed to prevent calamity-causing demons from entering the capital by intercepting and entertaining them in the streets. Conversely, the norito prayer recorded in the Engi Shiki (Engi Regulations) for this festival indicates that it celebrated three deities: Yachimata-hiko and other road deities. This suggests a dual interpretation of the festival's purpose: both as a means of protection from malevolent spirits and as a celebration of specific deities associated with the roads.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Notes ==
=== Annotations === {{Reflist|group="注"}} <references group="lower-alpha"/>
=== References === {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * Nishimuta, Takaoki. *Heisei New Edition Norito Dictionary.* 2003, ISBN 4-900901-32-6. * Kōda, Toshio. *Annotated Annual Events.* 1976, ISBN 4-7971-0525-9. * Tsugita, Jun. *Revised Norito Interpretations.* 2008, ISBN 978-4-900901-85-8.

revision.comment: Bot: add category Category:Festivals in Japan
revision.text:
{{User:Immanuelle/Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace updated}}
The Festival of the Road Gods (道祖神祭り, Michiae no matsuri) was an ancient ceremony prescribed by the Divinities Prescriptions (Jingiryō) intended to prevent evil spirits, such as demons and epidemic gods (ekishin), from entering the capital. This festival was conducted on the main roads (ōji) surrounding Kyoto in each of the four cardinal directions.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-09-29|title=Michiae no matsuri {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230929230332/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9286|access-date=2024-05-18|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
==Overview==
{{令制祭祀}} '''Michae no Matsuri''' (道饗祭, also read as ちあえのまつり) is one of the Shinto [[rites]]. It is held twice a year, in June and December, at the four corners of the capital's roads to enshrine the deities '''Yachimata-hiko''' (八衢比古神), '''Yachimata-hime''' (八衢比売神), and '''Kunado''' (久那斗神). The purpose of this ceremony is to prevent and protect the capital and the [[Imperial Palace]] from malevolent spirits such as [[demons]] and [[yōkai]], and to pray for divine protection.<ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/212207/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Michae no Matsuri] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> Although the ritual was centered in [[Kyoto]] under the [[Ritsuryō system]], it was also performed in rural areas during outbreaks of [[epidemics]].<ref name="da1">[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 World Encyclopedia, 2nd Edition: Michae no Matsuri] Heibonsha, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> In Musashi Province, the name "Ae-ba" (饗庭) appears frequently as a place name and surname, which folklorist [[Kunio Yanagita]] hypothesized to have originated from Michae no Matsuri.<ref name="da1"/> He also speculated that place names like "Aino-ta" and "Manota" in northern Musashi derived from "fields of Ae-ba."<ref>Kunio Yanagita, *Research on Place Names*, Kadokawa Bunko, 9th edition, 1974, p. 216.</ref> == Overview == Michae no Matsuri is a recurring festival prescribed under the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingiryō|qq=}}, one of the [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō]] system's codes.<ref name="da1"/> It is listed in the "June Festivals" section of the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]], compiled during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and categorized as a "minor ritual."<ref group="注">Rites conducted with one day of purification beforehand under the Ritsuryō system.</ref><ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/108492/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Minor Ritual] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> The ceremony was overseen by the {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōshiki|qq=}} (Capital Officials), with the {{ill|Urabe clan|ja|卜部氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urabe clan|qq=}} performing the purification.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 Britannica International Encyclopedia: Michae no Matsuri] Britannica Japanese Edition, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> It was often held in conjunction with the {{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fire-Pacification Festival|qq=}} in Kyoto.<ref name="n19">Nishimuta 2003, p. 119</ref>
While traditionally thought to occur after the [[:en:Ōharae-shiki|Ōharae]] (Great Purification) on the 30th day of the 6th and 12th lunar months, no specific festival date is recorded in the *Engishiki* or *Taika Reforms*, leading some to conclude that auspicious days were chosen instead, as suggested by the {{ill|Shūgaishō|ja|拾芥抄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shūgaishō|qq=}}.<ref name="tu1">Tsugita 2008, p. 378</ref>
Shinto traditions often observe half-yearly rituals based on [[:en:Religion and agriculture|seasonal agriculture]], the passage of {{ill|natural cycles|ja|自然観|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/natural cycles|qq=}}, and the transformations of plants and animals.<ref name="da1"/>
== Summary Table == {| class="wikitable" ! Item !! Description !! Remarks |- ! Deities | colspan="2"|Yachimata-hime (八衢比売神), Yachimata-hiko (八衢比古神), Kunado (久那斗神) |- ! Code | colspan="2"|Jingiryō, classified as a minor ritual |- | rowspan="3"|Legal References | * [[:en:Ōmi Code|Ōmi Code]] (668 CE) |- | * [[:en:Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka Kiyomihara Code]] (681 CE) |- | * {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|qq=}} (Chapter 6, Article 20, 718 CE) |- ! Dates | 6th and 12th lunar months || Specific dates vary |- ! Faith | rowspan="2"|Ancient Shinto || Includes natural worship |- | Rooted in ancestral worship |}
== Historical Interpretations == === *Kujigen* and *Nishinomiya-ki* === The {{ill|Kujigen|ja|公事根源|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujigen|qq=}} by [[:en:Ichijō Kaneyoshi|Ichijō Kanera]] misidentifies Michae no Matsuri as the "Festival of the Four Corners," combining it with the {{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Fire Calming Festival|ja|鎮火祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fire Calming Festival|qq=}}, which were separate.<ref name="ko1">Kōda 1976, p. 229</ref>
Meanwhile, the *Nishinomiya-ki* by {{ill|Minamoto no Takaaki|ja|源高明|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Takaaki|qq=}} clarifies the distinctions: the "Four Corners Festival" was conducted during epidemic outbreaks at Kyoto's periphery, and the "Four Borders Festival" targeted the nation's outermost provinces.<ref name="tu2">Tsugita 2008, p. 380</ref>
Despite these clear distinctions, misunderstandings led later generations to conflate the rites.<ref name="tu1"/> Epidemic outbreaks further blurred the line between Michae no Matsuri and rites honoring pestilence gods.<ref name="tu3">Tsugita 2008, p. 381</ref>
== Liturgical Text == A key portion of the Michae no Matsuri prayer: {{Quotation|'''Original Text:''' 高天之原尓事始氐、皇御孫命止称辞竟奉、...|source=Engishiki}}
== Significance == Key phrases in the prayer include: * '''Takamahara''' (高天原) signifies the celestial origin of the ritual. * '''Ōyamato''' (大八衢) denotes sacred crossroads that safeguard the imperial capital.
== Historical Context == The precise origins of the Festival of the Road Gods are unclear, but its formal characteristics suggest it is closely linked to the establishment of capital cities in Japan. It likely dates back to the founding of the Fujiwara Palace in Nara at the end of the seventh century. The festival's association with the introduction of capital cities suggests its purpose was to protect these new urban centers from malevolent supernatural influences.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Ritual Elements == The festival was performed by diviners (urabe) from the Department of Divinities (Jingikan), who utilized ritual implements made of fur. This indicates a strong connection to yin-yang divination practices (onmyōdō). The use of these implements and the involvement of the Department of Divinities highlight the festival's spiritual and protective purposes.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Decline and Disappearance == The Festival of the Road Gods gradually disappeared during the mid-Heian period, influenced by the rise of the Festival of the Four Directions (Shikaku sai) and other similar observances. As these other festivals gained prominence, references to the Michiae no matsuri became scarce in historical records.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Interpretations and Records == The Ryō no gige (Explanations of the Prescriptions) notes that the Michiae no matsuri aimed to prevent calamity-causing demons from entering the capital by intercepting and entertaining them in the streets. Conversely, the norito prayer recorded in the Engi Shiki (Engi Regulations) for this festival indicates that it celebrated three deities: Yachimata-hiko and other road deities. This suggests a dual interpretation of the festival's purpose: both as a means of protection from malevolent spirits and as a celebration of specific deities associated with the roads.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Notes ==
=== Annotations === {{Reflist|group="注"}} <references group="lower-alpha"/>
=== References === {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * Nishimuta, Takaoki. *Heisei New Edition Norito Dictionary.* 2003, ISBN 4-900901-32-6. * Kōda, Toshio. *Annotated Annual Events.* 1976, ISBN 4-7971-0525-9. * Tsugita, Jun. *Revised Norito Interpretations.* 2008, ISBN 978-4-900901-85-8.

Yanagita, *Research on Place Names*, Kadokawa Bunko, 9th edition, 1974, p. 216.</ref> == Overview == Michae no Matsuri is a recurring festival prescribed under the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingiryō|qq=}}, one of the [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō]] system's codes.<ref name="da1"/> It is listed in the "June Festivals" section of the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]], compiled during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and categorized as a "minor ritual."<ref group="注">Rites conducted with one day of purification beforehand under the Ritsuryō system.</ref><ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/108492/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Minor Ritual] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> The ceremony was overseen by the {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōshiki|qq=}} (Capital Officials), with the {{ill|Urabe clan|ja|卜部氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urabe clan|qq=}} performing the purification.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 Britannica International Encyclopedia: Michae no Matsuri] Britannica Japanese Edition, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> It was often held in conjunction with the {{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fire-Pacification Festival|qq=}} in Kyoto.<ref name="n19">Nishimuta 2003, p. 119</ref>
While traditionally thought to occur after the [[:en:Ōharae-shiki|Ōharae]] (Great Purification) on the 30th day of the 6th and 12th lunar months, no specific festival date is recorded in the *Engishiki* or *Taika Reforms*, leading some to conclude that auspicious days were chosen instead, as suggested by the {{ill|Shūgaishō|ja|拾芥抄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shūgaishō|qq=}}.<ref name="tu1">Tsugita 2008, p. 378</ref>
Shinto traditions often observe half-yearly rituals based on [[:en:Religion and agriculture|seasonal agriculture]], the passage of {{ill|natural cycles|ja|自然観|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/natural cycles|qq=}}, and the transformations of plants and animals.<ref name="da1"/>
== Summary Table == {| class="wikitable" ! Item !! Description !! Remarks |- ! Deities | colspan="2"|Yachimata-hime (八衢比売神), Yachimata-hiko (八衢比古神), Kunado (久那斗神) |- ! Code | colspan="2"|Jingiryō, classified as a minor ritual |- | rowspan="3"|Legal References | * [[:en:Ōmi Code|Ōmi Code]] (668 CE) |- | * [[:en:Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka Kiyomihara Code]] (681 CE) |- | * {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|qq=}} (Chapter 6, Article 20, 718 CE) |- ! Dates | 6th and 12th lunar months || Specific dates vary |- ! Faith | rowspan="2"|Ancient Shinto || Includes natural worship |- | Rooted in ancestral worship |}
== Historical Interpretations == === *Kujigen* and *Nishinomiya-ki* === The {{ill|Kujigen|ja|公事根源|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujigen|qq=}} by [[:en:Ichijō Kaneyoshi|Ichijō Kanera]] misidentifies Michae no Matsuri as the "Festival of the Four Corners," combining it with the {{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Fire Calming Festival|ja|鎮火祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fire Calming Festival|qq=}}, which were separate.<ref name="ko1">Kōda 1976, p. 229</ref>
Meanwhile, the *Nishinomiya-ki* by {{ill|Minamoto no Takaaki|ja|源高明|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Takaaki|qq=}} clarifies the distinctions: the "Four Corners Festival" was conducted during epidemic outbreaks at Kyoto's periphery, and the "Four Borders Festival" targeted the nation's outermost provinces.<ref name="tu2">Tsugita 2008, p. 380</ref>
Despite these clear distinctions, misunderstandings led later generations to conflate the rites.<ref name="tu1"/> Epidemic outbreaks further blurred the line between Michae no Matsuri and rites honoring pestilence gods.<ref name="tu3">Tsugita 2008, p. 381</ref>
== Liturgical Text == A key portion of the Michae no Matsuri prayer: {{Quotation|'''Original Text:''' 高天之原尓事始氐、皇御孫命止称辞竟奉、...|source=Engishiki}}
== Significance == Key phrases in the prayer include: * '''Takamahara''' (高天原) signifies the celestial origin of the ritual. * '''Ōyamato''' (大八衢) denotes sacred crossroads that safeguard the imperial capital.
== Historical Context == The precise origins of the Festival of the Road Gods are unclear, but its formal characteristics suggest it is closely linked to the establishment of capital cities in Japan. It likely dates back to the founding of the Fujiwara Palace in Nara at the end of the seventh century. The festival's association with the introduction of capital cities suggests its purpose was to protect these new urban centers from malevolent supernatural influences.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Ritual Elements == The festival was performed by diviners (urabe) from the Department of Divinities (Jingikan), who utilized ritual implements made of fur. This indicates a strong connection to yin-yang divination practices (onmyōdō). The use of these implements and the involvement of the Department of Divinities highlight the festival's spiritual and protective purposes.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Decline and Disappearance == The Festival of the Road Gods gradually disappeared during the mid-Heian period, influenced by the rise of the Festival of the Four Directions (Shikaku sai) and other similar observances. As these other festivals gained prominence, references to the Michiae no matsuri became scarce in historical records.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Interpretations and Records == The Ryō no gige (Explanations of the Prescriptions) notes that the Michiae no matsuri aimed to prevent calamity-causing demons from entering the capital by intercepting and entertaining them in the streets. Conversely, the norito prayer recorded in the Engi Shiki (Engi Regulations) for this festival indicates that it celebrated three deities: Yachimata-hiko and other road deities. This suggests a dual interpretation of the festival's purpose: both as a means of protection from malevolent spirits and as a celebration of specific deities associated with the roads.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Notes ==

<!--Michiae no matsuri--><!--Michiae no matsuri--><!--Michiae no matsuri--> [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:Lunisolar December]] [[Category:Lunisolar June]]

{{User:Immanuelle/Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace updated}}
The Festival of the Road Gods (道祖神祭り, Michiae no matsuri) was an ancient ceremony prescribed by the Divinities Prescriptions (Jingiryō) intended to prevent evil spirits, such as demons and epidemic gods (ekishin), from entering the capital. This festival was conducted on the main roads (ōji) surrounding Kyoto in each of the four cardinal directions.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-09-29|title=Michiae no matsuri {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230929230332/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9286|access-date=2024-05-18|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
==Overview==
{{令制祭祀}} '''Michae no Matsuri''' (道饗祭, also read as ちあえのまつり) is one of the Shinto [[rites]]. It is held twice a year, in June and December, at the four corners of the capital's roads to enshrine the deities '''Yachimata-hiko''' (八衢比古神), '''Yachimata-hime''' (八衢比売神), and '''Kunado''' (久那斗神). The purpose of this ceremony is to prevent and protect the capital and the [[Imperial Palace]] from malevolent spirits such as [[demons]] and [[yōkai]], and to pray for divine protection.<ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/212207/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Michae no Matsuri] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> Although the ritual was centered in [[Kyoto]] under the [[Ritsuryō system]], it was also performed in rural areas during outbreaks of [[epidemics]].<ref name="da1">[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 World Encyclopedia, 2nd Edition: Michae no Matsuri] Heibonsha, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> In Musashi Province, the name "Ae-ba" (饗庭) appears frequently as a place name and surname, which folklorist [[Kunio Yanagita]] hypothesized to have originated from Michae no Matsuri.<ref name="da1"/> He also speculated that place names like "Aino-ta" and "Manota" in northern Musashi derived from "fields of Ae-ba."<ref>Kunio Yanagita, *Research on Place Names*, Kadokawa Bunko, 9th edition, 1974, p. 216.</ref> == Overview == Michae no Matsuri is a recurring festival prescribed under the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingiryō|qq=}}, one of the [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō]] system's codes.<ref name="da1"/> It is listed in the "June Festivals" section of the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]], compiled during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and categorized as a "minor ritual."<ref group="注">Rites conducted with one day of purification beforehand under the Ritsuryō system.</ref><ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/108492/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Minor Ritual] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> The ceremony was overseen by the {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōshiki|qq=}} (Capital Officials), with the {{ill|Urabe clan|ja|卜部氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urabe clan|qq=}} performing the purification.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 Britannica International Encyclopedia: Michae no Matsuri] Britannica Japanese Edition, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> It was often held in conjunction with the {{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fire-Pacification Festival|qq=}} in Kyoto.<ref name="n19">Nishimuta 2003, p. 119</ref>
While traditionally thought to occur after the [[:en:Ōharae-shiki|Ōharae]] (Great Purification) on the 30th day of the 6th and 12th lunar months, no specific festival date is recorded in the *Engishiki* or *Taika Reforms*, leading some to conclude that auspicious days were chosen instead, as suggested by the {{ill|Shūgaishō|ja|拾芥抄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shūgaishō|qq=}}.<ref name="tu1">Tsugita 2008, p. 378</ref>
Shinto traditions often observe half-yearly rituals based on [[:en:Religion and agriculture|seasonal agriculture]], the passage of {{ill|natural cycles|ja|自然観|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/natural cycles|qq=}}, and the transformations of plants and animals.<ref name="da1"/>

Dates | 6th and 12th lunar months || Specific dates vary |- ! Faith | rowspan="2"|Ancient Shinto || Includes natural worship |- | Rooted in ancestral worship |}

Meanwhile, the *Nishinomiya-ki* by {{ill|Minamoto no Takaaki|ja|源高明|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Takaaki|qq=}} clarifies the distinctions: the "Four Corners Festival" was conducted during epidemic outbreaks at Kyoto's periphery, and the "Four Borders Festival" targeted the nation's outermost provinces.<ref name="tu2">Tsugita 2008, p. 380</ref>
Despite these clear distinctions, misunderstandings led later generations to conflate the rites.<ref name="tu1"/> Epidemic outbreaks further blurred the line between Michae no Matsuri and rites honoring pestilence gods.<ref name="tu3">Tsugita 2008, p. 381</ref>

revision.text:
{{User:Immanuelle/Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace updated}}
The Festival of the Road Gods (道祖神祭り, Michiae no matsuri) was an ancient ceremony prescribed by the Divinities Prescriptions (Jingiryō) intended to prevent evil spirits, such as demons and epidemic gods (ekishin), from entering the capital. This festival was conducted on the main roads (ōji) surrounding Kyoto in each of the four cardinal directions.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-09-29|title=Michiae no matsuri {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230929230332/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9286|access-date=2024-05-18|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
==Overview==
{{令制祭祀}} '''Michae no Matsuri''' (道饗祭, also read as ちあえのまつり) is one of the Shinto [[rites]]. It is held twice a year, in June and December, at the four corners of the capital's roads to enshrine the deities '''Yachimata-hiko''' (八衢比古神), '''Yachimata-hime''' (八衢比売神), and '''Kunado''' (久那斗神). The purpose of this ceremony is to prevent and protect the capital and the [[Imperial Palace]] from malevolent spirits such as [[demons]] and [[yōkai]], and to pray for divine protection.<ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/212207/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Michae no Matsuri] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> Although the ritual was centered in [[Kyoto]] under the [[Ritsuryō system]], it was also performed in rural areas during outbreaks of [[epidemics]].<ref name="da1">[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 World Encyclopedia, 2nd Edition: Michae no Matsuri] Heibonsha, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> In Musashi Province, the name "Ae-ba" (饗庭) appears frequently as a place name and surname, which folklorist [[Kunio Yanagita]] hypothesized to have originated from Michae no Matsuri.<ref name="da1"/> He also speculated that place names like "Aino-ta" and "Manota" in northern Musashi derived from "fields of Ae-ba."<ref>Kunio Yanagita, *Research on Place Names*, Kadokawa Bunko, 9th edition, 1974, p. 216.</ref> == Overview == Michae no Matsuri is a recurring festival prescribed under the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingiryō|qq=}}, one of the [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō]] system's codes.<ref name="da1"/> It is listed in the "June Festivals" section of the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]], compiled during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and categorized as a "minor ritual."<ref group="注">Rites conducted with one day of purification beforehand under the Ritsuryō system.</ref><ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/108492/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Minor Ritual] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> The ceremony was overseen by the {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōshiki|qq=}} (Capital Officials), with the {{ill|Urabe clan|ja|卜部氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urabe clan|qq=}} performing the purification.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 Britannica International Encyclopedia: Michae no Matsuri] Britannica Japanese Edition, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> It was often held in conjunction with the {{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fire-Pacification Festival|qq=}} in Kyoto.<ref name="n19">Nishimuta 2003, p. 119</ref>
While traditionally thought to occur after the [[:en:Ōharae-shiki|Ōharae]] (Great Purification) on the 30th day of the 6th and 12th lunar months, no specific festival date is recorded in the *Engishiki* or *Taika Reforms*, leading some to conclude that auspicious days were chosen instead, as suggested by the {{ill|Shūgaishō|ja|拾芥抄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shūgaishō|qq=}}.<ref name="tu1">Tsugita 2008, p. 378</ref>
Shinto traditions often observe half-yearly rituals based on [[:en:Religion and agriculture|seasonal agriculture]], the passage of {{ill|natural cycles|ja|自然観|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/natural cycles|qq=}}, and the transformations of plants and animals.<ref name="da1"/>
== Summary Table == {| class="wikitable" ! Item !! Description !! Remarks |- ! Deities | colspan="2"|Yachimata-hime (八衢比売神), Yachimata-hiko (八衢比古神), Kunado (久那斗神) |- ! Code | colspan="2"|Jingiryō, classified as a minor ritual |- | rowspan="3"|Legal References | * [[:en:Ōmi Code|Ōmi Code]] (668 CE) |- | * [[:en:Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka Kiyomihara Code]] (681 CE) |- | * {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|qq=}} (Chapter 6, Article 20, 718 CE) |- ! Dates | 6th and 12th lunar months || Specific dates vary |- ! Faith | rowspan="2"|Ancient Shinto || Includes natural worship |- | Rooted in ancestral worship |}
== Historical Interpretations == === *Kujigen* and *Nishinomiya-ki* === The {{ill|Kujigen|ja|公事根源|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujigen|qq=}} by [[:en:Ichijō Kaneyoshi|Ichijō Kanera]] misidentifies Michae no Matsuri as the "Festival of the Four Corners," combining it with the {{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Fire Calming Festival|ja|鎮火祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fire Calming Festival|qq=}}, which were separate.<ref name="ko1">Kōda 1976, p. 229</ref>
Meanwhile, the *Nishinomiya-ki* by {{ill|Minamoto no Takaaki|ja|源高明|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Takaaki|qq=}} clarifies the distinctions: the "Four Corners Festival" was conducted during epidemic outbreaks at Kyoto's periphery, and the "Four Borders Festival" targeted the nation's outermost provinces.<ref name="tu2">Tsugita 2008, p. 380</ref>
Despite these clear distinctions, misunderstandings led later generations to conflate the rites.<ref name="tu1"/> Epidemic outbreaks further blurred the line between Michae no Matsuri and rites honoring pestilence gods.<ref name="tu3">Tsugita 2008, p. 381</ref>
== Liturgical Text == A key portion of the Michae no Matsuri prayer: {{Quotation|'''Original Text:''' 高天之原尓事始氐、皇御孫命止称辞竟奉、...|source=Engishiki}}
== Significance == Key phrases in the prayer include: * '''Takamahara''' (高天原) signifies the celestial origin of the ritual. * '''Ōyamato''' (大八衢) denotes sacred crossroads that safeguard the imperial capital.
== Historical Context == The precise origins of the Festival of the Road Gods are unclear, but its formal characteristics suggest it is closely linked to the establishment of capital cities in Japan. It likely dates back to the founding of the Fujiwara Palace in Nara at the end of the seventh century. The festival's association with the introduction of capital cities suggests its purpose was to protect these new urban centers from malevolent supernatural influences.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Ritual Elements == The festival was performed by diviners (urabe) from the Department of Divinities (Jingikan), who utilized ritual implements made of fur. This indicates a strong connection to yin-yang divination practices (onmyōdō). The use of these implements and the involvement of the Department of Divinities highlight the festival's spiritual and protective purposes.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Decline and Disappearance == The Festival of the Road Gods gradually disappeared during the mid-Heian period, influenced by the rise of the Festival of the Four Directions (Shikaku sai) and other similar observances. As these other festivals gained prominence, references to the Michiae no matsuri became scarce in historical records.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Interpretations and Records == The Ryō no gige (Explanations of the Prescriptions) notes that the Michiae no matsuri aimed to prevent calamity-causing demons from entering the capital by intercepting and entertaining them in the streets. Conversely, the norito prayer recorded in the Engi Shiki (Engi Regulations) for this festival indicates that it celebrated three deities: Yachimata-hiko and other road deities. This suggests a dual interpretation of the festival's purpose: both as a means of protection from malevolent spirits and as a celebration of specific deities associated with the roads.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Notes ==
=== Annotations === {{Reflist|group="注"}} <references group="lower-alpha"/>
=== References === {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * Nishimuta, Takaoki. *Heisei New Edition Norito Dictionary.* 2003, ISBN 4-900901-32-6. * Kōda, Toshio. *Annotated Annual Events.* 1976, ISBN 4-7971-0525-9. * Tsugita, Jun. *Revised Norito Interpretations.* 2008, ISBN 978-4-900901-85-8.
{{Shinto Sidebar}} {{Shinto-stub}}

Despite these clear distinctions, misunderstandings led later generations to conflate the rites.<ref name="tu1"/> Epidemic outbreaks further blurred the line between Michae no Matsuri and rites honoring pestilence gods.<ref name="tu3">Tsugita 2008, p. 381</ref>
== Liturgical Text == A key portion of the Michae no Matsuri prayer: {{Quotation|'''Original Text:''' 高天之原尓事始氐、皇御孫命止称辞竟奉、...|source=Engishiki}}
== Significance == Key phrases in the prayer include: * '''Takamahara''' (高天原) signifies the celestial origin of the ritual. * '''Ōyamato''' (大八衢) denotes sacred crossroads that safeguard the imperial capital.
== Historical Context == The precise origins of the Festival of the Road Gods are unclear, but its formal characteristics suggest it is closely linked to the establishment of capital cities in Japan. It likely dates back to the founding of the Fujiwara Palace in Nara at the end of the seventh century. The festival's association with the introduction of capital cities suggests its purpose was to protect these new urban centers from malevolent supernatural influences.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Ritual Elements == The festival was performed by diviners (urabe) from the Department of Divinities (Jingikan), who utilized ritual implements made of fur. This indicates a strong connection to yin-yang divination practices (onmyōdō). The use of these implements and the involvement of the Department of Divinities highlight the festival's spiritual and protective purposes.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Decline and Disappearance == The Festival of the Road Gods gradually disappeared during the mid-Heian period, influenced by the rise of the Festival of the Four Directions (Shikaku sai) and other similar observances. As these other festivals gained prominence, references to the Michiae no matsuri became scarce in historical records.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Interpretations and Records == The Ryō no gige (Explanations of the Prescriptions) notes that the Michiae no matsuri aimed to prevent calamity-causing demons from entering the capital by intercepting and entertaining them in the streets. Conversely, the norito prayer recorded in the Engi Shiki (Engi Regulations) for this festival indicates that it celebrated three deities: Yachimata-hiko and other road deities. This suggests a dual interpretation of the festival's purpose: both as a means of protection from malevolent spirits and as a celebration of specific deities associated with the roads.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Notes ==
=== Annotations === {{Reflist|group="注"}} <references group="lower-alpha"/>
=== References === {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * Nishimuta, Takaoki. *Heisei New Edition Norito Dictionary.* 2003, ISBN 4-900901-32-6. * Kōda, Toshio. *Annotated Annual Events.* 1976, ISBN 4-7971-0525-9. * Tsugita, Jun. *Revised Norito Interpretations.* 2008, ISBN 978-4-900901-85-8.
