revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q21654797]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{User:Immanuelle/Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace updated}}
The Festival of the Road Gods (道祖神祭り, Michiae no matsuri) was an ancient ceremony prescribed by the Divinities Prescriptions (Jingiryō) intended to prevent evil spirits, such as demons and epidemic gods (ekishin), from entering the capital. This festival was conducted on the main roads (ōji) surrounding Kyoto in each of the four cardinal directions.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-09-29|title=Michiae no matsuri {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230929230332/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9286|access-date=2024-05-18|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
==Overview==
{{令制祭祀}} '''Michae no Matsuri''' (道饗祭, also read as ちあえのまつり) is one of the Shinto [[:en:rites|rites]]. It is held twice a year, in June and December, at the four corners of the capital's roads to enshrine the deities '''Yachimata-hiko''' (八衢比古神), '''Yachimata-hime''' (八衢比売神), and '''Kunado''' (久那斗神). The purpose of this ceremony is to prevent and protect the capital and the [[:en:Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]] from malevolent spirits such as [[:en:Demons|Demons]] and [[:en:Yōkai|Yōkai]], and to pray for divine protection.<ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/212207/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Michae no Matsuri] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> Although the ritual was centered in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] under the [[Ritsuryō system]], it was also performed in rural areas during outbreaks of [[:en:Epidemics|Epidemics]].<ref name="da1">[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 World Encyclopedia, 2nd Edition: Michae no Matsuri] Heibonsha, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> In Musashi Province, the name "Ae-ba" (饗庭) appears frequently as a place name and surname, which folklorist [[Kunio Yanagita]] hypothesized to have originated from Michae no Matsuri.<ref name="da1"/> He also speculated that place names like "Aino-ta" and "Manota" in northern Musashi derived from "fields of Ae-ba."<ref>Kunio Yanagita, *Research on Place Names*, Kadokawa Bunko, 9th edition, 1974, p. 216.</ref> == Overview == Michae no Matsuri is a recurring festival prescribed under the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingiryō|qq=}}, one of the [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō]] system's codes.<ref name="da1"/> It is listed in the "June Festivals" section of the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]], compiled during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and categorized as a "minor ritual."<ref group="注">Rites conducted with one day of purification beforehand under the Ritsuryō system.</ref><ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/108492/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Minor Ritual] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> The ceremony was overseen by the {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōshiki|qq=|1=Right Capital Ward Officer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11374719}} (Capital Officials), with the {{ill|Urabe clan|ja|卜部氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urabe clan|qq=}} performing the purification.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 Britannica International Encyclopedia: Michae no Matsuri] Britannica Japanese Edition, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> It was often held in conjunction with the {{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fire-Pacification Festival|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 厳島神社}} in Kyoto.<ref name="n19">Nishimuta 2003, p. 119</ref>
While traditionally thought to occur after the [[:en:Ōharae-shiki|Ōharae]] (Great Purification) on the 30th day of the 6th and 12th lunar months, no specific festival date is recorded in the *Engishiki* or *Taika Reforms*, leading some to conclude that auspicious days were chosen instead, as suggested by the {{ill|Shūgaishō|ja|拾芥抄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shūgaishō|qq=|1=Shūgaishō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11497878}}.<ref name="tu1">Tsugita 2008, p. 378</ref>
Shinto traditions often observe half-yearly rituals based on [[:en:Religion and agriculture|seasonal agriculture]], the passage of {{ill|natural cycles|ja|自然観|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/natural cycles|qq=}}, and the transformations of plants and animals.<ref name="da1"/>
== Summary Table == {| class="wikitable" ! Item !! Description !! Remarks |- ! Deities | colspan="2"|Yachimata-hime (八衢比売神), Yachimata-hiko (八衢比古神), Kunado (久那斗神) |- ! Code | colspan="2"|Jingiryō, classified as a minor ritual |- | rowspan="3"|Legal References | * [[:en:Ōmi Code|Ōmi Code]] (668 CE) |- | * [[:en:Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka Kiyomihara Code]] (681 CE) |- | * {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2575743}} (Chapter 6, Article 20, 718 CE) |- ! Dates | 6th and 12th lunar months || Specific dates vary |- ! Faith | rowspan="2"|Ancient Shinto || Includes natural worship |- | Rooted in ancestral worship |}
== Historical Interpretations == === *Kujigen* and *Nishinomiya-ki* === The {{ill|Kujigen|ja|公事根源|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujigen|qq=|1=Kujigen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391892}} by [[:en:Ichijō Kaneyoshi|Ichijō Kanera]] misidentifies Michae no Matsuri as the "Festival of the Four Corners," combining it with the {{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Fire Calming Festival|ja|鎮火祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fire Calming Festival|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 厳島神社}}, which were separate.<ref name="ko1">Kōda 1976, p. 229</ref>
Meanwhile, the *Nishinomiya-ki* by {{ill|Minamoto no Takaaki|ja|源高明|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Takaaki|qq=|1=Minamoto no Takaakira|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564373}} clarifies the distinctions: the "Four Corners Festival" was conducted during epidemic outbreaks at Kyoto's periphery, and the "Four Borders Festival" targeted the nation's outermost provinces.<ref name="tu2">Tsugita 2008, p. 380</ref>
Despite these clear distinctions, misunderstandings led later generations to conflate the rites.<ref name="tu1"/> Epidemic outbreaks further blurred the line between Michae no Matsuri and rites honoring pestilence gods.<ref name="tu3">Tsugita 2008, p. 381</ref>
== Liturgical Text == A key portion of the Michae no Matsuri prayer: {{Quotation|'''Original Text:''' 高天之原尓事始氐、皇御孫命止称辞竟奉、...|source=Engishiki}}
== Significance == Key phrases in the prayer include: * '''Takamahara''' (高天原) signifies the celestial origin of the ritual. * '''Ōyamato''' (大八衢) denotes sacred crossroads that safeguard the imperial capital.
== Historical Context == The precise origins of the Festival of the Road Gods are unclear, but its formal characteristics suggest it is closely linked to the establishment of capital cities in Japan. It likely dates back to the founding of the Fujiwara Palace in Nara at the end of the seventh century. The festival's association with the introduction of capital cities suggests its purpose was to protect these new urban centers from malevolent supernatural influences.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Ritual Elements == The festival was performed by diviners (urabe) from the Department of Divinities (Jingikan), who utilized ritual implements made of fur. This indicates a strong connection to yin-yang divination practices (onmyōdō). The use of these implements and the involvement of the Department of Divinities highlight the festival's spiritual and protective purposes.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Decline and Disappearance == The Festival of the Road Gods gradually disappeared during the mid-Heian period, influenced by the rise of the Festival of the Four Directions (Shikaku sai) and other similar observances. As these other festivals gained prominence, references to the Michiae no matsuri became scarce in historical records.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Interpretations and Records == The Ryō no gige (Explanations of the Prescriptions) notes that the Michiae no matsuri aimed to prevent calamity-causing demons from entering the capital by intercepting and entertaining them in the streets. Conversely, the norito prayer recorded in the Engi Shiki (Engi Regulations) for this festival indicates that it celebrated three deities: Yachimata-hiko and other road deities. This suggests a dual interpretation of the festival's purpose: both as a means of protection from malevolent spirits and as a celebration of specific deities associated with the roads.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Notes ==
=== Annotations === {{Reflist|group="注"}} <references group="lower-alpha"/>
=== References === {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * Nishimuta, Takaoki. *Heisei New Edition Norito Dictionary.* 2003, ISBN 4-900901-32-6. * Kōda, Toshio. *Annotated Annual Events.* 1976, ISBN 4-7971-0525-9. * Tsugita, Jun. *Revised Norito Interpretations.* 2008, ISBN 978-4-900901-85-8.

It is held twice a year, in June and December, at the four corners of the capital's roads to enshrine the deities '''Yachimata-hiko''' (八衢比古神), '''Yachimata-hime''' (八衢比売神), and '''Kunado''' (久那斗神). The purpose of this ceremony is to prevent and protect the capital and the [[:en:Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]] from malevolent spirits such as [[:en:Demons|Demons]] and [[:en:Yōkai|Yōkai]], and to pray for divine protection.<ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/212207/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Michae no Matsuri] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> Although the ritual was centered in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] under the [[Ritsuryō system]], it was also performed in rural areas during outbreaks of [[:en:Epidemics|Epidemics]].<ref name="da1">[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 World Encyclopedia, 2nd Edition: Michae no Matsuri] Heibonsha, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> In Musashi Province, the name "Ae-ba" (饗庭) appears frequently as a place name and surname, which folklorist [[Kunio Yanagita]] hypothesized to have originated from Michae no Matsuri.<ref name="da1"/> He also speculated that place names like "Aino-ta" and "Manota" in northern Musashi derived from "fields of Ae-ba."<ref>Kunio Yanagita, *Research on Place Names*, Kadokawa Bunko, 9th edition, 1974, p. 216.</ref> == Overview == Michae no Matsuri is a recurring festival prescribed under the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingiryō|qq=}}, one of the [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō]] system's codes.<ref name="da1"/> It is listed in the "June Festivals" section of the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]], compiled during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and categorized as a "minor ritual."<ref group="注">Rites conducted with one day of purification beforehand under the Ritsuryō system.</ref><ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/108492/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Minor Ritual] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> The ceremony was overseen by the {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōshiki|qq=|1=Right Capital Ward Officer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11374719}} (Capital Officials), with the {{ill|Urabe clan|ja|卜部氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urabe clan|qq=}} performing the purification.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 Britannica International Encyclopedia: Michae no Matsuri] Britannica Japanese Edition, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> It was often held in conjunction with the {{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fire-Pacification Festival|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 厳島神社}} in Kyoto.<ref name="n19">Nishimuta 2003, p. 119</ref>
While traditionally thought to occur after the [[:en:Ōharae-shiki|Ōharae]] (Great Purification) on the 30th day of the 6th and 12th lunar months, no specific festival date is recorded in the *Engishiki* or *Taika Reforms*, leading some to conclude that auspicious days were chosen instead, as suggested by the {{ill|Shūgaishō|ja|拾芥抄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shūgaishō|qq=|1=Shūgaishō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11497878}}.<ref name="tu1">Tsugita 2008, p. 378</ref>
Shinto traditions often observe half-yearly rituals based on [[:en:Religion and agriculture|seasonal agriculture]], the passage of {{ill|natural cycles|ja|自然観|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/natural cycles|qq=}}, and the transformations of plants and animals.<ref name="da1"/>
== Summary Table == {| class="wikitable" ! Item !! Description !! Remarks |- ! Deities | colspan="2"|Yachimata-hime (八衢比売神), Yachimata-hiko (八衢比古神), Kunado (久那斗神) |- ! Code | colspan="2"|Jingiryō, classified as a minor ritual |- | rowspan="3"|Legal References | * [[:en:Ōmi Code|Ōmi Code]] (668 CE) |- | * [[:en:Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka Kiyomihara Code]] (681 CE) |- | * {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2575743}} (Chapter 6, Article 20, 718 CE) |- ! Dates | 6th and 12th lunar months || Specific dates vary |- ! Faith | rowspan="2"|Ancient Shinto || Includes natural worship |- | Rooted in ancestral worship |}
== Historical Interpretations == === *Kujigen* and *Nishinomiya-ki* === The {{ill|Kujigen|ja|公事根源|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujigen|qq=|1=Kujigen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391892}} by [[:en:Ichijō Kaneyoshi|Ichijō Kanera]] misidentifies Michae no Matsuri as the "Festival of the Four Corners," combining it with the {{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Fire Calming Festival|ja|鎮火祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fire Calming Festival|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 厳島神社}}, which were separate.<ref name="ko1">Kōda 1976, p. 229</ref>
Meanwhile, the *Nishinomiya-ki* by {{ill|Minamoto no Takaaki|ja|源高明|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Takaaki|qq=|1=Minamoto no Takaakira|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564373}} clarifies the distinctions: the "Four Corners Festival" was conducted during epidemic outbreaks at Kyoto's periphery, and the "Four Borders Festival" targeted the nation's outermost provinces.<ref name="tu2">Tsugita 2008, p. 380</ref>
Despite these clear distinctions, misunderstandings led later generations to conflate the rites.<ref name="tu1"/> Epidemic outbreaks further blurred the line between Michae no Matsuri and rites honoring pestilence gods.<ref name="tu3">Tsugita 2008, p. 381</ref>
== Liturgical Text == A key portion of the Michae no Matsuri prayer: {{Quotation|'''Original Text:''' 高天之原尓事始氐、皇御孫命止称辞竟奉、...|source=Engishiki}}
== Significance == Key phrases in the prayer include: * '''Takamahara''' (高天原) signifies the celestial origin of the ritual. * '''Ōyamato''' (大八衢) denotes sacred crossroads that safeguard the imperial capital.
== Historical Context == The precise origins of the Festival of the Road Gods are unclear, but its formal characteristics suggest it is closely linked to the establishment of capital cities in Japan. It likely dates back to the founding of the Fujiwara Palace in Nara at the end of the seventh century. The festival's association with the introduction of capital cities suggests its purpose was to protect these new urban centers from malevolent supernatural influences.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Ritual Elements == The festival was performed by diviners (urabe) from the Department of Divinities (Jingikan), who utilized ritual implements made of fur. This indicates a strong connection to yin-yang divination practices (onmyōdō). The use of these implements and the involvement of the Department of Divinities highlight the festival's spiritual and protective purposes.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Decline and Disappearance == The Festival of the Road Gods gradually disappeared during the mid-Heian period, influenced by the rise of the Festival of the Four Directions (Shikaku sai) and other similar observances. As these other festivals gained prominence, references to the Michiae no matsuri became scarce in historical records.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Interpretations and Records == The Ryō no gige (Explanations of the Prescriptions) notes that the Michiae no matsuri aimed to prevent calamity-causing demons from entering the capital by intercepting and entertaining them in the streets. Conversely, the norito prayer recorded in the Engi Shiki (Engi Regulations) for this festival indicates that it celebrated three deities: Yachimata-hiko and other road deities. This suggests a dual interpretation of the festival's purpose: both as a means of protection from malevolent spirits and as a celebration of specific deities associated with the roads.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Notes ==

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:道饗祭]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q21654797]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{User:Immanuelle/Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace updated}}
The Festival of the Road Gods (道祖神祭り, Michiae no matsuri) was an ancient ceremony prescribed by the Divinities Prescriptions (Jingiryō) intended to prevent evil spirits, such as demons and epidemic gods (ekishin), from entering the capital. This festival was conducted on the main roads (ōji) surrounding Kyoto in each of the four cardinal directions.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-09-29|title=Michiae no matsuri {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230929230332/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9286|access-date=2024-05-18|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>

== Historical Interpretations == === *Kujigen* and *Nishinomiya-ki* === The {{ill|Kujigen|ja|公事根源|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujigen|1=Kujigen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391892}} by [[:en:Ichijō Kaneyoshi|Ichijō Kanera]] misidentifies Michae no Matsuri as the "Festival of the Four Corners," combining it with the {{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Fire Calming Festival|ja|鎮火祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fire Calming Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 厳島神社}}, which were separate.<ref name="ko1">Kōda 1976, p. 229</ref>
Meanwhile, the *Nishinomiya-ki* by {{ill|Minamoto no Takaaki|ja|源高明|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Takaaki|1=Minamoto no Takaakira|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564373}} clarifies the distinctions: the "Four Corners Festival" was conducted during epidemic outbreaks at Kyoto's periphery, and the "Four Borders Festival" targeted the nation's outermost provinces.<ref name="tu2">Tsugita 2008, p. 380</ref>
Despite these clear distinctions, misunderstandings led later generations to conflate the rites.<ref name="tu1"/> Epidemic outbreaks further blurred the line between Michae no Matsuri and rites honoring pestilence gods.<ref name="tu3">Tsugita 2008, p. 381</ref>
== Liturgical Text == A key portion of the Michae no Matsuri prayer: {{Quotation|'''Original Text:''' 高天之原尓事始氐、皇御孫命止称辞竟奉、...|source=Engishiki}}
== Significance == Key phrases in the prayer include: * '''Takamahara''' (高天原) signifies the celestial origin of the ritual. * '''Ōyamato''' (大八衢) denotes sacred crossroads that safeguard the imperial capital.
== Historical Context == The precise origins of the Festival of the Road Gods are unclear, but its formal characteristics suggest it is closely linked to the establishment of capital cities in Japan. It likely dates back to the founding of the Fujiwara Palace in Nara at the end of the seventh century. The festival's association with the introduction of capital cities suggests its purpose was to protect these new urban centers from malevolent supernatural influences.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Ritual Elements == The festival was performed by diviners (urabe) from the Department of Divinities (Jingikan), who utilized ritual implements made of fur. This indicates a strong connection to yin-yang divination practices (onmyōdō). The use of these implements and the involvement of the Department of Divinities highlight the festival's spiritual and protective purposes.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Decline and Disappearance == The Festival of the Road Gods gradually disappeared during the mid-Heian period, influenced by the rise of the Festival of the Four Directions (Shikaku sai) and other similar observances. As these other festivals gained prominence, references to the Michiae no matsuri became scarce in historical records.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Interpretations and Records == The Ryō no gige (Explanations of the Prescriptions) notes that the Michiae no matsuri aimed to prevent calamity-causing demons from entering the capital by intercepting and entertaining them in the streets. Conversely, the norito prayer recorded in the Engi Shiki (Engi Regulations) for this festival indicates that it celebrated three deities: Yachimata-hiko and other road deities. This suggests a dual interpretation of the festival's purpose: both as a means of protection from malevolent spirits and as a celebration of specific deities associated with the roads.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Notes ==
=== Annotations === {{Reflist|group="注"}} <references group="lower-alpha"/>
=== References === {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * Nishimuta, Takaoki. *Heisei New Edition Norito Dictionary.* 2003, ISBN 4-900901-32-6. * Kōda, Toshio. *Annotated Annual Events.* 1976, ISBN 4-7971-0525-9. * Tsugita, Jun. *Revised Norito Interpretations.* 2008, ISBN 978-4-900901-85-8.

[[ja:道饗祭]]
<!--Michiae no matsuri--><!--Michiae no matsuri--><!--Michiae no matsuri--> [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:Lunisolar December]] [[Category:Lunisolar June]] [[Category:Shinto festivals]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q21654797}}

revision.comment: Bot: Add Wikidata no short description - based on Wikidata English short description
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:道饗祭]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q21654797]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{User:Immanuelle/Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace updated}}
The Festival of the Road Gods (道祖神祭り, Michiae no matsuri) was an ancient ceremony prescribed by the Divinities Prescriptions (Jingiryō) intended to prevent evil spirits, such as demons and epidemic gods (ekishin), from entering the capital. This festival was conducted on the main roads (ōji) surrounding Kyoto in each of the four cardinal directions.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-09-29|title=Michiae no matsuri {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230929230332/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9286|access-date=2024-05-18|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
==Overview==
{{令制祭祀}} '''Michae no Matsuri''' (道饗祭, also read as ちあえのまつり) is one of the Shinto [[:en:rites|rites]]. It is held twice a year, in June and December, at the four corners of the capital's roads to enshrine the deities '''Yachimata-hiko''' (八衢比古神), '''Yachimata-hime''' (八衢比売神), and '''Kunado''' (久那斗神). The purpose of this ceremony is to prevent and protect the capital and the [[:en:Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]] from malevolent spirits such as [[:en:Demons|Demons]] and [[:en:Yōkai|Yōkai]], and to pray for divine protection.<ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/212207/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Michae no Matsuri] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> Although the ritual was centered in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] under the [[Ritsuryō system]], it was also performed in rural areas during outbreaks of [[:en:Epidemics|Epidemics]].<ref name="da1">[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 World Encyclopedia, 2nd Edition: Michae no Matsuri] Heibonsha, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> In Musashi Province, the name "Ae-ba" (饗庭) appears frequently as a place name and surname, which folklorist [[Kunio Yanagita]] hypothesized to have originated from Michae no Matsuri.<ref name="da1"/> He also speculated that place names like "Aino-ta" and "Manota" in northern Musashi derived from "fields of Ae-ba."<ref>Kunio Yanagita, *Research on Place Names*, Kadokawa Bunko, 9th edition, 1974, p. 216.</ref> == Overview == Michae no Matsuri is a recurring festival prescribed under the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingiryō|qq=}}, one of the [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō]] system's codes.<ref name="da1"/> It is listed in the "June Festivals" section of the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]], compiled during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and categorized as a "minor ritual."<ref group="注">Rites conducted with one day of purification beforehand under the Ritsuryō system.</ref><ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/108492/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Minor Ritual] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> The ceremony was overseen by the {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōshiki|1=Right Capital Ward Officer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11374719}} (Capital Officials), with the {{ill|Urabe clan|ja|卜部氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urabe clan|qq=}} performing the purification.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 Britannica International Encyclopedia: Michae no Matsuri] Britannica Japanese Edition, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> It was often held in conjunction with the {{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fire-Pacification Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 厳島神社}} in Kyoto.<ref name="n19">Nishimuta 2003, p. 119</ref>
While traditionally thought to occur after the [[:en:Ōharae-shiki|Ōharae]] (Great Purification) on the 30th day of the 6th and 12th lunar months, no specific festival date is recorded in the *Engishiki* or *Taika Reforms*, leading some to conclude that auspicious days were chosen instead, as suggested by the {{ill|Shūgaishō|ja|拾芥抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shūgaishō|1=Shūgaishō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11497878}}.<ref name="tu1">Tsugita 2008, p. 378</ref>
Shinto traditions often observe half-yearly rituals based on [[:en:Religion and agriculture|seasonal agriculture]], the passage of {{ill|natural cycles|ja|自然観|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/natural cycles|qq=}}, and the transformations of plants and animals.<ref name="da1"/>
== Summary Table == {| class="wikitable" ! Item !! Description !! Remarks |- ! Deities | colspan="2"|Yachimata-hime (八衢比売神), Yachimata-hiko (八衢比古神), Kunado (久那斗神) |- ! Code | colspan="2"|Jingiryō, classified as a minor ritual |- | rowspan="3"|Legal References | * [[:en:Ōmi Code|Ōmi Code]] (668 CE) |- | * [[:en:Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka Kiyomihara Code]] (681 CE) |- | * {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2575743}} (Chapter 6, Article 20, 718 CE) |- ! Dates | 6th and 12th lunar months || Specific dates vary |- ! Faith | rowspan="2"|Ancient Shinto || Includes natural worship |- | Rooted in ancestral worship |}
== Historical Interpretations == === *Kujigen* and *Nishinomiya-ki* === The {{ill|Kujigen|ja|公事根源|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujigen|1=Kujigen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391892}} by [[:en:Ichijō Kaneyoshi|Ichijō Kanera]] misidentifies Michae no Matsuri as the "Festival of the Four Corners," combining it with the {{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Fire Calming Festival|ja|鎮火祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fire Calming Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 厳島神社}}, which were separate.<ref name="ko1">Kōda 1976, p. 229</ref>
Meanwhile, the *Nishinomiya-ki* by {{ill|Minamoto no Takaaki|ja|源高明|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Takaaki|1=Minamoto no Takaakira|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564373}} clarifies the distinctions: the "Four Corners Festival" was conducted during epidemic outbreaks at Kyoto's periphery, and the "Four Borders Festival" targeted the nation's outermost provinces.<ref name="tu2">Tsugita 2008, p. 380</ref>
Despite these clear distinctions, misunderstandings led later generations to conflate the rites.<ref name="tu1"/> Epidemic outbreaks further blurred the line between Michae no Matsuri and rites honoring pestilence gods.<ref name="tu3">Tsugita 2008, p. 381</ref>
== Liturgical Text == A key portion of the Michae no Matsuri prayer: {{Quotation|'''Original Text:''' 高天之原尓事始氐、皇御孫命止称辞竟奉、...|source=Engishiki}}
== Significance == Key phrases in the prayer include: * '''Takamahara''' (高天原) signifies the celestial origin of the ritual. * '''Ōyamato''' (大八衢) denotes sacred crossroads that safeguard the imperial capital.
== Historical Context == The precise origins of the Festival of the Road Gods are unclear, but its formal characteristics suggest it is closely linked to the establishment of capital cities in Japan. It likely dates back to the founding of the Fujiwara Palace in Nara at the end of the seventh century. The festival's association with the introduction of capital cities suggests its purpose was to protect these new urban centers from malevolent supernatural influences.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Ritual Elements == The festival was performed by diviners (urabe) from the Department of Divinities (Jingikan), who utilized ritual implements made of fur. This indicates a strong connection to yin-yang divination practices (onmyōdō). The use of these implements and the involvement of the Department of Divinities highlight the festival's spiritual and protective purposes.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Decline and Disappearance == The Festival of the Road Gods gradually disappeared during the mid-Heian period, influenced by the rise of the Festival of the Four Directions (Shikaku sai) and other similar observances. As these other festivals gained prominence, references to the Michiae no matsuri became scarce in historical records.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Interpretations and Records == The Ryō no gige (Explanations of the Prescriptions) notes that the Michiae no matsuri aimed to prevent calamity-causing demons from entering the capital by intercepting and entertaining them in the streets. Conversely, the norito prayer recorded in the Engi Shiki (Engi Regulations) for this festival indicates that it celebrated three deities: Yachimata-hiko and other road deities. This suggests a dual interpretation of the festival's purpose: both as a means of protection from malevolent spirits and as a celebration of specific deities associated with the roads.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Notes ==
=== Annotations === {{Reflist|group="注"}} <references group="lower-alpha"/>

== Further Reading == * Nishimuta, Takaoki. *Heisei New Edition Norito Dictionary.* 2003, ISBN 4-900901-32-6. * Kōda, Toshio. *Annotated Annual Events.* 1976, ISBN 4-7971-0525-9. * Tsugita, Jun. *Revised Norito Interpretations.* 2008, ISBN 978-4-900901-85-8.

It is held twice a year, in June and December, at the four corners of the capital's roads to enshrine the deities '''Yachimata-hiko''' (八衢比古神), '''Yachimata-hime''' (八衢比売神), and '''Kunado''' (久那斗神). The purpose of this ceremony is to prevent and protect the capital and the [[:en:Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]] from malevolent spirits such as [[:en:Demons|Demons]] and [[:en:Yōkai|Yōkai]], and to pray for divine protection.<ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/212207/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Michae no Matsuri] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> Although the ritual was centered in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] under the [[Ritsuryō system]], it was also performed in rural areas during outbreaks of [[:en:Epidemics|Epidemics]].<ref name="da1">[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 World Encyclopedia, 2nd Edition: Michae no Matsuri] Heibonsha, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> In Musashi Province, the name "Ae-ba" (饗庭) appears frequently as a place name and surname, which folklorist [[Kunio Yanagita]] hypothesized to have originated from Michae no Matsuri.<ref name="da1"/> He also speculated that place names like "Aino-ta" and "Manota" in northern Musashi derived from "fields of Ae-ba."<ref>Kunio Yanagita, *Research on Place Names*, Kadokawa Bunko, 9th edition, 1974, p. 216.</ref> == Overview == Michae no Matsuri is a recurring festival prescribed under the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingiryō|qq=}}, one of the [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō]] system's codes.<ref name="da1"/> It is listed in the "June Festivals" section of the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]], compiled during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and categorized as a "minor ritual."<ref group="注">Rites conducted with one day of purification beforehand under the Ritsuryō system.</ref><ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/108492/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Minor Ritual] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> The ceremony was overseen by the {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōshiki|1=Right Capital Ward Officer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11374719}} (Capital Officials), with the {{ill|Urabe clan|ja|卜部氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urabe clan|qq=}} performing the purification.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 Britannica International Encyclopedia: Michae no Matsuri] Britannica Japanese Edition, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> It was often held in conjunction with the {{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fire-Pacification Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 厳島神社}} in Kyoto.<ref name="n19">Nishimuta 2003, p. 119</ref>
While traditionally thought to occur after the [[:en:Ōharae-shiki|Ōharae]] (Great Purification) on the 30th day of the 6th and 12th lunar months, no specific festival date is recorded in the *Engishiki* or *Taika Reforms*, leading some to conclude that auspicious days were chosen instead, as suggested by the {{ill|Shūgaishō|ja|拾芥抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shūgaishō|1=Shūgaishō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11497878}}.<ref name="tu1">Tsugita 2008, p. 378</ref>
Shinto traditions often observe half-yearly rituals based on [[:en:Religion and agriculture|seasonal agriculture]], the passage of {{ill|natural cycles|ja|自然観|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/natural cycles|qq=}}, and the transformations of plants and animals.<ref name="da1"/>
== Summary Table == {| class="wikitable" ! Item !! Description !! Remarks |- ! Deities | colspan="2"|Yachimata-hime (八衢比売神), Yachimata-hiko (八衢比古神), Kunado (久那斗神) |- ! Code | colspan="2"|Jingiryō, classified as a minor ritual |- | rowspan="3"|Legal References | * [[:en:Ōmi Code|Ōmi Code]] (668 CE) |- | * [[:en:Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka Kiyomihara Code]] (681 CE) |- | * {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2575743}} (Chapter 6, Article 20, 718 CE) |- ! Dates | 6th and 12th lunar months || Specific dates vary |- ! Faith | rowspan="2"|Ancient Shinto || Includes natural worship |- | Rooted in ancestral worship |}
== Historical Interpretations == === *Kujigen* and *Nishinomiya-ki* === The {{ill|Kujigen|ja|公事根源|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujigen|1=Kujigen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391892}} by [[:en:Ichijō Kaneyoshi|Ichijō Kanera]] misidentifies Michae no Matsuri as the "Festival of the Four Corners," combining it with the {{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Fire Calming Festival|ja|鎮火祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fire Calming Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 厳島神社}}, which were separate.<ref name="ko1">Kōda 1976, p. 229</ref>
Meanwhile, the *Nishinomiya-ki* by {{ill|Minamoto no Takaaki|ja|源高明|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Takaaki|1=Minamoto no Takaakira|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564373}} clarifies the distinctions: the "Four Corners Festival" was conducted during epidemic outbreaks at Kyoto's periphery, and the "Four Borders Festival" targeted the nation's outermost provinces.<ref name="tu2">Tsugita 2008, p. 380</ref>
Despite these clear distinctions, misunderstandings led later generations to conflate the rites.<ref name="tu1"/> Epidemic outbreaks further blurred the line between Michae no Matsuri and rites honoring pestilence gods.<ref name="tu3">Tsugita 2008, p. 381</ref>
== Liturgical Text == A key portion of the Michae no Matsuri prayer: {{Quotation|'''Original Text:''' 高天之原尓事始氐、皇御孫命止称辞竟奉、...|source=Engishiki}}
== Significance == Key phrases in the prayer include: * '''Takamahara''' (高天原) signifies the celestial origin of the ritual. * '''Ōyamato''' (大八衢) denotes sacred crossroads that safeguard the imperial capital.
== Historical Context == The precise origins of the Festival of the Road Gods are unclear, but its formal characteristics suggest it is closely linked to the establishment of capital cities in Japan. It likely dates back to the founding of the Fujiwara Palace in Nara at the end of the seventh century. The festival's association with the introduction of capital cities suggests its purpose was to protect these new urban centers from malevolent supernatural influences.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Ritual Elements == The festival was performed by diviners (urabe) from the Department of Divinities (Jingikan), who utilized ritual implements made of fur. This indicates a strong connection to yin-yang divination practices (onmyōdō). The use of these implements and the involvement of the Department of Divinities highlight the festival's spiritual and protective purposes.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Decline and Disappearance == The Festival of the Road Gods gradually disappeared during the mid-Heian period, influenced by the rise of the Festival of the Four Directions (Shikaku sai) and other similar observances. As these other festivals gained prominence, references to the Michiae no matsuri became scarce in historical records.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Interpretations and Records == The Ryō no gige (Explanations of the Prescriptions) notes that the Michiae no matsuri aimed to prevent calamity-causing demons from entering the capital by intercepting and entertaining them in the streets. Conversely, the norito prayer recorded in the Engi Shiki (Engi Regulations) for this festival indicates that it celebrated three deities: Yachimata-hiko and other road deities. This suggests a dual interpretation of the festival's purpose: both as a means of protection from malevolent spirits and as a celebration of specific deities associated with the roads.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Notes ==

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:道饗祭]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q21654797]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{User:Immanuelle/Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace updated}}
The Festival of the Road Gods (道祖神祭り, Michiae no matsuri) was an ancient ceremony prescribed by the Divinities Prescriptions (Jingiryō) intended to prevent evil spirits, such as demons and epidemic gods (ekishin), from entering the capital. This festival was conducted on the main roads (ōji) surrounding Kyoto in each of the four cardinal directions.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-09-29|title=Michiae no matsuri {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230929230332/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9286|access-date=2024-05-18|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>

== Historical Interpretations == === *Kujigen* and *Nishinomiya-ki* === The {{ill|Kujigen|ja|公事根源|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujigen|1=Kujigen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391892}} by [[:en:Ichijō Kaneyoshi|Ichijō Kanera]] misidentifies Michae no Matsuri as the "Festival of the Four Corners," combining it with the {{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Fire Calming Festival|ja|鎮火祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fire Calming Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 厳島神社}}, which were separate.<ref name="ko1">Kōda 1976, p. 229</ref>
Meanwhile, the *Nishinomiya-ki* by {{ill|Minamoto no Takaaki|ja|源高明|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Takaaki|1=Minamoto no Takaakira|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564373}} clarifies the distinctions: the "Four Corners Festival" was conducted during epidemic outbreaks at Kyoto's periphery, and the "Four Borders Festival" targeted the nation's outermost provinces.<ref name="tu2">Tsugita 2008, p. 380</ref>
Despite these clear distinctions, misunderstandings led later generations to conflate the rites.<ref name="tu1"/> Epidemic outbreaks further blurred the line between Michae no Matsuri and rites honoring pestilence gods.<ref name="tu3">Tsugita 2008, p. 381</ref>
== Liturgical Text == A key portion of the Michae no Matsuri prayer: {{Quotation|'''Original Text:''' 高天之原尓事始氐、皇御孫命止称辞竟奉、...|source=Engishiki}}
== Significance == Key phrases in the prayer include: * '''Takamahara''' (高天原) signifies the celestial origin of the ritual. * '''Ōyamato''' (大八衢) denotes sacred crossroads that safeguard the imperial capital.
== Historical Context == The precise origins of the Festival of the Road Gods are unclear, but its formal characteristics suggest it is closely linked to the establishment of capital cities in Japan. It likely dates back to the founding of the Fujiwara Palace in Nara at the end of the seventh century. The festival's association with the introduction of capital cities suggests its purpose was to protect these new urban centers from malevolent supernatural influences.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Ritual Elements == The festival was performed by diviners (urabe) from the Department of Divinities (Jingikan), who utilized ritual implements made of fur. This indicates a strong connection to yin-yang divination practices (onmyōdō). The use of these implements and the involvement of the Department of Divinities highlight the festival's spiritual and protective purposes.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Decline and Disappearance == The Festival of the Road Gods gradually disappeared during the mid-Heian period, influenced by the rise of the Festival of the Four Directions (Shikaku sai) and other similar observances. As these other festivals gained prominence, references to the Michiae no matsuri became scarce in historical records.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Interpretations and Records == The Ryō no gige (Explanations of the Prescriptions) notes that the Michiae no matsuri aimed to prevent calamity-causing demons from entering the capital by intercepting and entertaining them in the streets. Conversely, the norito prayer recorded in the Engi Shiki (Engi Regulations) for this festival indicates that it celebrated three deities: Yachimata-hiko and other road deities. This suggests a dual interpretation of the festival's purpose: both as a means of protection from malevolent spirits and as a celebration of specific deities associated with the roads.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Notes ==

[[ja:道饗祭]]
<!--Michiae no matsuri--><!--Michiae no matsuri--><!--Michiae no matsuri--> [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:Lunisolar December]] [[Category:Lunisolar June]] [[Category:Shinto festivals]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q21654797}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]
{{wikidata link|Q21654797}} [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:道饗祭]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q21654797]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{User:Immanuelle/Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace updated}}
The Festival of the Road Gods (道祖神祭り, Michiae no matsuri) was an ancient ceremony prescribed by the Divinities Prescriptions (Jingiryō) intended to prevent evil spirits, such as demons and epidemic gods (ekishin), from entering the capital. This festival was conducted on the main roads (ōji) surrounding Kyoto in each of the four cardinal directions.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-09-29|title=Michiae no matsuri {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230929230332/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9286|access-date=2024-05-18|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
==Overview==
{{令制祭祀}} '''Michae no Matsuri''' (道饗祭, also read as ちあえのまつり) is one of the Shinto [[:en:rites|rites]]. It is held twice a year, in June and December, at the four corners of the capital's roads to enshrine the deities '''Yachimata-hiko''' (八衢比古神), '''Yachimata-hime''' (八衢比売神), and '''Kunado''' (久那斗神). The purpose of this ceremony is to prevent and protect the capital and the [[:en:Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]] from malevolent spirits such as [[:en:Demons|Demons]] and [[:en:Yōkai|Yōkai]], and to pray for divine protection.<ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/212207/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Michae no Matsuri] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> Although the ritual was centered in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] under the [[Ritsuryō system]], it was also performed in rural areas during outbreaks of [[:en:Epidemics|Epidemics]].<ref name="da1">[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 World Encyclopedia, 2nd Edition: Michae no Matsuri] Heibonsha, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> In Musashi Province, the name "Ae-ba" (饗庭) appears frequently as a place name and surname, which folklorist [[Kunio Yanagita]] hypothesized to have originated from Michae no Matsuri.<ref name="da1"/> He also speculated that place names like "Aino-ta" and "Manota" in northern Musashi derived from "fields of Ae-ba."<ref>Kunio Yanagita, *Research on Place Names*, Kadokawa Bunko, 9th edition, 1974, p. 216.</ref> == Overview == Michae no Matsuri is a recurring festival prescribed under the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingiryō|qq=}}, one of the [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō]] system's codes.<ref name="da1"/> It is listed in the "June Festivals" section of the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]], compiled during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and categorized as a "minor ritual."<ref group="注">Rites conducted with one day of purification beforehand under the Ritsuryō system.</ref><ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/108492/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Minor Ritual] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> The ceremony was overseen by the {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōshiki|1=Right Capital Ward Officer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11374719}} (Capital Officials), with the {{ill|Urabe clan|ja|卜部氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urabe clan|qq=}} performing the purification.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 Britannica International Encyclopedia: Michae no Matsuri] Britannica Japanese Edition, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> It was often held in conjunction with the {{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fire-Pacification Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 厳島神社}} in Kyoto.<ref name="n19">Nishimuta 2003, p. 119</ref>
While traditionally thought to occur after the [[:en:Ōharae-shiki|Ōharae]] (Great Purification) on the 30th day of the 6th and 12th lunar months, no specific festival date is recorded in the *Engishiki* or *Taika Reforms*, leading some to conclude that auspicious days were chosen instead, as suggested by the {{ill|Shūgaishō|ja|拾芥抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shūgaishō|1=Shūgaishō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11497878}}.<ref name="tu1">Tsugita 2008, p. 378</ref>
Shinto traditions often observe half-yearly rituals based on [[:en:Religion and agriculture|seasonal agriculture]], the passage of {{ill|natural cycles|ja|自然観|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/natural cycles|qq=}}, and the transformations of plants and animals.<ref name="da1"/>
== Summary Table == {| class="wikitable" ! Item !! Description !! Remarks |- ! Deities | colspan="2"|Yachimata-hime (八衢比売神), Yachimata-hiko (八衢比古神), Kunado (久那斗神) |- ! Code | colspan="2"|Jingiryō, classified as a minor ritual |- | rowspan="3"|Legal References | * [[:en:Ōmi Code|Ōmi Code]] (668 CE) |- | * [[:en:Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka Kiyomihara Code]] (681 CE) |- | * {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2575743}} (Chapter 6, Article 20, 718 CE) |- ! Dates | 6th and 12th lunar months || Specific dates vary |- ! Faith | rowspan="2"|Ancient Shinto || Includes natural worship |- | Rooted in ancestral worship |}
== Historical Interpretations == === *Kujigen* and *Nishinomiya-ki* === The {{ill|Kujigen|ja|公事根源|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujigen|1=Kujigen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391892}} by [[:en:Ichijō Kaneyoshi|Ichijō Kanera]] misidentifies Michae no Matsuri as the "Festival of the Four Corners," combining it with the {{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Fire Calming Festival|ja|鎮火祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fire Calming Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 厳島神社}}, which were separate.<ref name="ko1">Kōda 1976, p. 229</ref>
Meanwhile, the *Nishinomiya-ki* by {{ill|Minamoto no Takaaki|ja|源高明|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Takaaki|1=Minamoto no Takaakira|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564373}} clarifies the distinctions: the "Four Corners Festival" was conducted during epidemic outbreaks at Kyoto's periphery, and the "Four Borders Festival" targeted the nation's outermost provinces.<ref name="tu2">Tsugita 2008, p. 380</ref>
Despite these clear distinctions, misunderstandings led later generations to conflate the rites.<ref name="tu1"/> Epidemic outbreaks further blurred the line between Michae no Matsuri and rites honoring pestilence gods.<ref name="tu3">Tsugita 2008, p. 381</ref>
== Liturgical Text == A key portion of the Michae no Matsuri prayer: {{Quotation|'''Original Text:''' 高天之原尓事始氐、皇御孫命止称辞竟奉、...|source=Engishiki}}
== Significance == Key phrases in the prayer include: * '''Takamahara''' (高天原) signifies the celestial origin of the ritual. * '''Ōyamato''' (大八衢) denotes sacred crossroads that safeguard the imperial capital.
== Historical Context == The precise origins of the Festival of the Road Gods are unclear, but its formal characteristics suggest it is closely linked to the establishment of capital cities in Japan. It likely dates back to the founding of the Fujiwara Palace in Nara at the end of the seventh century. The festival's association with the introduction of capital cities suggests its purpose was to protect these new urban centers from malevolent supernatural influences.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Ritual Elements == The festival was performed by diviners (urabe) from the Department of Divinities (Jingikan), who utilized ritual implements made of fur. This indicates a strong connection to yin-yang divination practices (onmyōdō). The use of these implements and the involvement of the Department of Divinities highlight the festival's spiritual and protective purposes.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Decline and Disappearance == The Festival of the Road Gods gradually disappeared during the mid-Heian period, influenced by the rise of the Festival of the Four Directions (Shikaku sai) and other similar observances. As these other festivals gained prominence, references to the Michiae no matsuri became scarce in historical records.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Interpretations and Records == The Ryō no gige (Explanations of the Prescriptions) notes that the Michiae no matsuri aimed to prevent calamity-causing demons from entering the capital by intercepting and entertaining them in the streets. Conversely, the norito prayer recorded in the Engi Shiki (Engi Regulations) for this festival indicates that it celebrated three deities: Yachimata-hiko and other road deities. This suggests a dual interpretation of the festival's purpose: both as a means of protection from malevolent spirits and as a celebration of specific deities associated with the roads.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Notes ==

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:道饗祭]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q21654797]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{User:Immanuelle/Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace updated}}
The Festival of the Road Gods (道祖神祭り, Michiae no matsuri) was an ancient ceremony prescribed by the Divinities Prescriptions (Jingiryō) intended to prevent evil spirits, such as demons and epidemic gods (ekishin), from entering the capital. This festival was conducted on the main roads (ōji) surrounding Kyoto in each of the four cardinal directions.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-09-29|title=Michiae no matsuri {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230929230332/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9286|access-date=2024-05-18|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
==Overview==
{{令制祭祀}} '''Michae no Matsuri''' (道饗祭, also read as ちあえのまつり) is one of the Shinto [[:en:rites|rites]]. It is held twice a year, in June and December, at the four corners of the capital's roads to enshrine the deities '''Yachimata-hiko''' (八衢比古神), '''Yachimata-hime''' (八衢比売神), and '''Kunado''' (久那斗神). The purpose of this ceremony is to prevent and protect the capital and the [[:en:Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]] from malevolent spirits such as [[:en:Demons|Demons]] and [[:en:Yōkai|Yōkai]], and to pray for divine protection.<ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/212207/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Michae no Matsuri] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> Although the ritual was centered in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] under the [[Ritsuryō system]], it was also performed in rural areas during outbreaks of [[:en:Epidemics|Epidemics]].<ref name="da1">[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 World Encyclopedia, 2nd Edition: Michae no Matsuri] Heibonsha, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> In Musashi Province, the name "Ae-ba" (饗庭) appears frequently as a place name and surname, which folklorist [[Kunio Yanagita]] hypothesized to have originated from Michae no Matsuri.<ref name="da1"/> He also speculated that place names like "Aino-ta" and "Manota" in northern Musashi derived from "fields of Ae-ba."<ref>Kunio Yanagita, *Research on Place Names*, Kadokawa Bunko, 9th edition, 1974, p. 216.</ref> == Overview == Michae no Matsuri is a recurring festival prescribed under the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingiryō|qq=}}, one of the [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō]] system's codes.<ref name="da1"/> It is listed in the "June Festivals" section of the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]], compiled during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and categorized as a "minor ritual."<ref group="注">Rites conducted with one day of purification beforehand under the Ritsuryō system.</ref><ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/108492/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Minor Ritual] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> The ceremony was overseen by the {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōshiki|1=Right Capital Ward Officer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11374719}} (Capital Officials), with the {{ill|Urabe clan|ja|卜部氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urabe clan|qq=}} performing the purification.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 Britannica International Encyclopedia: Michae no Matsuri] Britannica Japanese Edition, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> It was often held in conjunction with the {{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fire-Pacification Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 厳島神社}} in Kyoto.<ref name="n19">Nishimuta 2003, p. 119</ref>
While traditionally thought to occur after the [[:en:Ōharae-shiki|Ōharae]] (Great Purification) on the 30th day of the 6th and 12th lunar months, no specific festival date is recorded in the *Engishiki* or *Taika Reforms*, leading some to conclude that auspicious days were chosen instead, as suggested by the {{ill|Shūgaishō|ja|拾芥抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shūgaishō|1=Shūgaishō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11497878}}.<ref name="tu1">Tsugita 2008, p. 378</ref>
Shinto traditions often observe half-yearly rituals based on [[:en:Religion and agriculture|seasonal agriculture]], the passage of {{ill|natural cycles|ja|自然観|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/natural cycles|qq=}}, and the transformations of plants and animals.<ref name="da1"/>
== Summary Table == {| class="wikitable" ! Item !! Description !! Remarks |- ! Deities | colspan="2"|Yachimata-hime (八衢比売神), Yachimata-hiko (八衢比古神), Kunado (久那斗神) |- ! Code | colspan="2"|Jingiryō, classified as a minor ritual |- | rowspan="3"|Legal References | * [[:en:Ōmi Code|Ōmi Code]] (668 CE) |- | * [[:en:Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka Kiyomihara Code]] (681 CE) |- | * {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2575743}} (Chapter 6, Article 20, 718 CE) |- ! Dates | 6th and 12th lunar months || Specific dates vary |- ! Faith | rowspan="2"|Ancient Shinto || Includes natural worship |- | Rooted in ancestral worship |}
== Historical Interpretations == === *Kujigen* and *Nishinomiya-ki* === The {{ill|Kujigen|ja|公事根源|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujigen|1=Kujigen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391892}} by [[:en:Ichijō Kaneyoshi|Ichijō Kanera]] misidentifies Michae no Matsuri as the "Festival of the Four Corners," combining it with the {{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Fire Calming Festival|ja|鎮火祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fire Calming Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 厳島神社}}, which were separate.<ref name="ko1">Kōda 1976, p. 229</ref>
Meanwhile, the *Nishinomiya-ki* by {{ill|Minamoto no Takaaki|ja|源高明|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Takaaki|1=Minamoto no Takaakira|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564373}} clarifies the distinctions: the "Four Corners Festival" was conducted during epidemic outbreaks at Kyoto's periphery, and the "Four Borders Festival" targeted the nation's outermost provinces.<ref name="tu2">Tsugita 2008, p. 380</ref>
Despite these clear distinctions, misunderstandings led later generations to conflate the rites.<ref name="tu1"/> Epidemic outbreaks further blurred the line between Michae no Matsuri and rites honoring pestilence gods.<ref name="tu3">Tsugita 2008, p. 381</ref>
== Liturgical Text == A key portion of the Michae no Matsuri prayer: {{Quotation|'''Original Text:''' 高天之原尓事始氐、皇御孫命止称辞竟奉、...|source=Engishiki}}
== Significance == Key phrases in the prayer include: * '''Takamahara''' (高天原) signifies the celestial origin of the ritual. * '''Ōyamato''' (大八衢) denotes sacred crossroads that safeguard the imperial capital.
== Historical Context == The precise origins of the Festival of the Road Gods are unclear, but its formal characteristics suggest it is closely linked to the establishment of capital cities in Japan. It likely dates back to the founding of the Fujiwara Palace in Nara at the end of the seventh century. The festival's association with the introduction of capital cities suggests its purpose was to protect these new urban centers from malevolent supernatural influences.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Ritual Elements == The festival was performed by diviners (urabe) from the Department of Divinities (Jingikan), who utilized ritual implements made of fur. This indicates a strong connection to yin-yang divination practices (onmyōdō). The use of these implements and the involvement of the Department of Divinities highlight the festival's spiritual and protective purposes.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Decline and Disappearance == The Festival of the Road Gods gradually disappeared during the mid-Heian period, influenced by the rise of the Festival of the Four Directions (Shikaku sai) and other similar observances. As these other festivals gained prominence, references to the Michiae no matsuri became scarce in historical records.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Interpretations and Records == The Ryō no gige (Explanations of the Prescriptions) notes that the Michiae no matsuri aimed to prevent calamity-causing demons from entering the capital by intercepting and entertaining them in the streets. Conversely, the norito prayer recorded in the Engi Shiki (Engi Regulations) for this festival indicates that it celebrated three deities: Yachimata-hiko and other road deities. This suggests a dual interpretation of the festival's purpose: both as a means of protection from malevolent spirits and as a celebration of specific deities associated with the roads.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Notes ==
=== Annotations === {{Reflist|group="注"}} <references group="lower-alpha"/>
=== References === {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * Nishimuta, Takaoki. *Heisei New Edition Norito Dictionary.* 2003, ISBN 4-900901-32-6. * Kōda, Toshio. *Annotated Annual Events.* 1976, ISBN 4-7971-0525-9. * Tsugita, Jun. *Revised Norito Interpretations.* 2008, ISBN 978-4-900901-85-8.

name="n19">Nishimuta 2003, p. 119</ref>

Shinto traditions often observe half-yearly rituals based on [[:en:Religion and agriculture|seasonal agriculture]], the passage of {{ill|natural cycles|ja|自然観|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/natural cycles|qq=}}, and the transformations of plants and animals.<ref name="da1"/>
== Summary Table == {| class="wikitable" ! Item !! Description !! Remarks |- ! Deities | colspan="2"|Yachimata-hime (八衢比売神), Yachimata-hiko (八衢比古神), Kunado (久那斗神) |- ! Code | colspan="2"|Jingiryō, classified as a minor ritual |- | rowspan="3"|Legal References | * [[:en:Ōmi Code|Ōmi Code]] (668 CE) |- | * [[:en:Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka Kiyomihara Code]] (681 CE) |- | * {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2575743}} (Chapter 6, Article 20, 718 CE) |- ! Dates | 6th and 12th lunar months || Specific dates vary |- ! Faith | rowspan="2"|Ancient Shinto || Includes natural worship |- | Rooted in ancestral worship |}
== Historical Interpretations == === *Kujigen* and *Nishinomiya-ki* === The {{ill|Kujigen|ja|公事根源|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujigen|1=Kujigen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391892}} by [[:en:Ichijō Kaneyoshi|Ichijō Kanera]] misidentifies Michae no Matsuri as the "Festival of the Four Corners," combining it with the {{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Fire Calming Festival|ja|鎮火祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fire Calming Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 厳島神社}}, which were separate.<ref name="ko1">Kōda 1976, p. 229</ref>
Meanwhile, the *Nishinomiya-ki* by {{ill|Minamoto no Takaaki|ja|源高明|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Takaaki|1=Minamoto no Takaakira|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564373}} clarifies the distinctions: the "Four Corners Festival" was conducted during epidemic outbreaks at Kyoto's periphery, and the "Four Borders Festival" targeted the nation's outermost provinces.<ref name="tu2">Tsugita 2008, p. 380</ref>
Despite these clear distinctions, misunderstandings led later generations to conflate the rites.<ref name="tu1"/> Epidemic outbreaks further blurred the line between Michae no Matsuri and rites honoring pestilence gods.<ref name="tu3">Tsugita 2008, p. 381</ref>
== Liturgical Text == A key portion of the Michae no Matsuri prayer: {{Quotation|'''Original Text:''' 高天之原尓事始氐、皇御孫命止称辞竟奉、...|source=Engishiki}}
== Significance == Key phrases in the prayer include: * '''Takamahara''' (高天原) signifies the celestial origin of the ritual. * '''Ōyamato''' (大八衢) denotes sacred crossroads that safeguard the imperial capital.
== Historical Context == The precise origins of the Festival of the Road Gods are unclear, but its formal characteristics suggest it is closely linked to the establishment of capital cities in Japan. It likely dates back to the founding of the Fujiwara Palace in Nara at the end of the seventh century. The festival's association with the introduction of capital cities suggests its purpose was to protect these new urban centers from malevolent supernatural influences.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Ritual Elements == The festival was performed by diviners (urabe) from the Department of Divinities (Jingikan), who utilized ritual implements made of fur. This indicates a strong connection to yin-yang divination practices (onmyōdō). The use of these implements and the involvement of the Department of Divinities highlight the festival's spiritual and protective purposes.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Decline and Disappearance == The Festival of the Road Gods gradually disappeared during the mid-Heian period, influenced by the rise of the Festival of the Four Directions (Shikaku sai) and other similar observances. As these other festivals gained prominence, references to the Michiae no matsuri became scarce in historical records.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Interpretations and Records == The Ryō no gige (Explanations of the Prescriptions) notes that the Michiae no matsuri aimed to prevent calamity-causing demons from entering the capital by intercepting and entertaining them in the streets. Conversely, the norito prayer recorded in the Engi Shiki (Engi Regulations) for this festival indicates that it celebrated three deities: Yachimata-hiko and other road deities. This suggests a dual interpretation of the festival's purpose: both as a means of protection from malevolent spirits and as a celebration of specific deities associated with the roads.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Notes ==
=== Annotations === {{Reflist|group="注"}} <references group="lower-alpha"/>
=== References === {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * Nishimuta, Takaoki. *Heisei New Edition Norito Dictionary.* 2003, ISBN 4-900901-32-6. * Kōda, Toshio. *Annotated Annual Events.* 1976, ISBN 4-7971-0525-9. * Tsugita, Jun. *Revised Norito Interpretations.* 2008, ISBN 978-4-900901-85-8.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:道饗祭]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q21654797]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{User:Immanuelle/Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace updated}}
The Festival of the Road Gods (道祖神祭り, Michiae no matsuri) was an ancient ceremony prescribed by the Divinities Prescriptions (Jingiryō) intended to prevent evil spirits, such as demons and epidemic gods (ekishin), from entering the capital. This festival was conducted on the main roads (ōji) surrounding Kyoto in each of the four cardinal directions.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-09-29|title=Michiae no matsuri {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230929230332/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9286|access-date=2024-05-18|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
==Overview==
{{令制祭祀}} '''Michae no Matsuri''' (道饗祭, also read as ちあえのまつり) is one of the Shinto [[:en:rites|rites]]. It is held twice a year, in June and December, at the four corners of the capital's roads to enshrine the deities '''Yachimata-hiko''' (八衢比古神), '''Yachimata-hime''' (八衢比売神), and '''Kunado''' (久那斗神). The purpose of this ceremony is to prevent and protect the capital and the [[:en:Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]] from malevolent spirits such as [[:en:Demons|Demons]] and [[:en:Yōkai|Yōkai]], and to pray for divine protection.<ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/212207/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Michae no Matsuri] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> Although the ritual was centered in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] under the [[Ritsuryō system]], it was also performed in rural areas during outbreaks of [[:en:Epidemics|Epidemics]].<ref name="da1">[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 World Encyclopedia, 2nd Edition: Michae no Matsuri] Heibonsha, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> In Musashi Province, the name "Ae-ba" (饗庭) appears frequently as a place name and surname, which folklorist [[Kunio Yanagita]] hypothesized to have originated from Michae no Matsuri.<ref name="da1"/> He also speculated that place names like "Aino-ta" and "Manota" in northern Musashi derived from "fields of Ae-ba."<ref>Kunio Yanagita, *Research on Place Names*, Kadokawa Bunko, 9th edition, 1974, p. 216.</ref> == Overview == Michae no Matsuri is a recurring festival prescribed under the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingiryō|qq=}}, one of the [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō]] system's codes.<ref name="da1"/> It is listed in the "June Festivals" section of the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]], compiled during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and categorized as a "minor ritual."<ref group="注">Rites conducted with one day of purification beforehand under the Ritsuryō system.</ref><ref>[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/108492/m0u/ Digital Daijisen: Minor Ritual] Shogakukan, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> The ceremony was overseen by the {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōshiki|1=Right Capital Ward Officer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11374719}} (Capital Officials), with the {{ill|Urabe clan|ja|卜部氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urabe clan|qq=}} performing the purification.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%81%93%E9%A5%97%E7%A5%AD-138785 Britannica International Encyclopedia: Michae no Matsuri] Britannica Japanese Edition, accessed July 12, 2015</ref> It was often held in conjunction with the {{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fire-Pacification Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 厳島神社}} in Kyoto.<ref name="n19">Nishimuta 2003, p. 119</ref>
While traditionally thought to occur after the [[:en:Ōharae-shiki|Ōharae]] (Great Purification) on the 30th day of the 6th and 12th lunar months, no specific festival date is recorded in the *Engishiki* or *Taika Reforms*, leading some to conclude that auspicious days were chosen instead, as suggested by the {{ill|Shūgaishō|ja|拾芥抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shūgaishō|1=Shūgaishō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11497878}}.<ref name="tu1">Tsugita 2008, p. 378</ref>
Shinto traditions often observe half-yearly rituals based on [[:en:Religion and agriculture|seasonal agriculture]], the passage of {{ill|natural cycles|ja|自然観|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/natural cycles|qq=}}, and the transformations of plants and animals.<ref name="da1"/>
== Summary Table == {| class="wikitable" ! Item !! Description !! Remarks |- ! Deities | colspan="2"|Yachimata-hime (八衢比売神), Yachimata-hiko (八衢比古神), Kunado (久那斗神) |- ! Code | colspan="2"|Jingiryō, classified as a minor ritual |- | rowspan="3"|Legal References | * [[:en:Ōmi Code|Ōmi Code]] (668 CE) |- | * [[:en:Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka Kiyomihara Code]] (681 CE) |- | * {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2575743}} (Chapter 6, Article 20, 718 CE) |- ! Dates | 6th and 12th lunar months || Specific dates vary |- ! Faith | rowspan="2"|Ancient Shinto || Includes natural worship |- | Rooted in ancestral worship |}
== Historical Interpretations == === *Kujigen* and *Nishinomiya-ki* === The {{ill|Kujigen|ja|公事根源|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujigen|1=Kujigen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391892}} by [[:en:Ichijō Kaneyoshi|Ichijō Kanera]] misidentifies Michae no Matsuri as the "Festival of the Four Corners," combining it with the {{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Fire Calming Festival|ja|鎮火祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fire Calming Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 厳島神社}}, which were separate.<ref name="ko1">Kōda 1976, p. 229</ref>
Meanwhile, the *Nishinomiya-ki* by {{ill|Minamoto no Takaaki|ja|源高明|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Takaaki|1=Minamoto no Takaakira|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564373}} clarifies the distinctions: the "Four Corners Festival" was conducted during epidemic outbreaks at Kyoto's periphery, and the "Four Borders Festival" targeted the nation's outermost provinces.<ref name="tu2">Tsugita 2008, p. 380</ref>
Despite these clear distinctions, misunderstandings led later generations to conflate the rites.<ref name="tu1"/> Epidemic outbreaks further blurred the line between Michae no Matsuri and rites honoring pestilence gods.<ref name="tu3">Tsugita 2008, p. 381</ref>
== Liturgical Text == A key portion of the Michae no Matsuri prayer: {{Quotation|'''Original Text:''' 高天之原尓事始氐、皇御孫命止称辞竟奉、...|source=Engishiki}}
== Significance == Key phrases in the prayer include: * '''Takamahara''' (高天原) signifies the celestial origin of the ritual. * '''Ōyamato''' (大八衢) denotes sacred crossroads that safeguard the imperial capital.
== Historical Context == The precise origins of the Festival of the Road Gods are unclear, but its formal characteristics suggest it is closely linked to the establishment of capital cities in Japan. It likely dates back to the founding of the Fujiwara Palace in Nara at the end of the seventh century. The festival's association with the introduction of capital cities suggests its purpose was to protect these new urban centers from malevolent supernatural influences.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Ritual Elements == The festival was performed by diviners (urabe) from the Department of Divinities (Jingikan), who utilized ritual implements made of fur. This indicates a strong connection to yin-yang divination practices (onmyōdō). The use of these implements and the involvement of the Department of Divinities highlight the festival's spiritual and protective purposes.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Decline and Disappearance == The Festival of the Road Gods gradually disappeared during the mid-Heian period, influenced by the rise of the Festival of the Four Directions (Shikaku sai) and other similar observances. As these other festivals gained prominence, references to the Michiae no matsuri became scarce in historical records.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Interpretations and Records == The Ryō no gige (Explanations of the Prescriptions) notes that the Michiae no matsuri aimed to prevent calamity-causing demons from entering the capital by intercepting and entertaining them in the streets. Conversely, the norito prayer recorded in the Engi Shiki (Engi Regulations) for this festival indicates that it celebrated three deities: Yachimata-hiko and other road deities. This suggests a dual interpretation of the festival's purpose: both as a means of protection from malevolent spirits and as a celebration of specific deities associated with the roads.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Notes ==
